item.title: [Flirting with Models] Ben Wellington – Complex Feature Engineering at Two Sigma (S7E32)
item.dc:creator: Flirting with Models
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Two Sigma的"反自动化"悖论

大多数人听到AI+量化，第一反应是"更快、更多、更自动"。但Ben在整段对话中埋藏了一个尖锐的矛盾——他领导的团队正在极力对抗自动化的均质化倾向。他用"造纸箱"和"造Alpha"的对比一针见血：纸箱要求每次输出一模一样，而Alpha要求每次输出不一样。

这里藏着一个几乎所有AI布道者都忽略的深层结构：当工具的边际成本趋近于零，工具本身产生的"熵"也会趋近于零。也就是说，人人都能一键生成特征时，特征的信息含量反而在坍塌。Two Sigma的策略不是"用AI做得更多"，而是"用AI放大每个研究员的怪癖"。这是一种反熵减的AI使用哲学——与硅谷主流叙事完全相反。

二、一个跨领域连接：Ben其实在做"生态学"，不是"金融学"

留意Ben反复使用的词汇：orthogonality（正交）、diversity（多样性）、correlation death（相关性即死亡）、ecosystem of models（模型生态）。这套语言体系其实来自种群生物学与生态系统理论，不是传统金融理论。

有意思的跨领域映射： - 特征库 ≈ 基因池 - 每一个feature ≈ 一段DNA - Alpha衰减 ≈ 生态位被占据后的适应性下降 - LLM生成新特征 ≈ 突变率上升 - 全局共享平台 vs. Pod-shop ≈ 有性生殖 vs. 无性克隆

Pod-shop模式像克隆——每个pod独立进化，禁止基因交流；Two Sigma像开放交配的种群——高频基因重组产生更强的"混合优势"（heterosis）。这也解释了为什么Ben如此警惕"人人用同一个AI工具点同一个按钮"——那等价于生态系统的单一栽培崩溃（monoculture collapse），就像爱尔兰马铃薯饥荒。金融市场的"马铃薯疫病"，就是相关性突然飙升的那个瞬间。

三、被低估的洞见：Ben说的"墙"其实是研究的真正资产

他提到一个非常反常识的观点——主动去撞技术还没突破的墙，比等技术成熟再进场更有价值。这句话乍听像是"勤奋"的鸡汤，但仔细拆解，你会发现这是一种期权定价思维：

提前撞墙 = 买入一份"技术突破期权"的实物期权（real option）

当LLM能力跃升那一刻发生时，那些已经知道"墙在哪、形状如何、需要什么工具来拆"的团队，转化时间是几周；而临时入场的团队，需要几个月先去摸墙。18个月的先发优势，在Alpha生命周期里就是永恒。

四、一个真正让人后背发凉的细节：LLM里的"时间穿越污染"

Ben顺口提到的一句几乎被忽略的话——"LLM在回测时会把Enron和'坏'这个词永久绑定"——是整场对话最危险也最少被讨论的风险。

这已经不是传统的look-ahead bias，而是一种语义层的时间穿越污染（semantic temporal contamination）。当你用一个训练截止到2024年的LLM去分析2001年的Enron新闻，它对"Enron"这个词的情感嵌入本身已经被未来污染。这个问题在数值特征里完全不存在，是LLM时代独有的、结构性的、几乎无法完全清除的新型污染。

跨领域联想：这有点像考古学的碳-14污染——现代碳原子渗入古样本，让年代测定失效。金融量化正在进入一个"每一个词都带着未来记忆"的新世界，而大多数团队还在用20世纪的时间序列思维处理它。

五、彩蛋洞察：Ben的"公共数据"爱好，其实和他的工作是同一件事

他在结尾说自己"沉迷于公共数据"——停车罚单、超速拦截、餐厅卫生检查。表面上这是个人爱好，但仔细听：他兴奋的点是"AI让不需要技术的普通人也能问出问题"。

这和他工作里"AI放大人类原创性"的哲学完全同构。他真正着迷的其实是同一个元问题：当访问知识的技术门槛坍塌时，人类独特的提问能力会浮现出多少价值？ 他的职业和爱好不是两件事，是同一个问题的两个投影面。

这也许是这次对话最隐蔽的自我暴露——Ben这个人本身，就是一个关于"提问是最后的护城河"的活体研究项目。

📝 转录文本

开场

All right, Ben, are you ready to go? — I'm ready. — All right, three, two, one, let's jam.

Hello and welcome everyone. I'm Corey Hoffstein and this is Flirting with Models, the podcast that pulls back the curtain to discover the human factor behind the quantitative strategy.

[Disclaimer] Cory Hoffstein is the co-founder and chief investment officer of Newfound Research. Due to industry regulations, he will not discuss any of Newfound Research's funds on this podcast. All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinion and do not reflect the opinion of Newfound Research. This podcast is for informational purposes only and should not be relied upon as a basis for investment decisions. Clients of Newfound Research may maintain positions and securities discussed in this podcast. For more information, visit thinknewfound.com.

本期嘉宾介绍

My guest this episode is Ben Wellington, head of complex feature engines at Two Sigma.

In a modern quant process, one might argue that edge can live in three places: the data you can get your hands on, what you do with that data, and how you forecast from it. Ben lives squarely in the middle layer — feature generation — which also happens to be the place AI is reshaping the world fastest. So that's where we spend our time.

We start with what a feature even is and why. As raw data gets commoditized, the edge increasingly comes from what you build out of it. Then we follow the thread running through the whole conversation: large language models. Ben has a line I keep coming back to — that anything can be language now — and we trace what that unlocks and whether making feature creation this cheap just democratizes the edge away. We close on what a quant starting out today should be building toward, and which skills compound most in a career where AI is the dominant tool.

Please enjoy my conversation with Ben Wellington.

Ben的背景与Two Sigma之路

Corey: Ben, welcome to the show. This is going to be an exciting one for me to dive into. When I found out I was going to get the opportunity to chat with you, it was great, because I've had a lot of people come on to talk about equities and equity factors, but I think some of the work you're doing is really novel. Where to dive in here really gets to the cutting edge of what it means to be a systematic quantitative equity investor today. So, I really appreciate you joining me.

Ben: Thank you very much. I'm happy to be here. Thanks for having me.

Corey: So before we dive in, maybe we can give the listeners a little bit of lay of the land and your background — who you are, where you went to school, how you ended up at Two Sigma, what the beginning of your career looked like, and the quick run through to where you are today in the complex feature engines team.

Ben: Yeah, I did my PhD in natural language processing at New York University, with a focus in machine translation. You have to kind of go back in time a little bit — it was still quite odd to be speaking to a computer or using translation, before Siri, before Alexa, all this stuff. It just sounded like a cool field. So when I was looking to go to grad school, cuz I didn't know what to do with my life, I read some research areas and it just seemed fun. So I dove into what I guess fast forward to today turned out to be a pretty central part of the growing economy, but that was mostly luck, right?

I did my PhD and my first real job after was a place in Soho, New York called Two Sigma, with about 125 or so people in sort of a loft building. When I met people on the interview, I was like, these are interesting, cool, fun people. And so I jumped right in. Actually my first role there was on the data engineering team — responsible for starting to collect and ingest and clean up the data we were collecting at the time. I joined in 2007. I had my background in natural language processing but I was more just really being an engineer more broadly at the time.

As I did it, I started to look around and I noticed all these different data sets at Two Sigma that were really, really interesting. At one point, I came across some data that had been collected and I did this analysis — a political analysis of different news places and whether they were leaning one way or the other. I posted this in an internal message board and I got this call from someone at the company who said, "Hey, I like what you did. Maybe not the best to send everyone everything you like this — but what else can you do?" It turned out that was somebody over in modeling at Two Sigma.

From there I kind of shifted my work towards what began in the news space — news data — and eventually the textual space more broadly, and spent a good 10-plus years just studying how text predicts markets in various ways. So that's kind of how I went from grad school to an engineering role and then eventually to a more modeling-focused role, where our goal is to predict, using as much data as we can, how the things that we create are going to move — are they going to go up or down in value over different time horizons.

什么是"Feature"？

Corey: Your title today is head of complex feature engines, and right in there is that word feature. That word gets thrown around a lot in the world of quantitative investing, but at least anecdotally, when I talk to many people, it means different things to different people. I'd love to just start with you — what does the phrase feature mean? How does Two Sigma really use that word? Maybe if you can give us some examples that span the spectrum from the very familiar perhaps to the very alternative.

Ben: We've had many debates over the definition of the word feature. It's central to what we do. I think we've landed in different areas, but let me start with the examples — it's easier to give an example than a definition.

An example is something like the change in stock price over the last week of a company. It's a fact about something we trade that's economically meaningful. At least at Two Sigma, the economically part is important, but you could have futures in other industries where that's not part of the definition. So really, we look at it as saying: what is a fact about the world that is worth itself noting?

The fact that a stock went up 5% — the stock's price is sort of a raw piece of data. It goes up and down, but you can pull different features. What did it do since last year? How much did it move? What was the volatility? Those are more technical common features that you might hear about.

But then features can get more interesting: did an analyst recently upgrade the stock? It's kind of interesting — it's a feature about a stock or a feature about an analyst depending how you look at it. And then it can get progressively more specific. From my background in textual data: how much news coverage is there about a particular company compared to the history? That's a feature about that company — we're understanding that it's growing in its news coverage or reducing. Or when did they release this filing, what day of the week did the press release come out? Did it come out on a Friday after hours or did it come on an earlier time?

So, it's really a way to encapsulate these ideas that are usually driven by some hypothesis — like, hey, the fact that it might come out on a Friday afternoon is itself interesting enough that I'm going to make a feature out of it. The nice thing about making a feature is that you can then study that feature, instead of just saying, "I'm going to study all press releases in a giant pile and throw it at a computer and see what comes out."

By layering human intelligence and intuition over it, I can start to pick apart what's meaningful in there. I'm kind of helping a computer out by saying, "No, no, no — Friday afternoons are really interesting, as is the length, maybe as is…" I can use my knowledge of how companies operate. So I'm not asking the computer to check everything ever about a company at all times, which is a very large ask. By filtering down using human layers of knowledge, we can make that job tractable. In the end, each of those things is a feature.

We believe that instead of just — I mean, you can't just throw a bunch of raw data into some machine learning algorithm and tell it to predict the future. It's definitely a way to approach things, but we really believe there's value in this feature layer of converting data into features and then using those features as our predicting starting point to understand what's going to happen next.

Corey: In a year or two, we're going to have to revisit that feature example about Friday afternoons and see if it survives a 24/7 market environment.

Ben: That's true. [laughter] As things start to change, that's a good point.

平台模式 vs. Pod-Shop 模式

Corey: So in the world of quant equity, one of the big evolutions and big adoptions has been the pod shop model, and Two Sigma in contrast operates on — I guess we would call — a shared platform model. It's one core portfolio rather than a series of competing pods. How does that structural choice shape what you and your team focus on and optimize for day-to-day? And how do you think that differs from what a researcher at a pod shop would be focused on?

Ben: I'll first say that it's not that we have literally a single portfolio. We have many products and portfolios. But as modelers at Two Sigma who are trying to find alpha, we are not looking at it through the lens of any portfolio specifically. Our job is to make these predictions, and we don't control ultimately how these portfolios trade.

So we have teams out there trying to say, "Okay, here's what's going to happen to the IBM price over the next week. Here's what's going to happen to Nvidia" — each coming from a different angle — and they're not competing against each other because at the end of the day, all of these predictions are being centralized on some of the firm's larger portfolios and being aggregated. Decisions are going to be made based on the combination of all these individuals.

That creates a lot of interesting opportunity. In a world where you have pods competing, there is reason why you might say have a technology that you don't want to share with another group, or invest truly locally because you feel like you're trying to beat out another team. At Two Sigma, we're thinking: how can we globally optimize instead of locally optimize? We want to elevate some of the best stuff we do up to a shared platform so others can use it. That might be a set of features that are so interesting I'm going to put it out there for other teams to study, or it might be a really clever machine learning algorithm that somebody discovered and they're going to build a tool around it and share it.

This really allows our platform to scale in ways that is really remarkable because there's so many people contributing globally to make the overall platform better — as opposed to just a platform that exists that others kind of attach to and pull from. In a normal pod shop, there's a centralized platform that's mostly pulled from — it's the team utilizing the platform. Here it's cyclical: the platform is an iterative process, and our teams are constantly pushing up into the platform for others to share because we all are aligned in making Two Sigma make the best predictions possible.

Alpha的三个来源：数据、特征、算法

Corey: At the risk of perhaps oversimplifying the world, when I look at the quant process, I sort of see three main silos that alpha can really live in. 1. The data itself — getting your hands on alternative data that nobody else has. 2. Feature generation — people have the same data, but you're able to transform it into observations in a way that nobody else has. 3. The forecasting layer itself — everyone maybe has the same data and the same features, but you can apply some new method that extracts more information in a predictive way.

From where you're sitting today, curious how you would weight where alpha lives in those three buckets. And has your answer changed over the last 5 or 10 years?

Ben: Probably over 10 years it's changed a little.

The first bucket, data access — it is an important part of what we do. It would be naive not to realize that some of that has been commoditized over the last 15 to 20 years. When I started doing modeling within the news space, or really across most vendors, you would reach out and they would say things like, "Well, which company do you want data on?" And we'd say things like, "All of them." And they'd say, "What do you mean all of them?" And you'd say, "We want to track all the companies." "How does that — doesn't make any sense?" Clearly we were the first people interacting with many of these vendors thinking about a different way to package their data.

In those early days, I would say data access as a relative weight was probably larger than it is today. Though it's still important. I just think with more commoditization, it puts pressure on that proprietary data piece. We still have a lot of proprietary data. We have a long history that others might not have, which is very rich. We collect our own trading ideas from crowds and things like that. So we do have a lot of proprietary data — I'm not underselling it. I just think its proportional weight has probably dropped.

Your second part, what you do with it in terms of building features and ideas — I think that is just huge. The ability to think about in a clever and unique way how to take data and come up with really clever ideas of how to use it is just a huge edge. I could say that with confidence because I see how we build successful models that are predictive, and it's the creative process where we say, "Oh, that's really clever" — that drives a lot of what we do. A good part of what's creating this alpha is certainly the human ingenuity layer of how to convert piles of bits into features that are interesting, meaningful, clever, and maybe that you think others haven't thought of.

I'll give you a flavor. I like to talk about a classic one of an analyst. You might say, okay, an analyst is making some decisions. There's a lot of papers out there about whether an analyst target price moves markets, or their upgrade or downgrade. But how many different ways can you study an analyst? At first it's like it's not that much data. But then when you start to think about — well, where does an analyst live? What market are they in compared to who they're covering? How long have they been in this position? Do they go to the same school as the CEO of a company when they're covering them? These are small points, but in aggregate, if you can ask 200 of those questions and you've created 200 new features, it's that long list — "oh yeah, oh that's cool, I hadn't thought of that" — that in aggregate starts to make a difference. Any one of those things, it's like you're right 50.001% of the time — tiny, tiny signals — but it's the aggregation of all of that interesting ideas that I think really makes a huge difference. That's why we have these teams at Two Sigma that are centered around features. It's the core mission.

Your third part was the machine learning algorithms and the science there — that is also a very big part of it. We have a large team called techniques that really focuses on those algorithms themselves and how to make the most out of them. You have these feature-focused teams thinking about all the cool clever data I can make, and you have these techniques teams who are like, "Okay, given cool data, what are all the cool algorithms I can put on it?" It's at the intersectionality of those two areas where we see just amazing things happening. It's experts from different areas with different interests coming together to make predictions, and that diversity of approach really strengthens what we do.

如何在实践中生成有意义的Feature

Corey: I want to talk about the feature side a bit — you gave this example of the analyst: did they go to the same school as the CEO, do they live in the market that they cover? But given that you're trying to come up with all these unique and interesting features, I can imagine a world where I can sit and ideate a whole bunch of features that I would presume probably are meaningless. Like, how tall is the analyst? What color are the analyst's eyes? Do they and the CEO have the same name? Either N is too small or it's just frankly probably irrelevant. Can you walk me a little bit through what the process actually looks like in practice — how is your team taking a piece of widely available data and pulling something out of it that the rest of the market hasn't?

Ben: I think persistence is a part of it. You're right that probably eye color is going to have a prior that's pretty low and probably uninteresting. I'm not going to spend much time on that. In fact, I'd probably avoid it as an indicator because I think it would add noise. So there is a human layer of what's possible there. Our goal is not to just come up with any feature ever we come up of a human.

If we're talking about analysts, there's a lot of features about humans, right? But rather which ones do — oh yeah, where these ideas come from is varied. Obviously there's some academic work which is inspirational at times. I actually saw a paper where they oddly enough — this academic paper actually took analysts and mapped them to posts to try to determine whether they had children or not, and then they used that as an indicator. I won't tell you which direction it goes cuz I have children, so I'm not going to tell you — but we know whether or not having kids affects your ability to predict [laughter] stock prices. That's an example that just makes you understand how far these things can go.

We really stop and think and we approach it by diversity of thought. People from different backgrounds come to these problems — the physicist comes with the mathematician comes with a computer scientist — because they come from different backgrounds, they come up with different ways to ask these questions.

I think also we see things happen in the world. There's a train derailment somewhere, an article written that says, "Hey, this stock is down off this." And you say, "Oh, that's interesting. How do I generalize that?" How do I take this one instance that's happened in the world and — every time something happens to a company somewhere, we're kind of like, "Is that generalizable?" Can we take this instance and think about how you would write it up and play it back over a decade to all companies, or at least all companies in an industry? So we're kind of constantly on the lookout for clever ways to build out these features.

Once you do, what we don't want to do is just throw everything in some giant algorithm, run it, say go, and we're done. We have priors. If your prior is that a train collision is a bad thing — that's your prior — and then you go and study it and it's the opposite sign, that's not great. It kind of breaks with your — you want — you feel like, "Oh, well maybe it's because…" Yeah, you can always post-hoc rationalize anything, but if it doesn't meet your priors, that's sort of the scientific method: you state your hypothesis, you test it, and if it doesn't go the way you said, you can't just be like, "Well, I meant the opposite direction." We're approaching things scientifically that way, and we have to verify our priors when we can. That's part of the process. We have clean features, clean hypotheses, clean usage, and ultimately we feel like that ends up with a set of data that we can use to be predictive.

特征爆炸下的共线性问题

Corey: One of the things that strikes me here is if you have a team that is just constantly searching for features, there's the risk that they do identify novel interesting features, but those features may have a high degree of collinearity with other features that it may not seem like they do. So you're generating hundreds if not thousands of features that are independently interesting on their own, but actually make the portfolio construction process far more complicated. I'm curious how you deal with that.

Ben: So our job at its core every day is to come up with orthogonal and novel predictions. It's kind of easy to come up with things that have correlated to what we've done in the past, and that's the lowest hanging fruit. So almost by definition, we wake up every day as an alpha modeler searching for orthogonality. Collinearity is the death of a signal.

If you found something that someone else is trading through a different data set — I give a classic example of trading news data. You could say, "Okay, I'm going to trade news sentiment," and this is my new sentiment model — but what if it's just picking up post-earnings stories? What you call news sentiment is just chasing earnings numbers. You're sitting there saying, "I got this novel data source," and all it is is a dirty version of earnings numbers. You might get excited and think you found something clever, but by the time you've run through the tools, you've explored correlation structures — which is just a part of the job. If you can't do that, then you're not going to make it.

We have certain rules and thresholds where we are comfortable only when new alpha signals have a certain amount of orthogonality, because we just don't believe that throwing a bunch of things in that are equally correlated to things in the past is useful. It's core to what we do — you're driven by orthogonality, driven to avoid correlation. Your goal is to come up with the next signal that's not so correlated with our previous ones. It's kind of where you start. It's not even just like an after-the-fact thing.

预测周期与Feature的生命周期

Corey: How aware do the analysts have to be about the horizon over which they're trying to ultimately forecast returns and back out which features would be relevant to that horizon? I can imagine some features are highly relevant to the high-frequency space or the super slow-frequency space. How much is that in the back of their mind?

Ben: It's a big part of it. If your hypothesis is that you're studying people visiting stores — you have a good indication that people have been visiting Walmart a lot this week — you probably don't think that that's a signal that's going to be priced in in the next 5 seconds. It's got to make its way through, eventually show up on some same-store sales release, and it's going to be months away. Different signals have different hypotheses of how they're going to find a way in. So yeah, models are definitely thinking about that and aware — it's kind of built into what they're studying, often, what horizon.

Corey: I want to talk about that same-store sales data example. When that data maybe first comes out and there's not a lot of people using it, the horizon over which it forecasts may be slower — and then as more and more people get access to that data and act on it faster, you could actually imagine it getting the market moving on it much much faster. Same feature, but the forecast horizon continues to speed up until maybe the point it becomes so efficient it's no longer relevant. How does that play through the life cycle of features for you?

Ben: I mean, it is the life cycle of features. Your goal is to create something so creative that it doesn't come under that pressure. That's not always going to happen. There are going to be other firms that study things and put pressure on. So yeah, you do see exactly what you described — things speed up, and sometimes to the point where the signal is no longer viable.

We're all trying to be as creative as possible so that we don't get that pressure. At the end of the day, we have monitoring in place that tracks what's happening with a particular model, and at the firm level, the portfolio level is watching that and deciding how hard to trade any signal based on their own analysis. We as modelers are making these predictions; the portfolio itself, those that run on the portfolio, which is a whole another quant function, are going to be monitoring for that type of thing and setting weights, looking for alpha decay and appropriately setting weights to match that.

如何招聘"有创造力"的Quant

Corey: I want to talk about the people side here. What you're talking about sounds like a very creative role, and when people talk about quants, they typically think about the hard technical skills — math, statistics, coding. A lot of the features you're talking about require a degree of ingenuity and creative thinking, lateral thinking. How do you go about finding those people?

Ben: For a team like mine that looks for features, during an interview process, you have conversations that are open-ended. You ask, "Hey, here's some data. If you had access to a fire hose from social media, tell me all the cool things you could predict." Very open-ended questions that are not "solve this math equation" but rather things that don't have a right or wrong answer. The question is: how many clever ways and how many things that I've never heard anyone else say are they saying? That's a good indicator.

It reminds me — back in graduate school, I was looking at an interview at Microsoft. I guess they had these product testers as one of the roles. And the question to me was: you have a vending machine, your goal is to test it — how would you test the vending machine? Seems like a silly question, but there's no right or wrong answer. I just started talking for however long — you know, if you stick your hand in, does it cut off your hand? What happens if you unplug it? Can you put a piece of paper in? Can you put a coin on a rope? What if you shake it? I can go on and on. I'm always like, "Oh, what's next? What's next?"

So we're looking for that kind of spark of people who like to think and solve problems on sort of a continuous basis and can always find the next question. Although I don't think it goes for anything like the same role and I've never asked that specific question, it is inspirational in its openness — and obviously it's been around a while since somebody asked it to me 20 years ago.

AI与LLM的登场："任何东西都可以是语言"

Corey: Let's pivot to AI, which is where we're going to spend the majority of the rest of this conversation. In our pre-call, you said something that really stuck with me — I'm quoting here: "NLP was about turning language into numbers. With LLMs, I can turn numbers into language. Anything can be language now." I'd love you to just unpack what you meant by that. What does that shift mean practically? What does that mean for the types of features that can be built today that couldn't be done 3 to 5 years ago?

Ben: For a firm like Two Sigma that's been trading off of textual data for over 15 years, getting more textual data is pretty exciting. We've been collecting all sorts of text about the world, and there is a limited amount of things that you can collect out there. The internet has its bounds. Vendors have their bounds. You can only analyze what you can get your hands on.

What's really exciting about LLMs is that they generate textual data. So this idea of what is proprietary data — maybe it's a little wider tent than we thought. Before it was just data that we went out and collected or that we had unique access to, but now it's data that we can generate. Every output of an LLM is another textual data set.

For the group and the team and the company that's been studying that stuff for so long, and then here comes this technology — it's just like, if you know how to use oil and you've run low on oil and then somebody has this giant oil hose that's coming out — it's exciting. It's this revolution of opportunity.

What I meant about numbers into text was simply that you can have a conversation about anything with an LLM. You can upload an Excel sheet or your personal finances at home and you can say, "Tell me about it," and what you're getting out is text. That text, if it has meaning, is interesting in itself. I find that to be a really exciting moment for those of us who have been studying the use of text this way, because it feels like suddenly this onslaught of new information that is bigger than anything we've ever seen. It's a huge opportunity.

ROI崩塌：以前不敢做的Feature，现在都能做了

Corey: You gave this really interesting example during our pre-call about measuring micro-expressions during CEO earnings calls — measuring how often they blink or where their eyes go when they say certain words. Years ago, undertaking a project like that would have required a dedicated computer vision team. Today, with LLMs, the costs have collapsed. How has that changed the types of features your team is now willing to explore?

Ben: Massively, massively changed things. Everything was kind of return on investment. You have a prior — does the fact that the CEO is blinking three times more than they used to in this call... we have a baseline for every CEO (this is hypothetical, by the way). If you study CEOs historically and look at how much they're blinking, you could create a z-score of, "Well, how many standard deviations up or down is their blinking going?" Is that meaningful? Part of you is probably saying, "Yeah, that's kind of weird — why are they spiking in blinking?" So part of me says yes, that's an interesting signal, I want to look at that.

But then if someone said to you, "Well, it's going to take you six months of your team's time to study because you have to download things and run vision, bake these features," you're going to say, "Well, it's a good idea, but it's not that good idea. I have a lot to do here and I'd rather look at earnings numbers than CEO blinkings. I have higher priors on this data set."

But if you can really bring that ROI down — if that investment plummets, it really changes some of the calculation. All these ideas you had swimming around like, "I wish I could see this, I wish that would be cool" — suddenly they're viable. If you have 2,000 ideas in your list of things that you're never going to get to and suddenly you can look at them and say, "Hey, I can get to all this cool stuff I've been wanting to do" — it really frees the mind from having to worry so much about implementation as a constraint.

As a scientist, it's kind of like you work in a lab and suddenly there's no budget for your equipment. It changes things when you don't have to worry about building the particle collider — you have a thousand in front of you. Similarly, if you don't have to worry about a giant investment to answer every question, it just frees you to reorder your things and not worry so much about the investment part.

Does that mean that everything falls out for free out of an LLM? No, I'm not suggesting this is all solved. But it certainly can be 100 times faster than it used to be for a lot of these projects, and that opens up a lot of opportunity.

反驳：AI是否会让Edge快速消失？

Corey: One of the counters here might be that if LLMs help make feature creation much cheaper and the technology in and of itself is no longer a moat — you have fairly democratized access — it might be that edge gets eroded much more quickly. Even in those harder edge cases where edge was over this wall of difficulty, that wall has come down. How do you think about that critique?

Ben: It's absolutely the case that people can build features faster not just at Two Sigma but everywhere if they want to. So there is the lowering of that moat. On the counter side, I would say we have such deep experience building these things at scale that we're shovel-ready for this change. It's our DNA. When you have this 10x increase and you've been set up to systematically understand how to use these things, it's just really exciting. You're saying, "Oh wow, how do we sort through this? We have all this data now."

It is true that people can make a lot of data. I think we can make it at home on our laptops — you can generate as much as you want. But the then what is still really key. While the ability to create a data set is not the moat, the how, the what, is very much part of that. Experience is a big part of that. The fact that we've been doing this for so long just makes us ready for that moment in time.

AI的隐藏风险："降低输出的熵"

Corey: In our pre-call, you raised a concern about AI tooling specifically, and you had a great quote — you described the risk as AI potentially "lowering the entropy of the output." I'd love for you to expand on what you meant by that.

Ben: This is really interesting. Everyone says automation, let's automate everything — and it sounds right. We all want to be on the beach with tools doing our jobs for us, right? Why not? But automation is a nuanced thing.

If I'm building a cardboard box, automation — I want the output of that thing to be the same every time. I want my cardboard box to look like it is, I'm on an assembly line, and that's fine. The thing about adding alpha to a portfolio is you want the opposite. As we talked about earlier, we want orthogonality. We want originality. So if you over-automate, you could end up with a system that — it doesn't matter who's using it — it creates the same thing. And if everyone starts creating the same thing, your portfolio is not going to look nearly as good.

What might strengthen a portfolio is diversity. We all know that's a classic investing component. At a place like Two Sigma, it's the hundreds and thousands of models, each with their own data, hypothesis, researcher — this is key — each with their own person behind it that leads to that orthogonality. When these things come together, the correlation is low enough that they can make really strong predictions.

The fear is that if you give everyone a push-button tool — throw all your data in and make a model — they all hit the button and they all point at the same place, they're all going to be putting in the same output. "Wow, I did that really fast, that's wonderful. But we're all doing the same thing." That's terrifying to me for AI in an industry like ours where diversity is the point.

How do we fight back? We see AI as an amplification tool of our originality. Going back to that correlation question you asked earlier — I want to build tools that allow our people to multiply what's unique about them. So when I use the tool and you use the tool and we point to the same data set, because we approach it differently, historically do different things, have different thoughts, different ideas — the tools need to understand that. You can do that in AI via things like context that allows them to navigate the pathways and come up with different answers even though we're using the same tool.

If a tool doesn't allow two people to come up with different answers — if those two people have different backgrounds, a physicist and a computer scientist try to solve the problem and they end up with the same answer — I don't like that. That makes me nervous. We really need to think of these tools as amplification of what we're good at as humans, which is sort of originality and orthogonality. That's kind of front to our AI strategy when it comes to empowering our modeling teams.

Rational Inattention：现在硬啃 vs. 等能力成熟？

Corey: Years ago, I heard of this idea called rational inattention — that it can make complete sense to ignore something. That's been front of mind for me with LLMs, where the pace of capability improvement has been so rapid and exponential. Spending time trying to fight against the LLM may not make sense when it could be a solved problem in 12 to 18 months. So for any given idea, you can try today and fight against the technology, or you can wait 12–18 months when the idea is much more trivial. How do you think about that trade-off?

Ben: First of all, the difficulty is important to lean into just to understand what it is. The very act of being unable to solve a problem because the technology is not there yet — it's like having a flashlight seeing the wall that's blocking you. If you can't see that wall and then someone comes and they're like, "I can blow up a wall," and you're like, "I don't know where the wall is, so let's start looking for walls" — it's too late.

So I think it's healthy to push the boundaries faster than the tools come, so that when those tools appear — if you haven't solved that problem, which you might have — you know exactly where that tool goes because you've been, darn it, hitting that wall over and over again for six months. And it's like, "If only I could solve this," and this new technology drops and you're like, "Aha, this is what I need." So it's really important as a technology-first company to get out ahead of what's happening, to understand the limits.

I'll also say — you mentioned 12 to 18 months. Beating any competitor to a market as competitive as trying to trade on alpha signals — 18 months can be an eternity. If you are the only one in the market taking certain approaches, your alpha signals are just much more impactful when you're out first. Waiting 18 months, you might save yourself some engineering headache, but those 18 months can be quite exciting to be out coming up with ideas before the market is caught up.

基础设施选择：自建 vs. 用云端Frontier模型

Corey: I want to talk about the infrastructure side. There's sort of two major choices. You can use models that are living in someone else's data center — which introduces some IP concerns or dependency concerns about a vendor's versioning, pricing, roadmap. Versus you could bring everything on premise — that's going to come with its own costs. How is Two Sigma thinking about this set of trade-offs?

Ben: In the end, it's about diversification. You don't want to lean into any one way of doing things because then you're held hostage by the limitations there. That means you have to build in a way that will allow for growth. You don't want to make assumptions about the models that you're utilizing. If you're going to use a model — be it something from a vendor, some external frontier model, or something you've built internally — you want to make sure that your stack has the ability to swap that around when needed.

Maybe the trade-off you didn't mention, but I think is actually the most important, is control of the life cycle of a model. If you use a frontier model — let's say you're using something from Anthropic — and then they say, "Okay, well, we're turning this off in six months," and you're like, "But I just tuned all these models on it." You've created a situation where you have to now upgrade your thing to the next one. That's fine, but it is a moving target that you have no control over if it's externally hosted. If you want control, certainly internally hosted models help a lot there.

The other thing to be aware of is probably just look-ahead bias. If you don't understand what a model's trained on, you risk having it kind of fool you into thinking something that's not real. These models as they get more complex, it's going to be harder and harder to understand what's deep in their psyche. Does it connect the company Enron with negativity inherently in the back of the LLM, such that when you ask it anything about Enron in some point in time basis — you go back in time, you say, "Okay, well, what do you think?" "Bad." "Tell me about the stock." "Bad, bad, bad." And you're like, "I haven't even asked the question yet."

So you have to be careful, and control is important. All else being equal, you would prefer to have full control. However, it's also hard to not recognize that the frontier labs are pushing boundaries at a rate that is impressive. You need to balance both — that control so you can understand what's happening — with the state of the art. Figuring out when to use both is part of the journey.

P-Hacking与过拟合的风险

Corey: With more data and more capability to research ideas — both more novel and potentially cheaper and faster — I sort of feel like I have to ask the cliché question about the risk of p-hacking and overfitting. How are you dealing with this institutionally?

Ben: A couple things. First of all, if you as a researcher overfit terribly and somehow we don't catch it during the process, then ultimately your model is not going to do very well. That's going to catch up with you in your career, in your success, in what you're doing. Your job is to create models that can trade into the future. If you've overfit on the past so much that you can't do that — you can't overfit the future and succeed. You can and fail. There is a natural point that anybody building a model should be self-checking.

That being said, humans can get excited and fool themselves when they see data. They can come up with, "Hey, this is great, I found this thing, I'm so excited." How do we check for that? This is a problem we've been facing for a very long time. We've been doing scaled experiments with machine learning and large data sets for a long time. Questions about p-hacking are implicit in how we do things. We've developed tools to run tests, statistical tests. We have priors. There's just a lot of best practices that we've employed to protect us from too much p-hacking and too much overfitting.

Now, it's important to note that everybody overfits by definition — because the future is not the same as the past. If you've trained on the past and you're predicting the future, you overfit. Congratulations. The world changed since yesterday. So it's not about not overfitting — let's not pretend we don't overfit. Everything is overfit to some degree. It's about overfitting to a degree that you're comfortable with, that you can measure, and making sure that despite the fact that there's going to be some noise, you still have a signal that you're proud of and that's robust to the trading environment.

We can, at Two Sigma, monitor our models, monitor our portfolios, and we can measure — if we're overfitting, how much, how do we adjust for that — systematically at scale.

Does AI make that more scary? Yes, because if you have a thousand people running a thousand experiments, it's a lot more scary than three people running a thousand experiments. So we do need to make sure the education's there, that we're doing the checks, that we're doing robustness, that we're not letting our guard down as scale increases. It is putting pressure on those worries. But we're experienced. We feel robust to it. We're ready for the fact that things are scaling faster than we're used to.

数据升级：为什么不总是要重新训练模型？

Corey: We've been spending a lot of time talking about feature engineering. I do want to draw towards the close and ask about the data side. In our pre-call you made what I thought was a really interesting comment — data libraries that come out aren't necessarily fixed. You can get upgrades, better coverage, cleaner data, longer history. A lot of people would intuitively rebuild their model with new data. You pushed back on that. Talk me through that view.

Ben: It's an interesting nuanced question. One of the hardest parts about moving from academia to a place like Two Sigma is that in academia, you're focused on your PhD thesis. You're focused on this result, and your goal is to be the best thing you can ever do at this one thing. Putting all your things into this one way of doing things. If you can just eke out a new finding in this very specific thing, that's often part of that journey. And there's nothing wrong with that.

But if your job is to make a diverse portfolio, you might believe that the last 10% of gains in that journey is 90% of the work. That's the most novel stuff. There are diminishing returns as you push in one area to make it improve. What we really believe is that time might be better spent doing 10 things at 90% capacity than one thing at 95% capacity. If you're looking for a diverse portfolio, looking at it from 10 different ways — not necessarily the best you could ever do with another five years of work.

Any one of our models could be a PhD thesis. It could be a three-month project or it could be a five-year project. We all want to make things better. It's built in all of us. What makes a good researcher is like, "Okay, wait, no, no, stop, that's enough" — either it looks good enough to walk away and ship it (that's hard for people cuz they want to do better), or this isn't working, walk away (which is also hard). That skill, I think, is what makes a great quant at a place like Two Sigma — the ability to know when to move on.

Which brings us to your question about the fact that things change in the world. You have to ask yourself, when data gets better, when an LLM improves in its ability, when there's a new algorithm — in a perfect world, you could hit a button and everything would get retrained. Sure, that's great. But in the real world, things are more nuanced. You've built a model with certain assumptions. If you go and you restart that process — see my previous comment about a PhD student — you might be improving it, you might make it 10% better. But what is the time trade-off of making your thing 10% better versus going and building something completely different?

We really believe that it's that diversity of approach that we put first. Does that mean we don't ever update things? No, we do all the time. If we see opportunity, we will chase that opportunity down. But we do it with a business lens: what is the ROI of the time versus how much better do I really think this thing can get with this new data feed? A vendor turns from V3 to V4 — I could retrain my whole project, or I could say, "Look, I've already got something that uses that type of data. It's doing pretty well. Why not keep going on my agenda doing something else?" I will maybe go back and visit it if I think it's the best use of my time, but often it's not — just because there's so much of the world we haven't yet captured. If you're looking for orthogonality, it's rarely just going to be in "oh, this vendor made something slightly better."

规模化的特异性：AI打开的新战线

Corey: The intersection of data and AI. One of the ideas you brought up on our prep call was this idea of idiosyncrasy at scale. Historically, hyper-company-specific analysis was hard to use. But AI allows you to do something hyper-company-specific while simultaneously scaling it. Expand on that.

Ben: I use that term a lot — at scale — to inspire people who have generally maybe shied away from very company-specific measurement because they're looking for larger sample sizes with more companies and larger opportunity. You can believe that if you trade one company, it's less opportunity than if you trade 100. If you have a feature with one company, it's less exciting than one about 100 companies. That's kind of natural.

But the thing about AI is that it opens up this unique ability to dig in on a specific company and build categories of features that might be specific to that company, but might also be generalizable to other companies. You can reason your way down to the very very specific things about a company and then generalize that. What's my outlook on sales, or I don't know — the path there might be very idiosyncratic to a company.

If you think about what a discretionary investor does, a lot of it is just deep knowledge about some of the things that they're looking at. These things are hard to pick out at scale when looking at large earnings numbers — they're usually very company-specific opportunities that arise that aren't going to show up in that space. If you want to expand to be more competitive in that type of analysis, but also have this kind of scale attitude of Two Sigma, I deep down believe that AI is a big part of that opportunity. Because we can use AI to do something at 3,000 companies at once — while the thing it's doing for those 3,000 companies is itself unique.

That's an interesting thing — to both think about scale and specificity at the same time. Big part of some of the exciting expansions that we're thinking about here, and how to use AI to just go deeper and deeper into the more bespoke and hard-to-model parts of company data.

给年轻Quant的职业建议

Corey: One of my long-running jokes is that when I went to grad school in 2009, the capstone project of my grad school was pricing credit default swaps — which became a totally irrelevant skill on Wall Street within one year of me graduating. For feature engineers and researchers today, the landscape is moving very quickly. As you're working with junior feature engineers thinking about their careers, what should they be thinking about? What skills or instincts will compound the most over the next decade?

Ben: With the change of AI, the technical skills of being able to code up your idea or put it into a particular format to execute it are obviously going to drop in relative value — to the idea, to the skill of telling those things how to do or what to do. Somebody who is very adept at building the thing that somebody tells them to build — I feel like that as a skill is growing less valuable, because if you can ask an AI to build it, that person's additive value is less.

Where does that leave us? First, getting comfortable with AI and its capabilities. Whoever's in school right now is going to be going into a world with more AI. What you find with each generation of people that are coming out of, into, Two Sigma right now as we hire from schools is that they're more and more AI-native, more and more expecting things to look a certain way because that's what they have at school. It's happening very quickly.

You need to be comfortable how to use AI to bring your own skills forward. Not the skill of just "quote, build a website" — but what's unique to you? What does your knowledge and your education bring that you can do that others can't? How do you use AI to amplify that?

To succeed, you need to both stand out (see my orthogonality comment before) and scale. AI as a tool — many people think, "Oh, it's going to replace me or replace this role." I'm saying, no, no, no. Let it amplify you. Don't look at it as, "Oh, it's doing the thing I did." Say, "Wow, it can help me do what I did way better and faster, and I can use my ideas and just scale up what I want to do before, but much faster."

Maybe that's the main takeaway: getting comfortable with how to use this next generation of algorithms to do really unique and clever and creative things is going to be the skill that's going to carry over. Ultimately, it's going to be a world where there's going to be a lot of interacting through human language. The amount that your people are coding is going to go down, the amount of people that are prompting is going to go up. Therefore, it's the ideas that are going to carry the day. How do you use this in the most effective and creative way to get what you need done? A focus on that is kind of key.

Ben的个人痴迷：公共数据

Corey: I want to end with the same question I've asked everyone this season: what is something that you are currently obsessed with — ideally outside of work? An idea, literature, art, movie, something you can't get out of your mind. What is your obsession and why?

Ben: I am kind of obsessed with public data and the government — really. I know this is related to my work because I'm a data person, but done personally. I think it's fascinating that there's all this information out there — the parking tickets that we get, who gets stopped for speeding, health inspections at restaurants — there's so much interesting stuff in there. I really enjoy exploring the opportunity of how we can make our cities and society run better by utilizing the data that's already there.

The nice thing about AI is that it used to be that only data scientists could go in and use this data — you had to have the skill to utilize it. But if you don't need that skill anymore and you can just ask questions, suddenly that data is going to be driving power for urban planners and lawyers and anyone. It just democratizes the access to information for a public institution. I find that really exciting and inspiring — that there's no more tech requirement, or there won't be soon, between you and the questions you might have on public data. So that's an area that I study and explore and nerd out on related to my work, but also on a personal basis.

Corey: I would suggest that the more and more people who get tuned into that, the more it makes local or federal government look less efficient — the quicker that data will be pulled. [laughter] We'll be able to track how useful that information is over time.

Well, look, Ben, this has been a fantastic chat. I really appreciate you taking the time out of your day to share all this with the listeners. Thank you so much.

Ben: Yeah, a great time and a great conversation. Thanks so much for your time.

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Two Sigma的"反自动化"悖论
大多数人听到AI+量化，第一反应是"更快、更多、更自动"。但Ben在整段对话中埋藏了一个尖锐的矛盾——**他领导的团队正在极力对抗自动化的均质化倾向**。他用"造纸箱"和"造Alpha"的对比一针见血：纸箱要求每次输出一模一样，而Alpha要求每次输出**不一样**。
这里藏着一个几乎所有AI布道者都忽略的深层结构：**当工具的边际成本趋近于零，工具本身产生的"熵"也会趋近于零**。也就是说，人人都能一键生成特征时，特征的信息含量反而在坍塌。Two Sigma的策略不是"用AI做得更多"，而是"用AI放大每个研究员的怪癖"。这是一种**反熵减的AI使用哲学**——与硅谷主流叙事完全相反。
## 二、一个跨领域连接：Ben其实在做"生态学"，不是"金融学"
留意Ben反复使用的词汇：**orthogonality（正交）、diversity（多样性）、correlation death（相关性即死亡）、ecosystem of models（模型生态）**。这套语言体系其实来自**种群生物学与生态系统理论**，不是传

item.title: [Lex Clips] Historian explains human nature | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：历史学家反而是"人性恒常论"的最坚定拥护者

大多数人以为，越是深入研究不同时代的历史学家，越会觉得"人是被文化塑造的"。但Kaldellis的立场恰恰相反——他见过太多时代，反而更相信人性的地板是硬的。这里藏着一个隐秘的悖论：只有真正跨越千年的人，才敢说"人没变多少"；只在当代切片里打转的学者，反而更容易相信"每个时代的人都是全新物种"。视野越窄，越容易高估差异；视野越宽，越能看见重复。

这与天文学家的处境惊人地相似——望远镜看得越远，反而越确信物理定律在宇宙各处一致。人文学科的"后现代切片论"，本质上是一种近视性的谦逊：因为看不清远处，就假设远处一定不同。

跨领域连接：Thucydides、控制论与"文化调音台"

Kaldellis用了一个极精妙的隐喻——文化不创造旋钮，只调节旋钮的位置（dials go from 1 to 10）。这几乎是一比一的控制论（cybernetics）模型：系统的参数空间是给定的，反馈回路只是在参数空间里游走。

把这个模型套到三个看似无关的领域，会产生奇怪的共振： - 机器学习：预训练模型是"人性"，微调是"文化"。你无法用微调让GPT变成一只章鱼，但可以让它在同一套权重上呈现出维多利亚绅士或赛博朋克黑客。 - 音乐：十二平均律是"人性"，巴洛克与蓝调是"文化"。音符集有限，组合无限。 - 免疫系统：VDJ重组的组合空间是"人性"，你遇到的抗原是"文化"。

真正的洞察在这里：如果文化只是调音而非造琴，那么"文化冲突"的本质，不是不同物种的战争，而是同一台钢琴上不同调式的争执。这解释了为什么历史上敌对文明总能在几代之内互相通婚、互相翻译、互相皈依——因为琴是同一台。

隐藏的系统性真相：Steve Jobs与拜占庭皇帝共享同一种"时机技能"

Kaldellis轻描淡写地把Steve Jobs、Jaron Lanier和"戴奇怪帽子的家伙"并列，这看似是随口一提，但其实揭示了一个被历史学严重低估的现象：在参数被文化锁死的时代，极少数人拥有"越级调参"的能力。

这不是"英雄史观"的复辟。这是一种更微妙的说法——大多数人是文化的执行者，极少数人是文化的调音师。他们的技能不是"聪明"或"努力"，而是一种时机感（temporal fit）：在系统临界点上，用最小的力做出最大的偏折。

这与复杂系统理论里的"敏感依赖"完全同构——混沌系统里，蝴蝶不是更强壮的蝴蝶，而是恰好停在分岔点上的蝴蝶。乔布斯不是比同时代工程师聪明十倍，而是恰好站在个人计算从工具向身份转变的分岔点。查士丁尼不是比其他罗马皇帝更有远见，而是恰好在古典晚期崩塌的窗口期出手。

啊哈时刻：如果这个模型成立，那么"改变历史的能力"就不是可培养的天赋，而是可识别的时空坐标。真正的战略问题不是"如何成为乔布斯"，而是"如何识别自己正站在哪一个分岔点上"。历史学在这个意义上，比商学院更接近创业学——它教你阅读系统何时松动。

📝 转录文本

引子：人性有没有一个"恒常层"？

Lex Fridman：你之前说过，罗马帝国、也许古希腊、也许更早的社会，还有现在——我们所生活的社会——它们之间是非常不同的。所以在应用历史的教训时，必须小心。但是，是不是有一些东西在我们人类身上是相同的？在你研究过的几百年、几个世纪的一切中，是否存在一些人性方面是持续存在的？

后现代主义的"切片论"与它的问题

Anthony Kaldellis：你看，从思想上讲，我经历过一些阶段。比如我读研究生的时候——那时候大致上是所谓"后现代主义"还活跃、还没被彻底踹一脚的年代——很常见地会遇到一些同事，他们不相信存在所谓"人性"这种东西。

换句话说，他们不认为人类心理的参数——是的，其中有相当大的变异——但都处在某一组规范之内。比如，我们不会像不同物种那样行事。而他们不相信有这种东西存在。也就是说，他们认为整个人类历史本质上是一系列互不相通的差异切片（incommensurate slices of difference），你根本没法从一个切片走到下一个。

我认为这是一种非常有问题的观点，出于各种理由。比如说：你要把这些切片切得多细？

站在修昔底德一边：人性的"底色"

我在这一点上是站在修昔底德（Thucydides）这边的。他说，人类多多少少还是会……嗯，他们会追求他们所追求的东西。他们爱，他们恨，他们有野心，他们也无能。你会发现——在任何一个大约20到30人的群体里——你几乎都能找到同样的那几种类型，几乎覆盖了人性多样性的全部光谱。

文化的角色：调节旋钮，而不是重造机器

那么，文化随后介入，并且以各种奇怪的方式微调这些参数。而作为一个历史学家，这正是我想研究的。我喜欢研究文化如何拨动那些旋钮（set the dials）。

但那些旋钮的刻度是从1到10的。大多数人对此的反应是："嗯，是的，我大致同意。"

例外：那些能扭转潮流的少数人

不过偶尔——偶尔——会出现少数几个人。不管是史蒂夫·乔布斯（Steve Jobs），还是杰伦·拉尼尔（Jaron Lanier），或者某个留着奇怪胡子的家伙，或者戴着滑稽帽子的人。这些人可以扭转历史的潮流。

Lex：我也这么认为。

Kaldellis：偶尔，是的。

Lex：但他们只是在正确的时刻、正确的时机，拥有极致的技艺。

Kaldellis：噢，这一点我也相信。是的。

反对"文化极权论"：人可以跳出框架思考

换句话说，我也不认为文化系统或意识形态是那种极为有效的"极权装置"——好像它们能殖民每一个人的心智，让一种文化中的每个人都变成克隆体。不。你有时候会在历史学术研究中遇到这种倾向。

Lex：嗯。

Kaldellis：不，我认为人有时候就是可以为自己思考，他们可以跳出框架思考，他们可以做各种事情。当我面对历史中的行动者（historical agents）时，我始终希望让自己的心智对这种可能性保持开放。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：历史学家反而是"人性恒常论"的最坚定拥护者
大多数人以为，越是深入研究不同时代的历史学家，越会觉得"人是被文化塑造的"。但Kaldellis的立场恰恰相反——他见过太多时代，反而更相信人性的地板是硬的。这里藏着一个隐秘的悖论：**只有真正跨越千年的人，才敢说"人没变多少"；只在当代切片里打转的学者，反而更容易相信"每个时代的人都是全新物种"**。视野越窄，越容易高估差异；视野越宽，越能看见重复。
这与天文学家的处境惊人地相似——望远镜看得越远，反而越确信物理定律在宇宙各处一致。人文学科的"后现代切片论"，本质上是一种**近视性的谦逊**：因为看不清远处，就假设远处一定不同。
## 跨领域连接：Thucydides、控制论与"文化调音台"
Kaldellis用了一个极精妙的隐喻——**文化不创造旋钮，只调节旋钮的位置**（dials go from 1 to 10）。这几乎是一比一的控制论（cybernetics）模型：系统的参数空间是给定的，反馈回路只是在参数空间里游走。
把这个模型套到三个看似无关的领域，会产生奇怪的共振： - **机器学习**：预训

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1448期】AI FOMO进入关键拐点，下半年美股展望✨20260705#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Meta"卖算力"其实是投降的仪式，不是新业务

大多数人把 Meta 计划出售 AI 算力当作"新业务线"来读——但如果把它放在博弈论的坐标里看，这更像是大模型军备竞赛的正式退出仪式。理由极其反常识：任何一家真心要造前沿模型的公司，都不会在算力短缺的窗口期把稀缺资源卖给对手。Anthropic 出价再高，你自己训下一代模型的机会成本一定更高。所以小扎"愿意卖"的动作本身，就是在向市场传递一个他嘴上不会承认的信号——Llama 不再瞄准前沿。这也是为什么这条新闻和"小扎承认 AI Agent 落地不及预期"几乎同周放出：一个是对外的退场，一个是对内的重构。视频作者点到了 CapEx 拐点，但没点破的是——拐点的本质不是花钱变少，而是"造模"派系在 Meta 内部输给了"变现"派系。

跨领域连接：这是"云计算 2010"剧本的复刻，只是主角换成了推理算力

作者反复讲 Anthropic 70% 毛利、Hyperscaler 只吃 12 美元云费——这幅图景与 2010 年前后 Netflix 建立在 AWS 之上的那段关系几乎一比一同构。当年 AWS 是"卖水的"，Netflix 是"淘金的"，Netflix 越赚，AWS 越赚，直到 Netflix 大到必须自建 CDN（Open Connect）才把话语权拿回来。今天的剧本：Anthropic 是新 Netflix，Hyperscaler + Meta 的闲置算力是新 AWS。真正的预言不是"Meta 会不会卖算力"，而是"Anthropic 什么时候开始自建"——那一刻才是 AI 基建通胀真正见顶的信号，而不是 CapEx 增速放缓。这个类比还给出一个投资侧的隐含推论：视频里说市场"短视地只买硬件、不买大科技"是矛盾的——但历史告诉我们，卖水人的估值总是在淘金人证明能长期赚钱之前先见顶。所以硬件回调的时点，可能不是 Anthropic 变差的那一天，而是 Anthropic 变得太好、好到要自己动手的那一天。

隐藏的系统性真相：Token 价格是整个 AI 生态的"利率"

作者提到"Token 太贵，所以 Agent 落不了地"，但没有把这个观察系统化。事实上，Token 单价在 AI 经济里扮演的角色，等同于利率在传统经济里的角色： - Token 便宜 → 试错成本低 → Agent、长链推理、多智能体涌现（相当于低利率下的创业潮） - Token 贵 → 只有 ROI 最高的场景（Coding）能烧得起 → 生态窄化（相当于高利率下的资产集中）

这也解释了一个更深的悖论：中国开源模型对美国大科技，短期是利空、长期是"降息"。作者已经隐约看到了这一层（Coinbase 用开源模型压成本反而烧更多 Token），但没有把结论推到位——如果开源模型持续压低 Token 价格，下一波真正的 AI Alpha 不会出现在模型层，而会出现在"高 Token 消耗的应用层"：Agent 编排、企业流程自动化、垂类 SaaS。软件板块估值现在处于低位，恰恰是"AI 降息周期"尚未定价的死角。作者押注下半年故事线切回软件——从这个视角看，逻辑比他自己表达的还要坚实：这不是风格轮动，这是货币政策转向。

一个额外的意外角度：情绪面数据里的"英特尔悖论"

作者提到英特尔一天涨 9%、第二天跌 8%，用来说明散户情绪不稳。但更有意思的观察是——英特尔本身在他的仓位里超过 20%，是第一大重仓，却又是最能体现"市场疯狂"的标的。这构成一个投资心理学上的镜像：当你重仓的股票开始变成市场情绪的温度计时，你实际上在同时下注 α 和 β。这也是很多老练投资者最容易被自己骗的地方——你以为你在拿一家公司的基本面，实际上你的日 P&L 已经被市场情绪劫持了。这不是仓位管理问题，是认知锚点错位：视频里作者的框架（估值中性偏低、故事线切换、7-10% 回调）非常清晰，但仓位结构其实和框架有轻微的内在张力。这一点作者自己未必意识到——一个真正健康的信号，可能不是"英特尔不减仓"，而是"英特尔的日波动降到 3% 以内"。

📝 转录文本

投资TALK君1448期 | AI FOMO进入关键拐点，下半年美股展望

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场奉闲小陶。欢迎大家来到周日的更新。

不知道大家有没有时间看世界杯啊，我也是因为去看了世界杯，所以偶尔逃了几天课，没有关系，我还是回来了。

今天来为大家做一个更新呢： - 第一部分：我们先回顾一下上周资产价格的走势。上周其实是走的不错的，那么三大指数都出现了比较好的反弹，主要是体现在周一和周二的反弹。为什么出现反弹？在上周日那期视频里面，我给了非常明确的信号，在我们这个频道比较久的一些观众应该知道我给了什么信号，回过头去看一下上周日那期视频。 - 第二部分：我想聊一下上周和 Meta 相关的两条新闻，那这两条新闻其实和 AI 在整个行业里面的一个进展是有关系的。 - 第三部分：在上周三的时候，我在 APP 里面做了一个直播，那一期直播的话题是对于上半年的总结和下半年的展望，我今天抽几个重点出来和大家分享一下。

好吗？那我就废话不多说了，马上开始。在开始之前，大家记得留言点赞加转发，下一周我尽量做到起码和大家见面三次，好不好？

一、上周资产价格走势回顾

美元指数下跌，美债收益率出现反弹，TLT 下跌了 2%——1%，那再次把 YTD 的走势来到了负的 1.9%。上周的美债市场做得不是非常的好，起码不是凯文·沃什所喜欢的。两年期美债收益率上涨代表加息的概率出现了上涨，然后 10 年期美债收益率上涨的幅度更大，那又代表了房贷市场这一块的利率出现反弹。这并不是他所希望看到的——整个债券市场并没有往他所预期的方向去走。他所希望看到的是通过非常鹰派的言语，使得偿债这块的利率出现下降。

三大指数这边就像我前面说，上周都出现了比较好的反弹。IGV 上一周反弹的幅度非常的大，比特币上涨了 3.2%。

上一周其实板块这边是有一些分歧的，那走得比较好的前期大科技股出现了 5.1% 的反弹，等权指数 2%，所以大多数的板块吧，上一周都出现了上涨。那芯片板块在上一周是出现了回调，在过去两周的时间里吧，芯片板块差不多出现了 15% 左右这个回调，我认为是非常健康的一个回调。

技术面：纳指高位横盘走收缩三角形

从技术面的走势，我们来看一下纳指现在的一个走势。其实在过去一个多月的时间里面，纳指是出现在高位横盘，它是在走一个收缩三角形。理论上来说其实是一个比较（多）的信号，起码在一个收缩三角形的下区间，我们应该是在那个位置选择做多。

那么当然我们也不能无脑地去相信嘛。在高位如果出现收缩三角形的走势，接下去就一定再次会出现一波上涨？比如说上年的 11 月份，11 月份就出现了这样的一个走势，那当时这边就是出现了一个收缩三角形，但是接下去的 26 年的起码 1 月份、2 月份、3 月份，纳指是出现了回调。

那现在和当时最大的一个差别就是：估值在不同的位置。我一直和大家分享，在我自己的体系里边，估值在什么样的位置比技术面更加的重要。那即使技术面告诉我接下去比如收缩三角形有可能会出现上涨，但是如果估值在一个高位的话，该减仓仍然应该去减仓，那么该降 Beta 仍然应该去降 Beta。

但是现在技术面出现了一个高位横盘，出现了一个收缩三角形，且估值在一个相对比较低的位置，那对我来说我就会看多市场。

估值：三大指数处于中性偏低

那我们来看一下现在估值这个位置。这是我在上半年做总结的时候，当然我把这边的数据做了一个更新，这边数据是上周四的结果：

  • 标普：现在的估值在 22 倍，20.2 倍，我个人认为是中性偏低的位置。今年的 4 月份是在 19 倍，上年的 10 月份最高点的时候是在 23.2 倍，就是离现在这个位置起码还有 15% 的空间。
  • 纳指：22.8 倍，中性偏低的位置，和标普一样。4 月份是在 20 倍，上年的 10 月份是 23 倍，离 28 倍差不多还有 20% 的空间了，如果要去到最高点的话。
  • 芯片板块：23.8 倍，处在一个中性的位置，那也是因为在过去两周下跌了 15% 左右。那么今年的 4 月份是在 29 倍，非常高的位置；当年的 10 月份也是在 29 倍。

前段时间芯片的估值一直在 28 倍、29 倍的时候，我反复强调了嘛，我说如果去到 30 倍以上的话，你去主动止盈；如果在 29 倍的话，你可以做一个被动止盈或者移动止盈的行为。那起码过去这一段时间、过去这两周芯片板块出现的下跌就不会太过恐慌。如果仓位太高的话，做一个移动止盈，起码可以保住盈利。

所以从估值这个角度去看，我认为三大指数的标普、纳指都是在中性偏低的，然后芯片随着过去这两周的回调，现在也来到了一个中性的位置。那在现在这个位置，就没有必要对于大盘过于悲观了，所以从估值角度去看的话，都还好，都没有很贵。

二、Meta 相关的两条新闻：AI 落地格局正在明确化

在看下半年的展望之前，先来看上周两条和 Meta 相关的消息。其实我在我的展望里边有一点我讲到的是"AI Agent 落地相对延后了"。那在周四的新闻里面，正好 Meta 内部有聊到——小扎也认为 AI Agent 的落地没有他们期待的要快。

新闻一：Meta 计划开展云计算业务，出售 AI 算力

Meta 的第一条新闻，这条应该是在周三公布的吧，那那天发布完之后 Meta 是出现了反弹嘛。Meta 计划开展云计算业务，以销售人工智能计算能力。这条新闻本身我个人认为是利好两家公司。

首先我现在并不知道 Meta 到底是把算力卖给谁，但是我的一个猜测非常有可能是卖给 Anthropic。

从公司投资 ROI 的角度分析：

对于 Meta 来说，我下面举个例子。从一家公司的角度来说——我们个人投资的时候，我们会讲一个成本、讲回报，那从公司角度也是一样的，每一笔投资都是讲成本讲回报。

从 Meta 这个角度，他们的投资是什么？投资是投资算力，买的算力，前期花的资本开支。那他们的回报是：未来现在所花的算力，要不就是为自己的现有业务去赋能，要不就是开创一些新的业务，通过卖 API 去赚钱。

比如说像 Anthropic、像 OpenAI，但是如果想要和两家大模型去竞争的话，那这个投资的回报周期可能会比较的长，但是这个回报可能会比较的高——那么可能是前期 3 倍甚至于 4 倍的回报。

Anthropic 的高毛利结构如何改变游戏规则：

但是现在有一家公司 Anthropic，他通过自己去卖 API 的话，毛利润已经达到了 70%。也就是我这边给了一个算术，就差不多背后的一些数据：

  • 如果说 Anthropic 的毛利润能够达到 70%，那就是它的成本只有 30%。这个成本是交给谁的？成本是交给这些大云，就交给谁？Hyperscaler。
  • Hyperscaler 的利润是差不多 30% 到 40%，我假设它 40%。
  • 那也就是说，Anthropic 卖出 100 刀的 API，他自己就赚了 70 块钱。这些云的一些大厂，他们营收是 30 块钱，成本 18 块钱，云赚了 12 块钱。

现在 Anthropic 说我需要更多的算力。即使说原本你卖给我 30 块钱的算力（对于他们来说是成本），现在即使你卖给我 40 块钱，我都可以接受。为什么？因为即使你卖给我 40 块钱，我还是卖 100 块钱卖出去的话，那我的毛利润还是在 60%，对于他们来说还是比较高。并且如果需求非常的高，他们还可以在 100 刀的情况下可以继续涨价，那涨完价之后，他们有可能还是可以把毛利润保持在 65% 至 70% 左右。

那从云厂这个角度去看，以前的投资成本是不变的，那现在的投资成本——就现在供给给 Anthropic 所提供的这些算力，都是以前的这些投入，所以以前的投入成本是不变的，然后成本还是 18 块钱，但现在从原来的 30 块钱可以卖到 40 块钱。也就是莫名其妙 Operating Margin 一下子从 40%、50% 翻成了 100% 多。

为什么 Meta 愿意"卖算力"：

所以作为 XAI 或者是像 Meta 这类的公司来说，无论是 XAI 也好，还是 Meta 也好，他们原本都是要搞自己的大模型。但是现在 Anthropic 对于底层算力的需求非常非常的大，那使得短期的租赁价格出现了上涨。

那这样对于 XAI 或者 Meta 这一类的公司来说，就有了短期去变现的空间。与其等一段时间等这个相对比较长的回报周期去获取 3 倍或者 4 倍的回报，不如把我原来前期投入的算力直接卖给 Anthropic，这个变现能力现在非常的强。

再加上过去两年，市场其实就在通过股价的打压去惩罚 Meta 相对比较高的资本开支，且并没有看到一些水花出来。所以从这个角度，Meta 先去变现一部分的算力，把这个算力直接去卖给 Anthropic——因为 Anthropic 会拿一个非常大的议价去购买这个算力，所以对于 Meta 来说、对于 Anthropic 来说，它都是一个双赢的局面。

而这件事情本身它并不是"AI 有泡沫"，只是 Anthropic 在 AI 这一块的落地非常的强。这背后的一个逻辑是：AI 的变现能力的差异，决定了底层的算力到底往哪里去走。

其实我们把所有的公司 AI 的落地全部放在一起，我们试想一下——因为算力本身，就现在市场能够提供的算力，所有公司（Hyperscaler、Neo Cloud，包括 Meta 自己的算力）全部加在一起之后，我们会发现整个市场现在算力还是非常的紧缺。然后在落地这一块，像 Anthropic 这类的公司，它的落地落得非常的好，所以它的 Margin 非常的高。它 Margin 70% 就恰恰证明了落地非常的好。

所以从 Anthropic 这个角度去看，它就可以以一个议价的成本，向大云、向 Meta 这一类去购买算力。其实我们也可以理解为：整个市场的 AI 落地的格局正在进行整合和明确化。这是第一条新闻。

新闻二：小扎承认 AI Agent 进展不及预期

第二条新闻是小扎在一次内部会议上表示：公司人工智能代理系统的进展速度并未达到预期。另外，小扎表示重组过程不够顺利，高管们对变革的时机把握有误。这句话翻译我来说——未来有可能 Meta 还会继续进行裁员。

两条新闻的三点总结

把这两条消息我们放在一起，我觉得可以做三个总结：

第一，AI（特别是 Anthropic）的落地仍然非常的好。 所以他们 Margin 可以做到 70%，所以他们可以向其他公司去购买算力。而这两家公司原本是想着要和这些大模型去竞争的（一家是 XAI，一家是 Meta）。所以 AI 落地这一块的赢家已经开始慢慢走出来、慢慢浮出水面了，逼着其他两家公司开始去卖自己的算力。那卖自己的算力也不见得是一件坏事，起码对于前期这一块的投入马上就有回报，只是在模型这一块，肯定并不是想着和两家前沿的大模型去进行竞争了。你如果真的要去竞争的话，是不可能出售自己的算力。

第二，Token 价格贵反而拖慢 AI Agent 落地。 因为 AI 的落地非常的强，Anthropic 卖出来的 Token 非常的贵，变相也导致了整个市场现在的 Token 是不够、算力是不够的，那也使得现在 Token 的价格非常的贵。Token 的价格非常的贵，其实反过来也促使着 AI 智能代理系统的进展缓慢——你 AI Agent 的落地必须要 Token 的价格下来。

第三，Meta 可能成为第一家资本开支增速放缓的大科技。 这边我要稍微解释一下：资本开支的增速放缓和资本开支出现负增长是完全两个概念。 - 增速放缓：原本上一年你是 50%，今年可能 30%、20% 这个增速，仍然花更多的资本开支，只是增速和上年去做对比的话出现了放缓。 - 负增长：上年你花了 100 块钱，今年你花 90 块钱，那这个增速就是负的 10%。负增长就代表整个市场要进入熊市了。

而增速放缓，我认为整个市场只是稍微停一停，从公司这个角度去思考一下 ROI。另外从数学这个角度去看，增速放缓是必然会发生的结果。你不可能每一年对吧——像现在亚马逊和谷歌和上年同期做对比的话是翻倍了，26 年比 25 年是翻倍了，你说 27 年比 26 年还可能翻倍吗？不可能翻倍，他们没有那么多钱去做资本开支。所以增速放缓也是一个必然的过程。

三、下半年展望：AI FOMO 走到关键拐点

所以我在上周三做下半年总结的时候，我说近期很多的噪音里面有一些信号我们是必须要注意的。

拐点一：科技公司 CapEx 可能的转向

一个就是科技公司 AI FOMO 开始走到了拐点。我是在周三做的那期视频。拐点主要从两个角度去出发：

（1）CapEx 可能的转向 —— Meta 开始卖算力

（2）基建通胀成为 Wake-up Call

其主要的一个时间点就是基建通胀，使得苹果开始寻求中国的供应商——那天苹果出现涨价，那天苹果下跌了 6%。然后老马也在推上、也在 X 上说"我从来没有见到过那么大幅度的通胀"。

我相信这种新闻本身对于科技公司来说，其实是一个 Wake-up Call：就是你们自己在 FOMO 资本开支，使得背后的这些硬件公司吃得饱、吃得非常的饱，而最后这些激进的通胀是你们自己必须要去承受的。

所以这是第一点：CapEx 可能的转向。Meta 是有可能就市场现在也在聊了——你 Meta 是有可能成为第一家大科技里边资本开支放缓的一家公司。

（3）情绪面无法持续

我们知道，无论是个人在做投资，还是科技公司在做资本开支投资的时候，情绪面本身是没办法持续的。所以你一开始非常的 FOMO，情绪面非常的高，那最终一定这个情绪面会放缓下来。

拐点二：Coding 花费的节制

另外一个科技公司 AI FOMO 的拐点就是在 Coding 这一块花费上的节制。原本一开始是 Token Maxing，基本上科技公司管理层鼓励下面的员工能烧多少就烧多少。但现在已经从 FOMO 到了 ROI，注重投资和回报。

一个转折点或者那条新闻，就是 Coinbase CEO 说："我们的花费保持持平，但是我们烧的 Token 更多了。为什么？是因为我们使用了中国的开源模型。"

Anthropic 这个毛利润，也正是因为他们的 FOMO，使得 Anthropic 的毛利润可以达到 70% 至 80%。再加上一两周之前，中国 GLM 这个模型的面世，使得很多的科技公司现在也在考虑中国的这些开源模型，特别是在 Coding 这一块的能力。

中国开源模型的影响：短期利空，长期利好

那中国开源模型的进程，我个人认为对于大科技、对于这些 Hyperscaler 大云来说是一个利好。当然，有很多人认为，如果中国的开源模型进来，可能会伤害到两家大模型——伤害到 Anthropic 和伤害到 OpenAI 他们的毛利润、他们的盈利和营收。伤害到他们的话，可能会伤害到大科技，因为这两家大模型基本上和很多科技公司都已经绑定了，业务上面都已经绑定了。

那从新闻这个角度去看，我认为是会存在一定程度的利空，因为如果这两家大模型的运营模式、他们的落地受到影响，或者他们的利润受到影响的话，那可能会伤害到所有和他们相关的一些公司——基本上所有的公司都和他们相关。

但是你从一个底层逻辑去看，开源模型进来之后：一旦把 Coding 的成本给打压下来的话，你只会使得科技公司烧更多的 Token。Coinbase 是一个非常好的例子。而烧更多的 Token 对于这些基建公司、对于这些大云、对于 Hyperscaler 来说是一个利好。

并且就像我前面说的，你只有把整体市场 API 的价格给压下来之后，才有可能会看到下一步 AI 的落地——就是 AI 智能体 AI Agent。你会发现过去大半年左右吧，几乎已经没有人在聊 AI Agent 对吗？为什么？是因为在 Coding 这一块的 Token 的价格，大家都是 FOMO。所以两家大模型他们一定把算力全部放到 Coding 这一块，那么使得我们平时的正常用户我们的体验也下降。连我们正常的用户体验都下降的话，更不用去谈 AI Agent，AI Agent 就更不可能落地了。

所以这两个信号我认为是在很多噪音里面我们必须要注意到的，就是接下去会出现的拐点，一些拐点的事件已经在了。

市场情绪面：散户 FOMO，硬件板块高波动

然后另外一点也是我们需要注意的是：市场现在都在风靡硬件。就几乎每一天你在社交媒体上都在找市场，就里面很多的参与者和 KOL 都在找 Alpha。好像你每天的账户不涨个 10%、不涨个 15%，你就是找错了，你就没有找到这个 Alpha。整个市场的情绪面非常的高，散户非常的 FOMO，每天都在找 Alpha，然后每天的波动增加。

我自己持仓里面非常明确、感受非常深的就是英特尔——英特尔一天可以涨 9%，然后第二天可以跌 8%。那么大体量的公司每一天的波动那么大，这说明什么？这说明背后一些散户的交易者、机构的交易者，他们的情绪非常的不稳定。而当市场里边每日的波动增加的时候，这些都是市场情绪面在高位的特征。

所以就像我前面说的，在硬件这一块，还是做好一个移动止盈，这样的话即使它出现了下跌或者下跌的幅度非常大，你不会害怕以前的回报被市场给吃了。

四、下半年展望总结与资产配置

那最后就是把所有东西全部总结在一起，我对于下半年的展望：

期待 7%~10% 的回调

我仍然期待下半年那指会有一个 7% 至 10% 的回调。那这一次的回调，我期待是由硬件板块所带领。然后我会平衡一些自己的仓位。

加仓医疗板块

比如说我和大家讲，我想多加一些医疗板块。其实我在上周也加了 ISRG 这一档标的。为什么会加，这背后的基本面我还没有时间去做一期视频和大家讲解，我会做一期会员视频和大家讲一下，就主要是对于 ISRG 这家公司去做一个基本面的分析。但是仓位本身已经开了。医疗板块的话，我还希望加更多的一些仓位，去平衡一下仓位。然后英特尔这边我也不会做——暂时来说我也不会去做减仓。

大科技：市场短视，故事线终将回归软件

大科技这一块，上一年其实市场就开始担心 AI 这一块的落地，而其实大科技的财报今年起码第一季度财报都已经证明了，第二季度财报也一定会非常的好。特别大影响价格上涨，亚马逊刚刚就上涨了 20%——涨价涨了 20%。那就是第三季度的在云这一块的 Model 还会非常的好。所以财报已经证明了，但是市场仍然对于这些大科技不喜欢，那么市场仍然去反馈那些收支票的——大科技是给支票的，那些收支票的就是硬件公司。所以市场是非常的短视。

因为想一下，如果说特别我们现在还在讨论这些硬件公司，像美光啊，它的 Re-rating——那么它的估值有没有可能去到 10 倍、15 倍、20 倍？这个前提一定是建立在它的甲方这些大科技他们 AI 有落地。所以市场不可能一边在担心大科技的 AI 没有落地、大科技的 AI 这边没有 ROI，同时又去定价硬件这一块，美光它的 PE 能够到 15 倍、能够到 20 倍。这是不现实的——大科技的 AI 如果没有落地，大科技以前的投入如果没有 ROI、没有回报的话，它不可能保持他们的资本开支；大科技不保持他们的资本开支，美光怎么可能走出原来这种周期板块中周期板块的这种特性？不可能。就是这两个逻辑性是矛盾的。

所以我就说现在市场就非常的短视。市场只能够看到，因为这些硬件板块就短期一年、半年到一年的盈利非常的确定，而且现在收钱收到手麻了都。所以市场非常对于这些硬件公司就非常的偏好，但同时不喜欢这些大科技，因为他们认为对于他们的 AI 落地有一定的担心，但这两点之间这个逻辑线是完全是矛盾的。

然后同时软件板块现在又非常的便宜。然后英特尔这边我现在也不做减仓。

牛市未结束，市场故事线将转向软件

接下去，包括我前面说，大仓位这块的话，做好一些移动止盈。我认为牛市仍然是存在，牛市并没有结束，或者我们并没有进入一个熊市。还是会以 AI 为主。

接下去还是讲 ROI。大科技的财报还是必须要修外——你们看大科技的财报，营收特别是 Margin 这一块我认为要加速，EPS 这个增速我认为也要加速，因为前期这个投入必须要体现在到现在的营收和盈利里边。ROI、投资回报、EPS 增速，现金流为王。

我估计今年的下半年，包括到明年的上半年，市场的故事线应该会慢慢的回到软件这块。市场会慢慢的发现，似乎这两家大模型公司对于不是对于所有的软件公司都能够造成破坏，且这些软件公司的营收、盈利、基本面几乎没有造成任何的影响，甚至有些软件公司 EPS 还在加速。而与此同时，这些软件公司他们的现金流又非常的好，估值又非常的低，然后不断的再回购。

那么如果担心原本资本开支会没有 ROI 的大科技——你担心资本开支没有 ROI，那软件公司呢？他们没有任何的资本开支，又没有任何的债务，那现金流又非常的好。所以软件公司我认为市场故事线一定会转。

宏观噪音：凯文·沃什与 IPO 延后

最后是宏观的噪音。那宏观的噪音主要是来自于凯文·沃什。我上一周听了凯文·沃什在 ECP 上一次会议，我不改变我对于凯文·沃什这个看法。听完之后，我认为凯文·沃什仍然会照着市场给的加息的预期去走。如果他不去这么做的话，那他对于市场来说他将会失信。

那 IPO 的风险可能会降低一些。Anthropic 也好、OpenAI 也好，最近有些新闻——他们 IPO 可能要延到 2027 年。

那最后一点，第四点：AI Agent 的落地比想象的要慢很多，那么和 AI Agent 相关的这些标的可能会受到一些影响。这一点我是在上周三做的总结嘛，然后上周四、上周五我们听到 Meta——听到小扎也在内部会议里面也聊到了 AI Agent 这一块的落地比他们的想象要慢。

资产配置：黄金与比特币

另外，从资产配置的角度去看，最后两点： - 黄金：我认为长期是可布局的。 - 比特币：进入了一个加仓区域。

五、结语

所以最后做一个总结：

我期待下半年仍然会能够看到 7% 至 10% 的回调。其实很正常，一年里面有两次回调也很正常，总不能期待我们从 7 月份开始到今年的 12 月份市场不会出现任何的回调吧？这是完全不符合逻辑的，几乎不可能发生的事情。一定会出现回调。所以出现一次 7% 到 10% 的回调概率非常的高，那时候出现回调的话又将会是一个非常好的买点。

我自己的话，仓位会去做一些平衡： - 比如说去加一些医疗板块的仓位； - 同时在硬件这一块，比如说我超过 20% 仍然是我第一大的持仓，英特尔，我也不会去做任何的减仓——减仓在前段时间已经做过了； - 今年的下半年，我期待牛市仍然存在。

会有一些宏观的噪音。市场的故事线可能会转换，可能会看到硬件板块（一些市场短期比较确定的板块）可能会出现一些回调，然后资金可能会轮换到大科技这边，可能会轮换到现金流比较好的这些软件板块身上。

六、活动推广：Seeking Alpha 75 折

最后今天的活动是 Seeking Alpha 他们 Alpha Pick 这一次全网 75 折的活动。这个活动终于要结束了，在周一就结束了。所以大家如果感兴趣的话，千万要抓住最后一天的机会。

  • 他们的量化选股策略：标普 500 YTD 9% 不到的回报，那么他们的策略本身 42%。基本上是每两周会推一个标的，然后每一个标的都会做更新的减仓、加仓或者继续持有。在过去推的 7 个标的里面，5 个标的都跑赢了标普，还是非常的不错。这一个量化的选股策略原本是 499 美金一年，现在 75 折就直接便宜了 124 美金。
  • Alpha Premium 订阅：里边有很多关于投资的文章。那一个 Premium 的订阅有 7 天的免费试用，所以 7 天可以免费用，不喜欢随时可以取消。
  • 如果对于这两个订阅都感兴趣的话，还有一个 Combo 的价格。

所以我会把三个链接都放在置顶的留言里边，大家感兴趣的话，欢迎去订阅。

好嘛，那今天这期内容到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：Meta"卖算力"其实是投降的仪式，不是新业务
大多数人把 Meta 计划出售 AI 算力当作"新业务线"来读——但如果把它放在博弈论的坐标里看，这更像是**大模型军备竞赛的正式退出仪式**。理由极其反常识：**任何一家真心要造前沿模型的公司，都不会在算力短缺的窗口期把稀缺资源卖给对手**。Anthropic 出价再高，你自己训下一代模型的机会成本一定更高。所以小扎"愿意卖"的动作本身，就是在向市场传递一个他嘴上不会承认的信号——Llama 不再瞄准前沿。这也是为什么这条新闻和"小扎承认 AI Agent 落地不及预期"几乎同周放出：**一个是对外的退场，一个是对内的重构**。视频作者点到了 CapEx 拐点，但没点破的是——**拐点的本质不是花钱变少，而是"造模"派系在 Meta 内部输给了"变现"派系**。
## 跨领域连接：这是"云计算 2010"剧本的复刻，只是主角换成了推理算力
作者反复讲 Anthropic 70% 毛利、Hyperscaler 只吃 12 美元云费——这幅图景与 2010 年前后 **Netflix 建立在 AWS 之上的那段

item.title: [硅谷101播客] E242｜最快半年AI跑通自进化？与陈天桥首席科学家聊聊硅谷模型必争之地
item.dc:creator: 硅谷101播客
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的暗线：这不是一集关于AI的节目，而是一集关于"品味"的节目

表面上这是关于RSI（递归自我提升）的技术讨论，但如果把两位研究员反复回到的那个词圈出来——"品味"——会发现一个惊人的事实：AI 训练的终局竞争，不是算力、不是数据、不是算法，而是"审美学的稀缺性"。

Beibin 说他"想来想去"最后剩下的只有品味两个字；Simon 说顶级科学家不看论文数量而看最高那一篇的高度；陈天桥亲自参与撰写"模型宪法"——这些看似分散的细节，其实揭示了一个反常识的产业结构：当代码能力饱和之后，AI 公司的护城河从"工程壁垒"迁移到了"审美壁垒"。而审美是不可 scale 的，是只能靠极少数人"热启动"的。这意味着未来最贵的资产可能不是 GPU，而是愿意长期陪 AI 聊天、给它打分、纠正它品味的顶级人类。这也是为什么陈天桥要"亲自和顶级科学家对齐"——他在做的不是投资，是给未来 AI 注入"味觉基因"。

二、一个反直觉的观察：AI 越强，"错误的传染性"越可怕——它像金融系统中的系统性风险

节目里提到一个术语"递归漂移"（recursive drift），Beibin 说他睡不着觉的是"我们怎么知道模型没有跑偏"。这句话如果单独听，只是一个技术问题。但如果把它和 2008 年金融危机 并置来看，你会发现一个几乎一模一样的结构：

  • 银行用同一批模型评估风险 → 单个模型的偏差被系统性放大 → 整个系统崩盘
  • AI 用自己生成的数据训练自己 → 单次推理的微小偏差被递归放大 → 模型"世界观"整体漂移

RSI 本质上是一种"AI 内部的杠杆"。杠杆能让你更快地涨，也能让你更快地爆。金融监管发明了 Basel、压力测试、逆周期缓冲；而 AI 行业目前对"漂移"的应对，还停留在 Beibin "每天工作时间盯着模型看" 的原始阶段——用他自己的话说，是"一个笨方法"。

更让人不安的是：当漂移率从 10% 降到 0.1%，人类反而更危险。因为人类会因为它看起来"足够好"而停止监督，就像 2007 年的 AAA 评级让所有人放心一样。风险从来不是发生在坏的时候，而是发生在"看起来很好"的时候。

三、跨领域连接：这一集其实是"AlphaGo 时刻"在科研领域的重演，但有一个关键差异

节目里 Beibin 主动提到了 AlphaGo → AlphaZero 的迁移路径，这是全场最重要的类比。但他没有说透一个隐藏结论：

围棋有确定的胜负规则，所以 AlphaZero 可以"抛弃人类品味"独立进化。但科学没有胜负规则——一个假设是"重要"还是"灌水"、一个方向是"根本性"还是"增量式"，这些判断本身就没有客观真值。所以"科学版 AlphaZero"永远不可能完全脱离人类的价值判断。

这带来一个令人惊讶的推论：AI for Science 不会像 AlphaGo 那样有一个明确的"超越人类时刻"，而是会长期停留在一个"人类品味 + AI 执行力"的共生体中。真正的转折点不是 AI 变得多聪明，而是人类意识到自己无法再判断 AI 是对还是错的那一刻——那才是真正的失控点，而不是模型能力超过人类的那一刻。

再往深一层：播客中 Beibin 的"员工蒸馏"这个玩笑，其实是本世纪最深刻的哲学命题之一的技术版本——一个人的"品味"、"直觉"、"审美"，是否可以被压缩成一组参数？如果可以，那"我"是什么？如果不可以，那 AI 永远造不出乔布斯。这个问题，实验室里三个月内可能就要出答案了。这可能是继"图灵测试"之后，人类文明面对的第二次身份危机——不是"机器能不能思考"，而是"我这个人能不能被复制"。

📝 转录文本

开场：Anthropic 的警告与 Apodex 的登场

哈喽 大家好 欢迎收听《硅谷101》 我是泓君。

最近 Anthropic 它承认了一件事——他们现在 80% 的代码已经不是人写的，是 Claude 自己写的。这家全世界最谨慎的 AI 公司，同时发出了一个警告：AI 可能很快就要自己设计、自己去训练下一代的 AI 了。他们管这个叫做递归自我提升，英文是 RSI，也是硅谷今年对模型能力最热的讨论方向之一。

今天这集节目我们的含金量高了。我们请到了两位真正正在做这件事情的研究员，他们所在的公司叫做 Apodex，背后是陈天桥出资创立并亲自主导的。这家公司给自己的定位也非常特别——他们不做图片生成、不做视频生成，只做一件事，他们自己叫做 Heavy Duty Solver，意思是专啃那些没有标准答案、人类自己都不知道从哪下手的难题。

他们管自己想做的模型叫做 Discovery model（发现模型），不是从已知的信息里面找答案，而是要提出人类还没有想到过的假设，然后自己想办法去验证它、去解决这个世界上还没有被解决的科学难题。这也是为什么这家公司叫 Apodex，因为词源就是希腊语里的"证明和论证"。

如果 AI 真的可以教自己，人类科学家还剩下什么？让我睡不着觉的一个问题就是：我们怎么知道这个模型在自我进化的时候，它没有跑偏？

同时 Beibin 也在这集节目里面讲到了他自己蒸馏自己的想法。这个在过去职业生涯里曾经每天都在跟马斯克、跟陈天桥讨论技术问题的研究员，我采访的时候有那么一瞬间觉得，他下一刻就工作不保了。于是我问他：我们假设真的把 Beibin 蒸馏了，你会担心自己失业吗？

"说不担心肯定是假的，但即使我担心，我其实还是乐观的。"

那这集我们就站在 AI 自进化的分界点上，与两位一线的研究员聊一聊硅谷模型的核心发展趋势，以及未来模型能不能超越人类顶尖科学家的水平，真正地去提出问题、去发现问题。下面就请收听我们今天的节目。

嘉宾介绍

今天跟我在一起的两位嘉宾，一位是 Simon 杜少雷。

Simon：你好。

Simon 是华盛顿大学计算机系的副教授，同时也是 Apodex 的首席科学家，负责训练与推理方向。还有一位嘉宾是 Apodex 的首席科学家，负责代码与自进化方向的李辈滨。

Beibin：哈喽 你好。

欢迎两位。

一、自进化：不是新概念，而是能力的到达

我们刚刚提到了，今年有一个词很火，就是 RSI。我发现其实这个大模型，它的预训练、后训练，包括它的核心的训练方向，它可能是每年都有一些新的方向出来。我记得前几年我们在聊的最火的是 DeepResearch（深度研究），再到 Coding（代码方向），今年就到了自进化 Self-evolve 的方向。

首先想请 Beibin 跟 Simon 大家一起来分析一下，这后面其实是几个不同的方向，还是说它有一个核心的主线在主导这个模型往哪个方向走的？

Simon：我感觉从训练的角度来看，其实是同一个能力在提高。其实都是推理的能力、长链推理的能力，还有就是在环境中进行一些探索，执行一些动作，获得了这个信号之后再进行下一步的探索。不管是在 DeepResearch 还是 Coding 这些环境下，其实它的内核都是一样的，只是它的方向有一些细微的区别。

Beibin：我们说的自我进化这个概念，其实并不是一个新的概念了。我就不说远的，在八九十年代关于 AI 和 machine learning（机器学习）的自我进化，我就光说 Language model（语言模型）的自我进化，其实往前推的话也可以推到大概三年前左右。

那个时候 Google 就有一篇论文叫做"LLM as Optimizer"，就是说用大语言模型当作一个优化器，可以优化不同的东西。当时我在 AutoGen 那边也有和同事出一个类似的概念叫做 Agent optimizer（智能体优化器），就是说智能助手可以自己进化、自己优化自己。

只不过这两年大家发现模型的能力越来越强了，它可以自我优化的东西越来越多了。就比如说 Anthropic 有八成的代码是可以自己写的，训练模型不管是从哪个角度来说——从合成数据的生成、自然语言数据的清理，还是自我训练学习的代码、算法，以及 infra 底层代码——其实它都是一种代码的形式。一旦模型的写代码能力变强了，用它来进行自我提升、自己训练自己，其实是非常自然的一件事情。

所以我并不觉得突然今年大家发现我们要自我学习、自我进化、让模型自己训练自己。其实早在两三年前，大家已经开始慢慢地做这件事情了。

二、Beibin 的背景：从 AutoGen 到 xAI

Beibin，你之前的背景是一直在关注模型的自我进化这条线是吗？你要不要简单介绍一下自己的背景？

Beibin：对。我最开始是在微软研究院那边做多智能体的合作。当然那个时候我们不叫做自我进化，因为那个时候智能体都是一个比较新的概念，所以大家一般对外说都是说智能体。但是实际上我同事的一些工作就已经开始进行一些自我进化的，当时我们叫做 agent optimizer 和 teachable agent——智能体可以教智能体，下一次再做类似的事情的时候，可以用哪一些技能。

和现在的我们说的 Skills 这个概念有一点像，但那个时候大家用的概念还是比如说 context management 或者是 prompt engineering，就是上下文管理以及 prompt engineering，就是工程学怎么样修改 prompt。

后来我去了 Meta 和 xAI。在 xAI 的时候，我们就已经开始关注 Agentic RL，就是通过强化学习来加强智能体的一些能力，比如说用工具的能力、写代码的能力。当时还有个概念叫做 MCP Tools，当然现在也非常火，用 MCP 工具的这些能力。

所以我们其实在早期，Simon 和我大概一年前左右就发现了，其实模型提高自己是非常可行的一件事情。包括我们当时做的一些强化学习的数据生成，其实有很大一部分是代码写的。

到今年年初的时候，Andrej Karpathy 发表了关于 AutoResearch 就是自己进行科研的一个项目，突然一下这个概念就爆火了。

三、RSI：递归自我提升的关键变化

对，我觉得非常好的自我进化的总结。今年说到模型的自我进化，跟往年来说模型的自我进化，有什么不一样的地方吗？我觉得今年大家再提到 RSI 的时候，他说的是一种递归自我提升。Beibin 你要不要跟大家解释一下这个概念，以及比如说我们说这个概念的时候，是不是说以前我们在让 AI 进化的时候，考官还是人，现在考官逐渐变成 AI 了？它是不是在解决更难的问题？

Beibin：对的，这是一个非常好的问题。其实自我进化，如果大家想象成是一个圆环的话——比如说模型自己找问题、自己出题、自己解答、自己训练自己——那 RSI 里面的 R 就代表是 Recursive 递归式，就有点像垂直的递归式慢慢慢慢向上增长。

这一点确实是跟以往的一些自进化有一点点不一样。当然这一个想法也不是新的。今年跟往年的最大的区别就是在于：模型的能力变强了。

比如说 Anthropic 发表的论文或者是说发表的博客有提到，80% 的代码是它们写的。他们可以做人类大概一天左右时间做的事情。这个能力上来了之后，因为它是可以完成非常长程的任务，所以在一个长程的任务里面，这个模型可以提高自己好几遍。

不像过往的，比如我们说 LLM as Optimizer 或者是 agent optimizer 的时候，LLM 只会一次可能优化自己大概两三个小时。因为你优化太久了，它可能因为自己的偏差或者自己的能力不足就很难继续往下迭代。因为它可能会犯一个小错误，那个小错误会自我积累，越积累越大，到之后就没有办法进行自我的迭代。

而今年为什么这个 recursive、这个 R 出来了？因为大家发现这个模型可以进行很长程的任务，所以它可以让这个模型自己 recursive、多提高几遍。

四、长程任务：比摩尔定律更快的翻倍

对，我看这篇文章里面有一个数据，就是说 2024 年 3 月份 Claude 3 的 Opus 它可以完成人类约 4 分钟完成的任务，一年后就是 3.7 Sonnet 就可以处理需要 1.5 小时处理的任务，又过了一年这个 Claude 4.6 Opus 可以处理长达 12 个小时的任务。整个我们看到大模型的改进速度是在翻倍的，而且这个比我们计算机领域的经典的摩尔定律，它的翻倍的速度还要快。

Simon：是的。当然这个业界各家都有不同的说法，但是好像大家比较接受的判断是这个模型能做到的事情对于人类时间来说，每 7 个月会翻一倍。

对。您刚刚提到 RSI 它可以处理长程任务，我们理解这个长程是不是可以理解成，它是一个需要很多个步骤、很多个环节来互相印证的一系列任务？假设我给一篇音频的文字稿给到模型去校正这个事实性的错误，这只算一步。但是比如说它能从开始的规划一直到最终这个播客做出来，这中间可能有十几个步骤，它能把每一个步骤都做好。长程任务是指这个意思吗？

Beibin：是的，我说的长程任务是这个意思。当然不同的人对长程的定义也有点不一样，但是大体方向大家都同意这一点，就是模型可以自己在人类不监督的情况下可以工作很长。

像您刚刚说的对于播客文件处理，比如说这个模型可以先剪切一下这个音频，自己听一遍，发现哪里剪得不对重新剪，这样可以循环几次，然后就可以进行到下一个点。这种任务可能平时对一个人类来说，可能要花 20 个小时或者 30 个小时可以做完。在这个时候，如果我们的模型可以在人类不监督的情况下把这个任务完成的话，我觉得它就完成了这个长程的任务。

五、长程任务的三大技术挑战

它是怎么做到的呢？这里就有非常多的技术细节。

首先就是在模型的架构上，可能就要进行一些创新。 比如说传统的 Self-attention（自注意力机制），其实是一个 O(n²)，就是我们在算法领域说的是一个 Polynomial time（多项式时间）的算法。所以当我们的 tokens 比较多的时候，特别是在 100 万个上下文的时候，模型首先就是在推理的时候就会花非常多的资源。这个我们有很多现成的解法，就比如说 GDN、GDNv2、DeepSeek-V4 Pro 它也有自己的一套架构。

第二就是训练数据。 我们要 100 万上下文，Inference support（推理支持）其实不是特别难，我们只要把模型训练好了，放到 SGLang 或者 vLLM 里面，给它参数都设置好，它可以支持 100 万上下文甚至更多的上下文。但是如果你的模型没有在这种非常长的数据下进行训练的话，那它可能就是我们所谓的 Out of distribution（分布外）。因为 100 万上下文是非常非常长的一个概念，就大概《哈利·波特》好几部书放在一起。而我们的代码，即使是最大的代码仓库，也很难会填满来满足这 100 万的上下文。所以在预训练和后训练的数据处理上，也是非常大的一个挑战。

第三点就是 infra（基础设施）也是一个难度。 不仅是说在测试时的 Infra，而且是在训练时候的 infra。因为你要如果让这个语言模型就直接输出 100 万个 token 的话，我们需要比如说在 GPU 上做一些什么样的优化，在 kernel（内核）上改一些什么样的代码，这些也是非常困难的事情。

所以这个就是为何训练这种长程任务相对于普通任务会是指数级的难度增长。

了解，了解。Simon 有补充的吗？

Simon：还有就是真想解决长程问题的话，可能从 agent 架构上也可以有很多可以做的。因为即使是我们有 100 万的上下文可以处理，但是真正长程的任务可能都还是不止 100 万。这个时候我们还是需要比如说在有限的上下文里处理甚至无限的上下文。那怎么做呢？还是需要一些比如说 agent 技术、记忆的技术等等，去处理这种超长程任务所涉及的超长上下文这样的问题。

六、Coding 为什么是所有模型的底座

我们听起来很简单，但是这个中间可能涉及到了很多具体细分的技术体系。之前我记得 Beibin 你有讲过，训练一个模型其实本质就是数据（Data）、基础架构（infra）和算法这三件事，其实这三件事都高度地依赖写代码。所以我们今天看到像 Claude 它能表现出这么强的基础模型的研发能力，主要是因为它 coding 能力的提升？自进化的能力它也是跟 coding 的能力是紧密相关的？我可以这样理解吗？

Beibin：对，我基本上同意这个看法。

对，所以 coding 它会变成所有模型的一个标配的底座？

Beibin：我觉得它会变成大部分模型的标配底座，但并不是所有模型的。比如说聊天型的模型可能不太需要写代码的能力。当然了，聊天型的小模型可能也是通过一个大模型进行蒸馏的，所以它后面这个大模型需要非常大的比如说 reasoning 推理能力，需要自我进化。所以能自我进化来蒸馏到小模型里面，所以它背后的这些大模型肯定还是需要高度优化写代码的能力的。

我可不可以理解成单纯的聊天还好，因为现在我们其实都在讲怎么让 agent 完成一系列的事情，如果一旦涉及到了多步骤，让 agent 去完成不同的复杂的任务的时候，Coding 能力就会变得特别关键了？

Beibin：是的，没错。因为 coding 其实——写代码不仅是写代码本身，而且可以通过写代码训练自己。另外它在 coding 里面要进行的归纳、演绎，这些逻辑推理的基本的思想和算法，其实也可以帮助模型在其他的一些方向上进行优化和 generalize（泛化），就是可以泛化到其他不同的领域里。

七、递归漂移：让人睡不着觉的根本难题

我发现在学术界，其实自我进化它还有一个很根本的障碍，我看大家起了一个学术的名词叫做递归漂移。它的意思就是说，模型在自己去生成它的训练数据的时候，它推理的错误会在一次的错误中一代一代去累积。就比如说有的时候我们看它给我们的一个答案，这个答案的结果可能是对的，但是它的推理过程可能是错的，在这种让它自我进化的过程中，它就会把这个错误一代一代累积，最后可能进化的结果就越来越歪。这是两位在真正的训练模型中遇到的一个比较根本性的难题吗？

Beibin：我非常同意这个看法。这个现象其实也是我们大家一直有注意到的。Apodex 有一个不一样的地方，就是我们比较注重 verification 就是自我验证。

模型自己在训练自己的时候，如果它的验证有任何的漂移或者是 bias（偏差）的话，会对结果造成不好的影响，因为它可以累积起来。所以我们在做比如说写代码和做数学计算的这些任务的时候，我们比较容易验证，可以通过 rule-based（基于规则），就是可以通过跑一套测试代码来确定它有没有对。所以在代码领域，相对来说更好解决 Recursive drift（递归漂移）的问题。

当然了，如果对代码领域有了解的一些专家可能还会发现，即使是有 test script（测试脚本）、即使有 test code（测试代码），它其实偶尔还是会发生漂移。比如说 test case（测试用例）不管是人还是 AI，在写测试代码的时候可能偶尔会太宽、偶尔会太窄。太宽的时候可能会让错误的解题代码通过，但如果太紧的话有时候会惩罚写得对的代码。所以这种情况下也是还会发生细微的递归漂移，但是它会简单很多。

八、Apodex 的验证体系：Agent Team 与冗余

我其实很好奇你们怎么去做验证。因为我知道 Apodex 验证其实是你们主打的一个核心功能。因为我去试用了一下你们的产品，之后我是有收到一封邮件，这个邮件的标题非常有意思，它叫做"很多 AI 系统生成答案，而 Apodex 验证它们"。后来我才知道原来你们公司的名字 Apodex 它是源自于一个希腊语，它的意思就是"证明跟论证"。所以相当于你们从成立的开始，就把验证、较真写到了名字里面。

Simon：验证这一步，我们有很多套方法，因为不同的领域确实需要不同的验证方法。

比如说代码，一般来说你可以用单元测试进行验证。当然你怎么写，有时候会过宽、有时候过窄，这是另一个很难的问题了。数学可以通过证明，证明现在有 Lean 这样的 formal（形式化）验证的方法去验证。但不得不说还是有很多的问题，其实是更多依赖于人的一种判断，甚至都并不是一种 100% 正确或不正确。

这种时候我们其实是依赖一个 Agent 系统去做我们现在 Apodex 1.0 的验证的。我们管这个叫 Agent Team（智能体团队）。大概就是说把一个问题最开始会分解，我们会有不同的子 agent，有的是去解决这个问题，我们会有另一个子 agent 单独地去验证另一个子 agent 写出来的解法。

之所以要分成两个子 agent，还是因为上下文的问题——不要让一个 agent 干所有事情，分成两个 agent 它其实效果会好很多。 这是一个细节。而且一定要拆成两个 agent，同时两个 agent 给它的指示也不太一样。

除此之外，还有一些比较重要的技巧——我们最好是有一些冗余。同一个问题你让不止一个 agent 去做，我们会得到不止一份答案。其实这个冗余的思想在计算机里面有很长的历史了。但我得到不止一个答案之后，我们会让一个全局 agent 去判断一下哪个 agent 反馈的答案更准确一些。这样会比只用一个 agent 去解一个方面的任务会效果好很多。这是从 agent 方面我们有这些设计去做一些验证。

在训练的时候我们也会专门针对验证去训练一些（能力）了。比如说我们会让 agent 去判断一下，不同信息源搜集的信息哪些是更靠谱一些，哪些不靠谱一些。比如说一个论坛上的可能就不如教材上的关于同样的问题靠谱了。

Beibin 有补充吗？

Beibin：另外我们在进行强化学习的时候，我们的裁判也会在训练的过程中一起学习。这样可以避免模型学会一些不好的行为，可以避免它进行一些奖励黑客。
