item.title: [Lex Clips] The critical role of Constantinople in the Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、君士坦丁堡不是"首都"，而是一个"地缘力学装置"

大多数人把君士坦丁堡理解为"东罗马的首都"，但 Kaldellis 描述的其实是一个物理意义上的夹钳（clamp）——它不是被选中当首都的，而是被选中来咬合两块本会分裂的大陆板块。这里藏着一个反直觉的观察：罗马帝国真正的断层线不在国境线上，而在博斯普鲁斯海峡。每次内战，帝国都会沿着这条海缝"劈开"，一位皇帝在亚洲、一位在欧洲。君士坦丁堡的天才之处在于——它被建在裂缝的正中央，用一座城市把两片欲分裂的地壳焊死。

这让我想到桥梁工程里的"预应力"概念：不是等结构裂了再加固，而是在建造时就把张力反向嵌进去。君士坦丁堡本质上是罗马地缘的预应力节点。

二、"选东不选西"背后隐藏的资本流动逻辑

Kaldellis 提到一个惊人的统计：几乎所有有选择权的皇帝，都选择留在东部，只有 Valentinian 一人例外。表面看是军事和后勤考量，但深挖下去，这是一场精英资本再配置运动。

新参议院有 2500–3000 人，他们不是被"任命"来的，而是必须投资才能获得席位——用今天的语言说，这是一次"入会即入股"的股权众筹。爱琴海、小亚细亚、叙利亚的富豪把家族资本注入这座新城市，从此他们的命运与这个节点绑定。这解释了一个更深的谜团：为什么 395 年帝国分裂时，断层没有再出现在博斯普鲁斯，而是向西平移到了亚得里亚海？

因为资本已经把裂缝焊死了。人不会背叛自己重仓的资产。君士坦丁堡的稳定性不来自城墙，而来自东部精英的资产负债表。

三、跨领域连接：君士坦丁堡与硅谷、与你的注意力

这里有一个让人意外的类比——君士坦丁堡的运作机制，和现代平台经济中的"网络效应枢纽"惊人一致：

  • 地理位置价值 → 网络位置价值：它位于黑海↔地中海、欧洲↔亚洲的双向节点，就像今天的 API 网关。
  • 强制精英"投资入场" → SaaS 的锁定成本：一旦你把数据、流程、身份绑到平台上，你就成了平台的一部分。
  • 裂缝"平移到亚得里亚"→ 竞争前线的位移：当核心枢纽足够强，冲突会被推到系统的边缘地带发生。

更有意思的一个隐喻：你自己的注意力系统也像一个罗马帝国。你有多个"前线"（工作、家庭、创作、健康），如果你把"皇帝"（意识焦点）放在其中一端，另一端就会失控叛乱。你需要找到自己的君士坦丁堡——那个位于所有战线中间、能让你快速调度的节点。对很多人来说，这个节点可能是"晨间的第一小时"，或者"某个特定的思考仪式"。

啊哈时刻：帝国的稳定不来自扩张，而来自找到那个"哪里都不太远"的中心点。这个洞察，在管理时间、经营团队、构建产品、乃至设计一段人生时，反复起作用。

📝 转录文本

关于君士坦丁堡的地理重要性

Lex： So maybe can you speak geographically the importance of Constantinople? It's an interesting place cuz it uh...

Kaldellis: Yeah. [sighs and gasps] When you're talking about a land so large as the West and the East — with enemies, with threats from all kinds of different directions — the location where the emperor sits is important.

Very. So look at Rome. Right? So it's right in the middle of Italy, right in the middle of the Mediterranean. Very safe location in a way, but it's very far from the frontiers.

军人皇帝的时代与罗马位置的不便

And this is a period when emperors need to be with the armies, right? They're not senatorial emperors who are sitting in Rome having meetings with senators. They are military emperors who are marching along the frontiers, uh, mostly with generals rather than civilian administration. So they're doing their job.

Like this is the hard work. They're slogging through the mud of winter campaigns, right? Uh, they're not just sitting in a palace enjoying themselves when they could. Um, but Rome just becomes inconvenient because of that.

君士坦丁堡：多瑙河与幼发拉底河之间的枢纽

Um, and if you look at Constantinople, it is kind of halfway between the Danube frontier and the Euphrates frontier. So if you want to prioritize the East — or treat it as an equal part of the Roman Empire, as these emperors all did — in fact, almost all emperors who had a choice about whether they would stay in the East or stay in the West chose the East. There's only one who chooses the West, and that's Valentinian later in the 4th century. All the others understand that the East is where it's at.

And Constantinople is right between the Danube and the Euphrates. These are the two major frontiers. And so it allows emperors to move between the two.

南北东西的四向节点

And also look at its location: north, south, east, west, right?

  • North–south: it is the node between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean.
  • East–west: it's precisely where Europe meets Asia.

Right? And because there's a break there — so there's a Bosphorus, the Sea of Marmara, the Hellespont — the Roman Empire during the civil wars, it would tend to break there. And you would have one emperor on one side in Asia, and you'd have another emperor on the other side. And this just kept happening.

作为"夹钳"的君士坦丁堡

And Constantinople ultimately functions as a kind of clamp that unifies this whole area. So, basically, the Balkans and Asia Minor and Syria as a kind of unit. Right?

And you know, what is a new Rome? A new Rome is, among other things, a new Senate. Right? And Constantine and his successors recruit this new Senate for the new Rome. So we're talking about maybe 2,500, 3,000 men, um, you know, at at at its peak.

And these are all recruited from all of these areas. You know, some come from Rome, but others come from the Aegean region, from Asia Minor, uh, eastern Mediterranean. So these are the wealthiest people, usually, most well-connected elites from the whole eastern Mediterranean, and they come together and form a new purpose, common purpose, in Constantinople.

断层线的西移：从博斯普鲁斯到亚得里亚

And the empire never breaks there again. Right? So it it holds together this part of the world. The breaking point now, interestingly, moves to the Adriatic. Right?

So when in 395, we talked about east and west kind of splitting, it breaks at the Adriatic, cuz Constantinople is now holding that whole eastern world together. Cuz all of these elites, they now they're invested in it. They're literally investing in it. They have to invest in it in order to be senators in Constantinople.

战略、政治与文化的多重功能

Uh, so that's kind of one of the functions that it that it performs. It's a strategic function. It's a political function. It's also a a cultural one, but, um, you know, we can talk about that, too. Uh but, that's what you see there on that map — the northwest, east, south.

观看原视频 →

## 一、君士坦丁堡不是"首都"，而是一个"地缘力学装置"
大多数人把君士坦丁堡理解为"东罗马的首都"，但 Kaldellis 描述的其实是一个**物理意义上的夹钳（clamp）**——它不是被选中当首都的，而是被选中来**咬合两块本会分裂的大陆板块**。这里藏着一个反直觉的观察：罗马帝国真正的断层线不在国境线上，而在博斯普鲁斯海峡。每次内战，帝国都会沿着这条海缝"劈开"，一位皇帝在亚洲、一位在欧洲。君士坦丁堡的天才之处在于——它**被建在裂缝的正中央**，用一座城市把两片欲分裂的地壳焊死。
这让我想到桥梁工程里的**"预应力"概念**：不是等结构裂了再加固，而是在建造时就把张力反向嵌进去。君士坦丁堡本质上是罗马地缘的预应力节点。
## 二、"选东不选西"背后隐藏的资本流动逻辑
Kaldellis 提到一个惊人的统计：**几乎所有有选择权的皇帝，都选择留在东部**，只有 Valentinian 一人例外。表面看是军事和后勤考量，但深挖下去，这是一场**精英资本再配置运动**。
新参议院有 2500–3000 人，他们不是被"任命"来的，而是**必须投资才能获得席位**——用今

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why Are LLMs bad at Writing (but Good at Code)? – Grant Sanderson (@3blue1brown )
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的"分工契约"：LLM 不是差写手，而是差作者

有个被忽略的关键区分——写作 ≠ 表达。你已经在无意中让 LLM 承担了"表达层"的工作（解释、蒸馏、翻译成人话），却仍在期待它承担"作者层"的工作（决定什么值得被表达）。这两层被人类语言习惯地混淆在一个词"writing"里，但它们其实是两个完全不同的认知任务。

令人意外的观察：Grant 的"揭示性偏好"（revealed preference）——即便面对活人专家，他也想暂停对话去问 LLM——其实是在给人类做降维处理：把专家降为"独有知识节点"，把 LLM 提升为"通用解释层"。这意味着未来知识工作的价值锚点会剧烈迁移——懂得多不再稀缺，敢于不可预测才稀缺。

二、自回归的诅咒：为什么"下一个 token"永远写不出好散文

这里藏着一个跨领域的深连接——LLM 的写作困境本质上是热力学问题，不是智力问题。

自回归生成在每一步都在做"局部最大似然"的选择，就像一个永远走下坡路的球：它能找到局部最优，但无法主动选择爬坡。而 Grant 敏锐地指出："好的写作需要在准确的那个点做不可预测的动作"——这不是提高 temperature 就能解决的，因为随机的不可预测和有意的不可预测是两回事。

跨领域连接：这与爵士乐即兴、围棋"神之一手"、乃至进化生物学的"间断平衡"（punctuated equilibrium）是同构的——它们都要求系统能识别"什么时候该打破自己的规则"。生物演化通过大灭绝完成这件事，人类作家通过多年沉淀后的直觉完成，而 LLM 目前的架构——每一步都对齐分布中心——恰恰是反演化的。它是一台"回归均值机器"，而好写作的本质是有理由地偏离均值。

三、更深的反转：也许我们低估了"探索的成本"作为文明的隐藏基础设施

这是最容易被 AI 讨论遗漏的一层——一本值得被 LLM 蒸馏的书，其存在本身依赖于作者花了数年在无回报的探索中筛选"什么值得说"。这是一种慢速的、高熵的、以生命时间为单位的搜索过程。

让人不安的洞察：如果我们把"读原著"逐渐替换为"读 LLM 摘要"，短期我们享受了效率红利，但我们正在悄悄不再资助那些让原著得以存在的探索行为。这不是危言耸听，而是一个热力学层面的可持续性问题——LLM 是二阶生成器，它消费的是人类一阶探索沉淀下来的"信息势能"。

跨领域类比：这与土壤农业惊人地相似——化肥（LLM 蒸馏）能让当季作物暴增，但会耗竭土壤中数百年积累的有机质（原创性作者的思想土壤）。真正的问题不是"LLM 能不能写作"，而是——当社会把奖赏结构从"生产内容"迁移到"蒸馏内容"时，谁还会去做那份不合算的、不可预测的、承担被误解风险的原创探索？

也许 LLM 时代最反直觉的稀缺品，不是算力，也不是数据，而是愿意承担"低概率高价值"生成路径的人类心智。

📝 转录文本

一个悖论的提出

Why is it not the case that the same progress that allows you to go from merely functional to like clean and and like emergent PR doesn't also result in um like clearer writing?

Yeah. It's a good point. I mean, also has it not?

揭示性偏好：我其实更爱让 LLM 解释

Like I agree there's many ways in which they're um terrible writers, but for a lot of writing I consume, I find it's better to just copy-paste it into uh an LLM and just say like "explain this to me." The explanation will be better than the thing that is produced uh by the human.

So, it's funny that we say like these are such terrible writers. And also my revealed preference is just like — can I just have an LLM explain it?

Even when I'm talking to a human expert like live on a call. Mhm. Um if it's a piece of knowledge they have that only they have that's not encoded in uh the distribution, I want them to explain it to me. But then if in order to understand that I need to understand a more basic concept, I would prefer if it was socially acceptable for me to just be able to say, "let's pause there. I'm just going to ask uh an LLM how that works and then we can come back to your um your your special piece of knowledge."

但是——"蒸馏"不等于"写作"

Well, it sounds — I mean, that's distillation, right? An explanation.

And so, if if you're — if I'm thinking of like quality of you as an essay writer, um if it's that I give you a book to read and I want a book report, right? Then I might believe that okay, the LLM maybe gives me a better book report.

Um but I think what we — what people are really getting at when they say it's better, right? Like what is writing? It's not just distillation of pre-existing ideas. It's not just like how do you explain clearly, cuz there are good explainers. It's like — what is the insight?

自回归的困境：不可预测性从何而来？

And and and this is this is where it gets like — just auto regression is a very weird way to generate stuff. Because um like when you're writing, you sort of — you sort of know in order for it to be good, you have to have an element of the unpredictable.

And it's it's not just like increasing temperature in your mind or something, right? It's like knowing exactly the correct point when you want to make an unpredictable move — and that that's going to be what's more insightful.

追问上游：那本书本身是谁生成的？

And so, even if it's like better at explaining a pre-existing thing, it's like — what generated that book that you wanted distilled in the first place, right? It wasn't — it wasn't an LLM that like generated it and you just needed it.

It's like some author who who through a lot of exploration of ideas in the world and then deciding what aspects of it were interesting and which ways of presenting it were like the the coherent um well-motivated narrative. It's like they put that all together in some way.

And you know, if they're a good author, it's probably one that actually you would uh err on the side of reading their book instead of the distillation. But still, what makes it worthwhile to like explore at all in the first place and you're uploading it at all?

结论：写作差，差在哪里？

Um I think it's all of that side of it that's the like — when when people will cite them being bad at writing. And it's that element of unpredictability, of being deliberately um choosing something that's novel that's like very directly contradictory to like the way that things are being produced.

Yeah. If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Lex Clips] Constantine's ruthless rise to power as emperor of the new Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的悖论：反世袭制度反而催生了最强的世袭王朝

戴克里先设计的"四帝共治"(Tetrarchy) 本质上是一场反血缘的制度实验——高级皇帝退休、初级皇帝晋升、再招募新人，试图用"绩效"取代"血统"。但历史的黑色幽默在于：正是这套反世袭系统，孵化了君士坦丁——一个被跳过的儿子，靠军队爱戴+内战淘汰赛翻盘，最终建立了统治拜占庭三个世纪的君士坦丁王朝。

跨领域连接：这与硅谷"反创始人崇拜"的悖论惊人相似——越是宣称"任人唯贤"的制度，越容易在权力真空中被最善于操纵人际网络的野心家夺取。戴克里先的四帝共治更像是当代科技公司的"董事会轮值制"，而君士坦丁则是那个懂得把每一次联姻都当作 term sheet、把每一场内战都当作敌意收购的 CEO。

二、君士坦丁堡不是首都，它是一个"物理钳夹"

一般叙述把君士坦丁堡说成"新罗马"、宗教/文化中心。但 Kaldellis 藏了一个更冷酷的洞见：这座城市的真正功能是"地质学式"的——它是一枚打在欧亚断层线上的铆钉。

罗马帝国每次内战都会沿着博斯普鲁斯–马尔马拉海–赫勒斯滂这条水系裂开，就像地壳沿着断层带反复错动。君士坦丁堡建成后，这条千年裂缝再也没有裂开过，帝国的断裂点被强行推到了西边的亚得里亚海。

出人意料的观察：君士坦丁堡的元老院招募 2500–3000 名东地中海精英，让他们用真金白银在城市里投资才能获得元老资格——这不是政治荣誉，这是强制性的股权绑定。君士坦丁发明的其实是"用不动产做抵押的地缘政治粘合剂"。这套逻辑与今天新加坡的永久居留投资门槛、迪拜的黄金签证、乃至欧盟"用共同货币锁死财政"的机制一脉相承——都是通过让精英在同一个篮子里放蛋来防止分裂。

三、历史评价的隐藏算法：为什么"杀子谜案"反而抬升了君士坦丁的地位

Kaldellis 承认君士坦丁杀了儿子克里斯普斯和妻子，没有任何官方解释——而通常皇帝做这种事都会大张旗鼓地污名化受害者。这个"沉默"本身是史料学中的一个奇异信号：能够沉默，说明权力已经稳固到不需要辩解。

更微妙的是 Kaldellis 的排名逻辑：他把君士坦丁列为第一，不是因为"喜欢"，而是因为"决策的后果影响力"。这暴露了历史学家一个很少明说的评估算法——历史地位 ≈ 决策的不可逆性 × 影响半径，而与道德评价几乎正交。

跨领域连接：这与投资界评估创始人的"Founder Mode"框架几乎同构——Steve Jobs、Elon Musk 这类"你不会想和他喝啤酒"的人物之所以被封神，不是因为人格魅力，而是因为他们做出了不可逆且高影响半径的决策。历史学的"伟大皇帝"评价体系，本质上是一套没有明说的期权定价模型：短期的道德成本被折现，长期的路径依赖被放大。君士坦丁之所以是"第一"，是因为他一次性买入了两个巨型看涨期权——迁都东方 + 皈依基督教——而这两个期权此后 1700 年都在持续行权。

📝 转录文本

开场：进入东罗马帝国

All right, we're back and let's talk about Constantine and uh Constantinople. Let's go to the rise of the East Roman Empire. Who was Constantine? Tell me about uh his rise to power and the founding of Constantinople in uh 330 AD.

君士坦丁的出身与四帝共治制度

Sure. So, Constantine was a son of one of Diocletian's colleagues. So, he comes from within the system. And uh the system is actually designed to be non-hereditary. In other words, the senior emperors would retire, the junior ones would move up to the senior position, and then they would recruit two new ones.

Mhm.

And they tried to skip the dynastic element. So, it — so, it would be kind of, let's just say kind of meritocratic, not whose son, you know, you were. Constantine was a son, he was passed over, but the armies liked him. He was actually quite competent.

Mhm.

内战淘汰赛：从六人到一人

And so, there's a series of civil wars among all of these uh successors of Diocletian, and Constantine emerges as the winner. There are periods when there are quite a few of them. There are like six at the at a time, but they —

[laughter]

— they gradually whittle down to one. Um so, you know, it's — as long as you have the quarterfinals, semifinals, and then the final war, and Constantine emerges in 324 as the sole ruler of the entire Roman Empire.

So, that's who he is. He's — he's definitely an insider, um very, very competent general at civil war. And um he gradually moves eastward. So, as he's conquering his rivals, who are often at times his fathers-in-law or his brothers-in-law, but because these people are all intermarry and they have alliances and they fall out, it's one of those kinds of situations.

东进与君士坦丁堡的选址

He moves eastward, and by the time he defeats his last rival, this uh an emperor called Licinius, in 324, uh he's in the East, and Licinius had his headquarters, uh or one of his headquarters, at this town called Byzantium. Byzantium is a city right where Constantinople was founded.

Mhm.

And it is at exactly the point where Europe meets Asia. So, on the Bosphorus. And it's a very strategic location. It always had been, right? So, whoever controls the straits of the Bosphorus — yeah, we don't need to explain how important that is.

Um and Constantine decides to create a city named after himself, Constantinople, on that spot. Byzantium. So, Byzantium essentially ceases to exist as a city and is replaced by Constantinople, aka New Rome.

"新罗马"作为一种可复制的概念

So, it's pretty clear that right from the beginning Constantine intended this to be a kind of branch office of Rome in the East, a kind of copy and paste of Rome in the East. And it's a plan that his successors continued, right?

Now, there are um a number of other cities in this uh, let's say in the generation or two before this, that were also treated as sort of quasi Romes. Basically, there was this historian in the 3rd century who said, "Well, wherever the emperor is, that's Rome." Right? Cuz emperors are now itinerant. They're no longer staying at Rome. They're mostly on the frontier. And Constantine is one of these frontier emperors. They will sometimes visit Rome, maybe once or twice, uh maybe once a decade, you know, something like this.

Um so, Rome becomes something that can be sort of copied and pasted in other parts of the empire. Uh a bunch of other cities get called, you know, a new Rome, another Rome, an alternative Rome, my Rome. Um and Constantinople is the last and most successful of those.

地理位置的战略意义

So, maybe can you speak geographically the importance of Constantinople? It's an interesting place cuz it uh —

Yeah.

— when you're talking about a land so large as the West and the East, with enemies, with threats from all kinds of different directions, the location where the emperor sits is important.

Very. So, look at Rome. Right? So, it's right in the middle of Italy, right in the middle of the Mediterranean. Very safe location, in a way, but it's very far from the frontiers. And this is a period when emperors need to be with the armies, right? They're not senatorial emperors who are sitting in Rome having meetings with senators. They are military emperors who are marching along the frontiers. Um mostly with generals, rather than civilian administration. So, they're doing their job. Like, this is the hard work. They're slogging through the mud of winter campaigns, right? Uh they're not just sitting in a palace enjoying themselves, when they could.

Um but Rome just becomes inconvenient because of that. Um and if you look at Constantinople, it is kind of halfway between the Danube frontier and the Euphrates frontier. So, if you want to prioritize the East, or treat it as an equal part of the Roman Empire, as these emperors all did. In fact, almost all emperors who had a choice about whether they would stay in the East or stay in the West, chose the East. There's only one who chooses the West, and that's Valentinian later in the 4th century. All the others understand that the East is where it's at.

四方节点：南北东西的枢纽

And Constantinople is right between the Danube and the Euphrates. These are the two major frontiers. And so, it allows emperors to move between the two.

And also, look at its location north, south, east, west, right? So, north, south, it is the node between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean. Right? And east, west, it's precisely where Europe meets Asia. Right? And because there's a break there, so there's a Bosporus, the Sea of Marmara, the Hellespont, the Roman Empire during the civil wars, it would tend to break there.

Mhm.

And you would have one emperor on one side in Asia and you have another emperor on the other side. And this just kept happening. And Constantinople ultimately functions as a kind of clamp that unifies this whole area. So, basically the Balkans and uh Asia Minor and Syria as a kind of unit, right?

新元老院：精英的政治绑定

And you know, what is a new Rome? A new Rome is among other things a new Senate, right? And Constantine and his successors recruit this new Senate for the new Rome. So, we're talking about maybe 2 and 1/2 thousand, 3 thousand men, um you know, at at at its peak. And these are all recruited from all of these areas. You know, some come from Rome, but others come from the Aegean region, from Asia Minor, uh Eastern Mediterranean. So, these are the wealthiest people usually, most well-connected elites from the whole Eastern Mediterranean, and they come together and form a new purpose, common purpose in Constantinople.

And the empire never breaks there again, right? So, it — it holds together this part of the world. The breaking point now, interestingly, moves to the Adriatic, right? So, when in 395 we talked about East and West kind of splitting, it breaks at the Adriatic cuz Constantinople is now holding that whole Eastern world together. Cuz all of these elites — they now, they're invested in it. They're literally investing in it. They have to invest in it in order to be senators in Constantinople.

Uh so, that's kind of one of the functions that it — that it performs, a strategic function, there's a political function, there's also a cultural one, but um you know, you can talk about that, too. Uh but that's what you see there on that map, the northwest, east, south.

插曲：十大皇帝榜单

So, uh just some — linger on it. So, you uh you do a podcast, presenting with friends, but you're also a guest on podcasts and I should mention you [snorts] being a guest multiple times on the excellent The History of Byzantium podcast hosted by Robin Pierson. I highly recommend people listen to it — to all of your appearances. You have a lot of really fascinating ones.

But the reason I bring all of that up is one of the episodes you do the fun thing of selecting the top 10 emperors of Byzantium. And spoiler alert, everybody should go listen to the full list cuz you justify all the different options. Uh but Constantine ends up in the number one spot.

Yeah.

And so can you just add a little bit more depth why he would be in your number one spot now that we're talking about him?

君士坦丁的残酷面

Yes, it's not because I sort of like him as a person. Right? [laughter] I mean, as you said, he was pretty murderous.

Yeah.

Right? All those wars. So, clearly ambitious, murderous, ruthless, basically.

Can you actually speak to the murderous aspect? He — he seemed to have murdered a lot of people in his family.

Yes, that's because what we call his family were also alliances of convenience that he made on the way to the top. Um and when those alliances or, you know, those, you know, those wives or those fathers-in-law were no longer convenient or or turned against him, he had no hesitation in removing them.

克里斯普斯之谜：无法解释的处决

Um but he's also, you know, he gets a little extra bonus because he also murdered his own son, Crispus, who was apparently a very competent and popular kind of general. And we don't know why.

Mhm.

Um he executes Crispus soon after defeating Licinius and then follows it up with the murder of his wife, not Crispus's mother, and we don't know why.

So, the funny thing is that when emperors do this sort of thing, if there's any kind of remotely like a good reason, they'll blast it through all of the media, right? Like Crispus was rebelling against me or whatever. There's nothing.

Yeah.

He doesn't even try to justify it, which means it must have been something unjustifi- — like I don't know, I can't process it.

And also I read that there's historians of the time would just ignore that that happened.

Yes. Um well, sort of. So, when Crispus is alive, it's all like praising Crispus as a — oh, you Constantine, you great emperor and your wonderful son who will whatever. And then Crispus is executed and he just disappears from the — like they never talk about it.

评价标准：为什么君士坦丁仍居榜首

Actually, there's a — there's another side to a side. You — I think you mentioned uh whether it's on your podcast or elsewhere that if — if you had to have a beer with any of the emperors that you probably wouldn't. So, it's not like some of — some of the most impactful, some of the most um influential and great emperors that we talk about — they, they may not be very pleasant people.

Right. So, let's set aside whether you'd like to have a beer with these people. The reason I put Constantine at the top of the list is because the decisions that he made were so consequential. Just important and uh for the most part su- um not bad decisions. Uh so, I couldn't not put him at the top of the list.

The f-, you know, the creating Constantinople and converting to Christianity and setting the empire on a path to conversion. These are major, like world history level decisions. Right? Now, not many emperors do that. In fact, Constantine is in like his own league when it comes to those kinds of decisions.

My other criteria for top emperors were basically those who did the job well. Right? Not making, you know, these kinds of earth, you know, shattering decisions or, you know, world history changing decisions, but just who performed the role competently with results that impacted the lives of their subjects for the better.

Mhm.

So, those are the other criteria that I used.

[snorts]

It's difficult to juggle these different criteria, right? In order to create a systematic list. And by the way, we historians generally don't do these kinds of things, but you said it's fun. It is fun.

Was fun.

Um and it allows you to talk about certain things that you — you might not otherwise do. So, yeah, that's why Constantine was at the top.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的悖论：反世袭制度反而催生了最强的世袭王朝
戴克里先设计的"四帝共治"(Tetrarchy) 本质上是一场**反血缘的制度实验**——高级皇帝退休、初级皇帝晋升、再招募新人，试图用"绩效"取代"血统"。但历史的黑色幽默在于：正是这套反世袭系统，孵化了君士坦丁——一个被跳过的儿子，靠军队爱戴+内战淘汰赛翻盘，最终建立了统治拜占庭三个世纪的君士坦丁王朝。
**跨领域连接**：这与硅谷"反创始人崇拜"的悖论惊人相似——越是宣称"任人唯贤"的制度，越容易在权力真空中被最善于操纵人际网络的野心家夺取。戴克里先的四帝共治更像是当代科技公司的"董事会轮值制"，而君士坦丁则是那个懂得把每一次联姻都当作 term sheet、把每一场内战都当作敌意收购的 CEO。
## 二、君士坦丁堡不是首都，它是一个"物理钳夹"
一般叙述把君士坦丁堡说成"新罗马"、宗教/文化中心。但 Kaldellis 藏了一个更冷酷的洞见：**这座城市的真正功能是"地质学式"的**——它是一枚打在欧亚断层线上的铆钉。
罗马帝国每次内战都会沿着博斯普鲁斯–马尔马拉海–赫勒斯滂这条水系裂开，就像地壳沿着断层带

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Commercial Problem of The Tour de France
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、环法本质上不是体育赛事，而是一场"付费朝圣"

大多数分析框架会问："为什么环法不卖门票？"——这个问题本身就问错了。真正惊人的事实是：环法从诞生第一天起，就不是一门"赛事生意"，而是一门"注意力批发生意"。1903年它被创造出来，目的就是卖报纸——赛事是内容，报纸是产品。一百多年过去，媒介从报纸变成了电视和数字流媒体，但商业内核完全没变：它把体育变成了一次持续三周、横跨1300公里的注意力流，然后向广告主批发这条流。

这也解释了那个看似荒谬的现象——1200万现场观众竟然一分钱不用付。因为在这套模型里，观众不是"顾客"，而是布景。他们挤满小镇、堆满山口的画面，本身就是赛事卖给赞助商和转播方的"产品成分"之一。你如果向他们收门票，等于把免费的临时演员变成付费的挑剔评委——收入没多多少，画面却毁了。这个逻辑跟麦加朝觐、圣地亚哥朝圣之路是同构的：它们都是被"守护者"（Amaury家族 ≈ 教士阶层）刻意保持在非最优商业化状态的公共仪式。视频里把它类比高尔夫大师赛是准确的，但类比麦加更贴近本质。

二、职业车队其实是"伪装成体育队的媒体公司"——那个"逃出主车群30分钟"的骑手，是全球最纯粹的注意力套利者

视频里一笔带过的一个细节值得放大一万倍：次级车队会安排骑手在赛程中段孤身冲出主车群，因为直升机会跟拍他半小时，这半小时就是纯赞助商曝光时间。这是我在这段转录里读到最震撼的一句话。

它意味着什么？它意味着这些骑手在"竞技意义上完全无用"的时刻——他们不可能赢，逃逸迟早会被主车群吞回——却在商业意义上完成了一次教科书级的绩效营销。这跟一家SaaS初创公司花预算买Google关键词、跟一个YouTuber为了算法故意做长视频、跟一支加密货币团队砸钱买交易所"逃逸曝光"，在数学结构上是完全一致的：牺牲局部效率，换取一段可以在下轮合同里定价的"注意力时长"。

所以那位车队老板的名言——"足球俱乐部拥有球场、转播分成、球员转会资产；自行车队拥有一个球衣logo和一句'祈祷赞助商续约'"——其实低估了车队的资产。他们真正拥有的资产是"可产生逃逸事件的骑手"，也就是一批能按需生产电视时长的注意力工人。整个环法车队产业，本质上是一个把肌肉燃烧转化为品牌曝光秒数的转换器。

三、兴奋剂不是环法的"污点"，而是它商业结构的必然产物；Ineos的溃败则揭示了一条跨行业规律

大多数评论把兴奋剂当成道德问题或监管问题，但从商业结构看，环法的兴奋剂问题是被商业模型硬编码进去的： - 获胜的边际收益是天文级的赞助溢价（Sky时代Braillesford的"边际收益累积"哲学被商业转译成品牌形象）； - 失败的边际成本却被外部化给骑手个人的身体； - 而收益归车队与赞助商，风险归运动员——这种收益社会化、风险个体化的结构，正是催生系统性作弊的经典条件。

它跟华尔街2008、跟Wells Fargo的假账户、跟Uber早期的Greyball，是同一个模式：当一个系统给参与者提供了"赢了归你、输了归别人的身体"的支付函数时，作弊不是异常值，而是均衡解。1960年代Tommy Simpson死在赛道上，Armstrong时代的系统性doping，Sky/Freeman医生的丑闻——不是一连串"坏苹果"，而是同一个激励结构的三次重演。

而Ineos在Ratcliffe接手后的"死亡螺旋"，则暴露了另一条更普世的规律——"公司化"如何杀死"创业性"。Sky时代的Braillesford用一种近乎黑客式的"边际收益"思维（本质是精益创业的体育版）征服了这项运动；Ineos带来了"结构、合规、大公司节奏"，冠军骑手Pidcock在合同结束前就走了，理由是"太企业化"。这跟Google收购DeepMind后的文化摩擦、Facebook收购Instagram后的创始人出走、任何一次"大公司吃掉初创黑客团队"的案例，是同一个反应式——规模化的管理系统会自动过滤掉当初创造价值的那种非理性激情。

而Ratcliffe同时也在改造曼联。所以最有趣的一个跨界预测是：如果你想预判Ratcliffe的曼联改革结局，不要看足球圈的分析，去看Ineos车队这三年的曲线——那才是他的管理风格的高保真沙盘。

📝 转录文本

Introduction: A Sporting Event That Sits Apart

One of the greatest sporting events in the global calendar — and one which kind of, I think, slightly sort of sits apart in a way, doesn't it? It sort of sits on its own, partially because of how long it takes. It's such a different type of event in all sorts of ways: from ticketing (lack thereof) to the way in which, um, the sort of sheer duration every day but also over a weekly period.

We are of course talking about the Tour de France, um, the world's greatest cycling race, um, which starts, Charlie, on 4th of July I think it is, isn't it? So what's the date today — the 2nd? So it starts, yeah, starts on Saturday.

Um, and therefore, you know, we probably… one of our most requested topics. We actually haven't covered cycling at all on this show. So we've known for a couple of months that we were going to, and as with everything I think it's great to do it as and when there's a moment of attention about to be thrust upon the sport.

Um, and as you very elegantly highlighted there, the Tour is obviously the main feature of the cycling calendar and, as I've taken a look at this, you know, that's obviously amazing, but it's also, you know, a significant problem for cycling with so much dependence, particularly commercially, falling on one event.

Charlie's Personal Experience & the Four Greats

Charlie, how much did you know about, um, the Tour before? You know, of course, I mean, you know, beyond obviously — maybe a fair amount. I visited it myself, um, when I was, um, in my 20s and went on a summer sort of break travelling with friends in France, um, and went up into the mountains specifically to watch a Tour stage, um, which was great and extraordinary. You get this enormous caravan descending on what are quite small villages and towns and just totally swamping them. So yeah, a sort of fair amount.

This was the era of Miguel Indurain, who is one of the four greats of cycling history if you look back. Um, four men have won the Tour five times, if that makes sense: Jacques Anquetil, Eddy Merckx (a Belgian), Bernard Hinault (another Frenchman) and Miguel Indurain (Spanish) — Indurain being the most recent, which was around the sort of turn of the century, around that era back then.

Charlie's right in saying that the Tour is the biggest deal in cycling. But there are two other what we call Grand Tours: the Giro d'Italia and the Vuelta a España. So that's the Italian and Spanish tours. And these are the three major European countries that have got big cycling history. This is where cycling is sort of an integral part of the national psyche.

The British Cycling Surge: Team Sky, Marginal Gains, and Ineos

Now, this will slightly surprise people, particularly if anyone's sort of come from overseas to visit the UK in the last 10 years and we go, "Well, surely it's a massive thing in the UK as well because there are cyclists everywhere." That definitely was not the case 30 to 40 years ago.

But what you saw was a bizarre — um, I wouldn't even say resurgence, just call it surgence — of, um, British cycling that happened between about 2005 to 2020. And that was partially led on the Olympic track, where the emergence of lottery money to fund British Olympic sports — um, a lot of that was ploughed into cycling — and it was determined this was a sport where Britain could pick up a lot of medals in the Olympics, and they started testing youngsters for just, you know, specifically the traits that would make them track cyclists. Um, and that was very successful.

And then for a time the road cycling, which is what, um, the Tours are, um, also took off. And that was under, um, the guise of something called Team Sky, which was overseen by a man called David Brailsford — now Sir Dave Brailsford — which came into the historic cycling world, an upstart English team coming into the established, you know, EU cycling scene, and bringing what famously became known as the accumulation of marginal gains, which was Brailsford's sporting philosophy which said: if you pay very minute attention to every single element of a sport, in this case cycling, those will add up over time into a big advantage.

And Team Sky, as a result of that, became the dominant, um, cycling team during the 2010s. And you saw, you know, psy… British cycling greats like Bradley Wiggins and Chris Froome and actually Geraint Thomas winning the Tour de France, um, and Team Sky being the dominant team on top of that.

Of course, as we'll come on to, that was all clouded by a doping controversy which almost came out a bit subsequent to, um, a lot of that success — we can come on to that in a moment. And that team, in time, was bought by Ineos — Jim Ratcliffe — and really since Ineos took it over, it's ended into a sort of a bit a death spiral, that team, and has never quite been the same again.

The Economics: A Family-Owned, Secretive Structure

Anyway, so that's a sort of landscape view. But Charlie, you've been looking into the economics of it as well. Um, what do you make of it?

Well, firstly, what I make of it is: it's all very secretive. And that of course comes — and sorry, secretive from a financial standpoint. So everything that you look at is reported figures.

And that's because the Amaury family — which is French family, the owners of L'Équipe, the French, um, newspaper sports publication — the Tour was actually built out, um, from newspapers. You know, it was created to sell newspapers in the early 1900s when the boom of cycling really took off. And there's a few really cool videos on YouTube that I highly recommend going and watching, just about those early years of the Tour, because it's fascinating to see how it's developed. But the audience and the fanfare around it, even in those early 1900s, was absolutely incredible.

And so clearly very early on, you know, there was a massive demand and a product that fans were deeply engaged with. But what it ensured really was that the structure was very central. And so the Amaury family, L'Équipe, basically controlled the majority of the Tour, and their kind of sports organization ASO is, you know, the kind of majority controller.

The big problem with that, Charlie — we've seen it in a lot of sports — is that with so much control then comes, um, centralized decision-making that doesn't necessarily benefit the growth of the event or the total sport. It actually operates quite similar to something like the Masters. They don't maximize the commercial opportunity.

Revenue Breakdown: No Tickets, All Broadcast & Sponsorship

Um, and therefore the numbers that have come out suggest that the Tour, despite having 12 million or so people that line the streets or roadsides throughout the 3-week event — which is, you know, a huge number — none of them pay for tickets. All of the money really is driven by the commercial value and the broadcast, right?

So broadcast is about, they think, between 50 to 70%, sponsorship let's say 30 to 40%. Um, and then you have some host fees as well at about 5. But the estimate is anywhere up to €150 million that's generated throughout the Tour, which is, you know, a lot of money — a significant amount of money — but is it a huge amount of money when you look at the number of people engaged in it, when you look at the value attributed to some of the teams?

The prize money sitting around 2 million, I think, Charlie, for the winner — which is actually, you know, a tiny amount of money when some of the top teams' budgets, um… you mentioned Ineos, UAE, uh, which is, uh, Tadej Pogačar, who — you know, he's going for his fifth. You mentioned the people that have won five — Pogačar is going for his fifth Tour de France this year. They spend up to about 50 to 60 million euros — that's their budgets, right at the top of it. But then you have teams also around 20 million, so you have a big spread.

But also, if those teams aren't earning from the success in the Tour, you get a business model trying to figure out where the value really lies commercially — is where it really nets out for the teams.

The Sponsorship Model & The "Breakaway Rider" Arbitrage

Um, you mentioned Sky. I mean, the value for the Tour has always been: you sky, you buy the infantry, you put your badge everywhere, and hopefully for 3 weeks you are front and centre.

Um, but there are some quite cool stories — and I didn't really think about this, Charlie — but they were saying, you know, if you're maybe let's say a slightly lesser team whose brand doesn't get as much value, you'll suddenly see someone break from the peloton and they'll just have a charge, and for half an hour there's a helicopter above them — they are on TV for that whole time, and that is full sponsor time for that person.

Now really, from a sporting sense, there's no need for that rider to do that. It's not helping his race. But it helps drive the value for the brand because he's been on TV for the last 30 minutes and they can trade that in at their next negotiation.

Um, so Charlie, a long way round of saying: I think that there's a fascinating model here. There's one that, um, has solid numbers behind it — you know, yes, good broadcast, yes, good commercial. There's one that has no ticketing revenue, which is very strange for a sporting event. Um, but at the same time, with significant amount of control sitting in one family and a significant amount of the sport of cycling reliant on a lot of the interest generated by the Tour, and some of the money generated by the Tour…

How has the sport of cycling positioned itself to move forward and develop in — as we talk about every week — this ever competitive sporting environment? And that's where I think a lot of the questions that we've got on the Tour have come about. It's when looking at all these various sports, when looking at, um, the golfs, the smaller sports, the places where things have broken away in the last few years — how does cycling sit on that? Um, and so that was something that I just found fascinating taking a look at this.

A Defense of the Amaury Family: Guardians, Not Optimizers

Yeah, it's a great summary, Charlie. You know, I don't disagree with a word you've just said. Um, I think what one is in danger of underestimating is the extent to which the Amaury family are guardians of their own event — just like the Masters committee are guardians of their own event — and because they don't have to satisfy higher pay masters in terms of optimizing every single little bit of revenue.

Part of what they're doing here is they're maintaining the spirit of the Tour. So if you were to start looking at ticketing people who are just standing by the side of the road, you would lose some of the essence of what the Tour is.

And I think the Amaury family — I obviously can't speak on their behalf, but I would speculate that they think that, "Look, we do really well out of this, right? We created it as a family to basically market newspapers. Um, it's grown into this amazing thing unto itself, which makes us a profit of about 30 to 40 million euros a year. That's enough. We don't need to do more than that because actually we're just a family and we care about the spirit of what we've created. And in fact, maybe even commercially we might potentially endanger that going down the track if people start to perceive that it's not what it was."

And the net result of that is: it's one of these events which is conspicuously almost exactly the same as it always has been. As you say, you can watch YouTube Tour stuff from the 1950s and it's essentially the same. Now, I know in the detail people will say, "Well, in the detail of what the teams are doing in terms of the engineering around the bikes, in terms of the physical side around the players and around the riders, I should say, and so on so forth." Of course, that has all moved and changed. But from a spectator point of view, it is really the same as when Bernard Hinault was the sort of great global hero.

And I think that is something precious, which is very, um, easy to lose if one does try and squeeze it for every last pip. And in terms of the profile of cycling, personally I would say the last 20 years has been probably cycling's most high-profile period. Certainly of my lifetime, it's been very, very high profile. So I don't think one can really say that cycling's got a problem and the Tour is the problem. I would look at it the other way round and say cycling has become very high-profile, and the Tour de France remaining as this pristine sort of, you know, spirit-of-the-Tour type thing is fantastic.

The Non-Pristine Side: Doping Scandals from Simpson to Ineos to Armstrong

Now, we can't avoid the non-pristine bit, which is doping problems. And by the way, that is as much part of Tour history as any other part. Right? So you go back to the 1960s and you find, um — I'm trying to remember the name — I think it was Tommy Simpson, the British rider who died during the Tour. That was also an era where riders were taking substances. This has been going on an awful long time.

Now obviously, like other sports, there is, you know, extensive doping testing that goes on. But it's such a big part of the history of cycling that the suspicion has always been that there are some people who are pushing the boundaries beyond where the testers can really get to.

Um, and I think it's just worth reflecting on the Ineos scandal. Um, because it is very much related to that era when Team Sky was dominant within the sport. And this was when effectively the then medical director of that team, um, Richard Freeman — um, I mean, I'm just speaking a bit from memory here — but there was a sort of incident where effectively he was carrying, um, a parcel including certain elements, certain substances, um, those to the team.

Anyway, there was a long, sort of, uh, scandal — sporting scandal — went on over this, because this was in the team. This was in the era when Wiggins and Froome were at their peak and very much Brailsford and Co. had been marketing themselves and had been marketed as the clean face of, of, of cycling, of road racing.

Um, and this came along and it really blindsided people because it then threw question marks over those victories which had been won previously by this team. Um, and in the end Richard Freeman was — um, I think actually he was stripped of his medical licence, um, certainly for a while. So effectively there was, you know, an acceptance by the sport that that had gone wrong.

And then of course, predating that, going back 20 years into the Lance Armstrong era, when it became clear that not just Lance Armstrong himself but that other riders who'd been riding at the front of the peloton had been, um, involved in various substances which were either fully illegal against the rules or pushing the boundaries in various different ways.

So I think we just can't have a discussion about cycling without acknowledging the fact that this is a sport that has been significantly blighted by doping scandals.

The Teams' Problem: All Cost, Little Return

And of course, when that happens, right, it's an image problem. And an image problem is a damage to the money that will be spent on it by brands and the associations that they want to have. And really, when a huge amount of the development of your sport is dependent on the commercial development of your sport, that is a significant problem.

Because the team side fascinates me. You know, you mentioned there — and I agree to an extent about, you know, the need to maximize it. But at the same time, when you're asking teams, or you're giving, you know, huge costs of entries, budgets to teams to spend and you're giving very little back, at some point there's going to be a point where a team or an owner turns around and goes, "Well, I'm going to want more for my investment than a few million quid for winning it."

And that's why, you know, say if you're looking at those kind of 50 million spends, there eventually needs to be a point of return. And something that the Cofidis, one of the team's boss, said that I thought was quite interesting — we do like a quote. Um, this is a quote directly from him. He said:

"A football club owns a stadium, a share of league TV money, and a transfer asset base. A cycling team owns a logo on a jersey and a prayer that the sponsor resigns. That's the whole balance sheet."

So for these teams, building a business model on something as fragile as that — which, as you've highlighted, can come crashing down for various reasons — that as a sports entity, as an investment, as a decision-making moment to decide, well, where do I want to put my money? I think it's an interesting question to ask.

Cost Structures: Are the Riders Overpaid?

It is a range. We just come from a discussion about tennis players' pay. Um, and we're coming on to this now. And I guess, so first of all, the moment which it becomes a strategic question for the Amaury family is when they don't have enough teams entering. Right? So if there are enough teams entering, then de facto is not a problem. If they don't have enough teams entering, then it is a problem, and then they have to start looking at whether more of their profits of 30 to 40 million a year need to be recycled back into the teams.

I would say that for teams to complain, they may need to have a look at their own cost structures. You know, these cyclists are very, very, very well paid. I don't know if you had a look at this, but…

Well, Pogačar gets about 8 to 10 million a year, I think. And I think quite a lot of the teams — correct me if I'm wrong — are spending 60% at least, some more, on their wages and staff costs.

They are. And even an average professional cyclist is being paid the thick end of a million bucks a year, or million pounds a year. I think it's £700,000 a year on average — that includes those who just ride down in the peloton, just domestiques who are just sort of part of making up the back end of the team.

And the question I think I would ask the teams is: okay, perhaps you need to on the one hand be speaking to the Amaury family, but on the other hand you need to be discussing amongst yourselves about having a better cost cap system. I mean, if this is a sport that doesn't generate vast amounts of revenue, then what on earth are you doing paying leading practitioners the thick end of 10 million a year? I mean, 10 million a year is absolutely colossal. There are world leading golfers who don't earn that, in a sport that's frankly much more widespread and has much bigger TV rights revenue.

So I think, you know, the bottom line is: at the moment it seems there are enough teams. Obviously they are, if not happy, then capable to meet their bills in one way, shape or form or another. At the moment the whole ecosystem is working. But I do take your point that ultimately it looked quite creaky and it looks like it's overdependent on commercial revenue.

Um, and whilst — funny enough, we discussed this at SailGP a couple of weeks ago — so SailGP is heavily dependent on commercial revenue, but equally the whole cost structure is very, very controlled and limited. Um, whereas what you're looking at here is a situation where you're entirely dependent on commercial revenue and your cost control system is not really there. Um, and that is a dangerous place to be, I think.

The Predictability Problem: A Formula 1 Parallel

Well, Charlie, you're getting into a Formula 1 type, um, discussion, you know. And again, reading a lot of the commentary of the people that are big fans and very much in the know — more so than I am — um, you know, we're pointing towards the predictability problem attached to the spend problem.

So Pogačar's team, the UAE team, basically all-conquering — one of the biggest budgets, the best rider potentially of all time — makes it predictable. And the Tour's always had an element of unpredictability, which has been a huge part of the appeal. And if you start getting success tied to the biggest budget teams, and you have teams on 17 million not 50 million, and you lose some of that competitive edge based on the finances and the structures of the sport commercially, then they do need to probably have a look at it, because that's a danger to the product.

And again, you know, if you look at the big moments that changed something like a Formula 1 — bringing something that, you know, was just losing huge amounts of money because there was an ability to spend whatever you wanted, creating a competitive environment where everyone felt that they could attribute X amount of cash to develop a really competitive entity — was the turning point. Now, if you did something like this in a cycling environment and encouraged those people to invest because of that — as you said, because the teams had better discipline and controls in place — would it attract more?

New Ownership: Nation-States Enter the Peloton

And look, just finally on the teams — you know, it's interesting. You see the UAE involved, Bahrain, Kazakhstan are involved. You know, the types of team owners have shifted from, you know, maybe a type of Sky brand to now you're getting more kind of countries involved, similar to as you've seen in other sports.

The Disruptor: "One Cycling" and PIF

Um, Charlie, I don't know if you've ever seen the bits on One Cycling. But you know, this is always interesting because people are always in the market for new opportunity, new IP, and new ideas, uh, as we know. And when you've got a legacy product that's quality but maybe not optimizing itself effectively on the business side, generally there's someone sniffing around looking to do something better.

One Cycling is, um, a kind of vision that's come out of PIF or SURJ Investments. It's basically the latest — I don't want to LIV Golf it, because it's just a cheap way of saying it — but it's a challenge, should we say, to the existing structures at cycling to try and build something that is really commercially viable, that does give it a future in ways that the tour — well, they see the Tour doesn't — and really tries to maximize a commercial output in ways where the Tour leaves money on the table.

Uh, they've naturally struggled to move the needle a huge amount in this because of the governance challenges that exist and, again, the control the Amaury family have of the organization and the Tour obviously being the key legacy product. Again, you can't just take people out and ignore the fact that the Tour is going to be the thing that everyone wants to ride in. But we've started to see moves over the last kind of couple of years of people trying to disrupt this space.

The Ineos Warning: When Corporate Kills Sporting Spirit

Yeah, um, well, it's going to be interesting to see, I think. You know, as we've been clear all along, our belief is it's exceedingly difficult and expensive to disrupt the big legacy sports events, um, for all sorts of different reasons.

Um, just leave on one last point to do with Ineos, um, and the demise of that team under the leadership of Jim Ratcliffe. And there are just a little few little warning signs there about how his management style works within sport.

Um, when Tom Pidcock — who's the leading British rider right now, ranked I think fifth or sixth in the world — when he left the team well before the end of his contract, he sort of made some sort of reference to this, the sort of the management style being pretty corporate, um, corporate in style.

Yeah. And I think that it's just a really interesting thing that I'm a great believer that sports needs sports performance and needs professionalizing. People need to look at things in a systematic way. But it still needs to feel like you're in a sporting organization, not a corporate organization. It's that sort of balance.

Um, and it just feels like on the Ineos side that what they had at Sky was something quite entrepreneurial, quite sort of freewheeling and quite innovative — leaving aside some of the corporate problems we've mentioned already. Um, and they've moved into something where there's more centralized control, more sort of, um, corporate style sort of speed.

There's one former rider who is unnamed, who in a recent article about the demise of Ineos said:

"Things were good with Sky, but with Ineos there is too much structure, compliance, and trying to act like a big corporation."

And it will just be interesting for me to see over the next few years what happens at Man U, because that very much is what he's trying to do there as well, right? He's trying to make Man U act like a big corporation. And I think there's always just got to be a little question mark over whether that really works in sport.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、环法本质上不是体育赛事，而是一场"付费朝圣"
大多数分析框架会问："为什么环法不卖门票？"——这个问题本身就问错了。真正惊人的事实是：**环法从诞生第一天起，就不是一门"赛事生意"，而是一门"注意力批发生意"**。1903年它被创造出来，目的就是卖报纸——赛事是内容，报纸是产品。一百多年过去，媒介从报纸变成了电视和数字流媒体，但商业内核完全没变：**它把体育变成了一次持续三周、横跨1300公里的注意力流，然后向广告主批发这条流**。
这也解释了那个看似荒谬的现象——1200万现场观众竟然一分钱不用付。因为在这套模型里，观众不是"顾客"，而是**布景**。他们挤满小镇、堆满山口的画面，本身就是赛事卖给赞助商和转播方的"产品成分"之一。你如果向他们收门票，等于把免费的临时演员变成付费的挑剔评委——收入没多多少，画面却毁了。这个逻辑跟麦加朝觐、圣地亚哥朝圣之路是同构的：**它们都是被"守护者"（Amaury家族 ≈ 教士阶层）刻意保持在非最优商业化状态的公共仪式**。视频里把它类比高尔夫大师赛是准确的，但类比麦加更贴近本质。
## 二、职业车队其实是"伪装成体育队的媒体公司"

item.title: [Lex Clips] The Roman tax system that held an empire together for 1,000 years | Anthony Kaldellis
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、税收不是"取走"，而是"写入"——一种被误读的国家操作系统

大多数人把税收理解为国家从社会抽取资源的行为。但 Kaldellis 在这里描绘的东罗马税制其实颠覆了这个直觉：一年三次上门、既收钱、又收实物、还征劳役和兵源的税吏，本质上并不是"收割者"，而是帝国的写入头——把国家的时间感（日历）、空间感（地籍）、价值感（货币）和身份感（罗马公民）一遍遍地刻进每一个村庄的意识里。

有一个非常反直觉的观察值得停下来品味：Kaldellis 说，"隔绝的农民"这种浪漫化的中世纪意象，在东罗马史料中根本找不到证据——甚至连隐修的圣人也是用"连人口普查员都到不了那里"来定义"深山"的。也就是说，这个帝国的偏远程度不是用距离度量的，而是用税务能否触达来度量的。这与我们通常想象的"中世纪松散、封建、割裂"完全相反。真相是：一个每四个月就派人下乡一次的国家，事实上比多数现代宗教都更频繁地"临在"于臣民生活之中。

二、宦官：一种解决"委托–代理问题"的生物学方案

宦官为什么长期在东罗马权力中枢扎堆？主流解释常常停在"防止君主戴绿帽"或"忠诚可靠"这类道德化说法。但 Kaldellis 的分析实际上把它推向了一个组织理论的高度：宦官是皇帝防止官僚体系"喧宾夺主"的平行权力轨道。

跨领域的联想会让人拍案——这几乎是现代公司治理里"独立董事"的古代原型：一个没有家族利益、没有派系网络、其存续完全依赖于最高权力者的角色，被专门放进决策链条以打破既得利益的自我复制。更进一步说，这也像生物免疫系统里的 NK 细胞：不属于任何组织系统，正因如此才能识别并压制那些"看起来正常但正在坐大"的细胞集群。甚至可以拉到区块链——宦官这种"无家族传承"的设计，本质上是一种硬件层面的零知识证明：他们没办法为下一代谋利，因此他们代表的利益天然不会分叉。你可能第一次意识到：一千年前的皇宫内廷，在用生物学解决 21 世纪企业还没解决好的代理人问题。

三、真正"不可能"的数据点：一千年，几乎没有用军队镇压过自己人

如果只能从这段对谈里挑一个最令人震撼的历史事实，我会挑这个：一个拥有 10 万到 25 万常备军的国家，在千年时间里几乎从不把军队作为社会控制工具。 这在人类政治史里是极其罕见的信号——从秦到清、从罗马前期到近代埃及的穆巴拉克，"调军队对付百姓"都是权力工具箱里的默认选项。

而东罗马之所以"用不上"，Kaldellis 给出了一个几乎被现代政治学忽略的答案：因为不需要。这里出现了一个跨领域的深层类比——它更像是一个健康的免疫系统：不是靠不断压制炎症来维持稳定，而是压根就没有慢性炎症。这背后是一个我们通常不敢承认的政治真相：长期合法性不是被"建构"出来的，而是当国家的税收、货币、日历、教会、司法真的下沉到最小的村庄时，"我们"这个共同体就已经在无声中被完成了。所谓 monarchic republic（君主制共和国）不是一个学术措辞的调皮，而是在说：这里没有"皇帝 vs 人民"的对立结构可以让军队去镇压，因为皇帝本人在制度想象中就是为 res publica 服务的公职人员。

四、附赠：一个让你重新看待美国税法的观察

对话里那段关于 IRS 的调侃其实埋着一个精彩的历史学观察——东罗马根本没有"税法"，只有一堆"豁免"。修道院豁免、贵族豁免、灾年豁免、村庄豁免……皇帝每隔一段时间要"清屋"一次，把所有豁免作废重来。听起来像不像现代软件工程里的技术债 (technical debt)？每一个 feature flag 都是善意加上去的，但久而久之，代码库变成了没人敢重构的黑箱。Kaldellis 无意中揭示了一个人类组织学的通则：任何一个足够久的规则系统，最终都会退化为"例外的集合"，而不是"规则的集合"。 美国税法今天的样子，东罗马一千年前就演过一遍。

📝 转录文本

Why the East Lasted: Structure of Government

Lex: I think it's fair to say that the reason the east — part of the reason the east lasted as long as it did — is the topic that we've mentioned, which is the structure of government. So can we just zoom out once again and explain the key ways that government functioned in the East Roman Empire and how it evolved over the lifetime of the empire?

Kaldellis: Very broadly speaking, there's civilian and military, right? And you might actually add the church. It's entirely plausible to treat the church as a kind of government institution at this time, but let's just stick with military and civilian.

The Military

The military is pretty straightforward, would be recognizable to any student of military history. This is how you recruit, organize, equip, pay, and lead soldiers. And the East Roman state tends to have a larger military — at least paper strength. Active is a different matter. Like how many soldiers you take on a campaign is a different matter, but how many soldiers you maintain in the provinces generally, right, is — it tends to be on the large side for medieval society. So it compares favorably to say the caliphate later on, which is a much more massive territorial entity.

So the military — it can have anywhere between a 100,000 at a minimum at a low point up to maybe about 250,000 at a high point depending on the period. It's a lot of people. It's a lot of men. Factor in their families, right? These are also part of the whole military-economic complex, if you will.

Lex: And they're career military. So like this is their job. And is the military, for most of the history of the East Roman Empire, defensive military?

Kaldellis: Mostly, but not always.

Lex: Yeah. And we'll talk about Justinian.

Kaldellis: Sure.

The Civilian Administration

And so then you have the civilian administration. Civilian administration does a number of things. Principally, it is to find the funds to pay for this army, which is the single largest expense on the state budget, right? And so in order to find those funds, it has to have census of the assets and it has to be able to tax them. And there are lots of different ways of doing that. So the civilian administration has a number of tax bureaus, records keeping, this sort of thing. Also a legal branch for resolving disputes with the imperial fisk.

Okay. But the civilian administration does a number of other things too. For example, the whole legal regulation of the empire, the courts, judges, courts of appeal, or also issuing laws, keeping records, all of this. And those are the main bureaus that have to do with the subjects of the empire.

The emperors obviously maintain bureaus that are for things like diplomacy, right? Or running the palace staff. Because the palace is a huge institution. And then there are a number of endowments — major churches, Sophia for example, has hundreds of staff, clergy to, you know, readers to whatever, and so they need to be paid, and so they need endowments. The imperial household, it has expenses, so it needs endowments, right? So there's a lot of land that's earmarked for these kinds of expenses that requires administration. So this multiplies, right? I mean, go on and on and on.

The Tax Code — Really a Code of Exemptions

Lex: And I'm sure the tax code is not a simple flat tax. It's probably incredibly complicated. So it's just like in the United States. It feels like majority of the United States is basically lawyers and the IRS.

Kaldellis: That's 90–98% I think is lawyers. I'm not sure — is scientific.

Lex: They shrunk the IRS down now to a size that it can't actually do anything.

Kaldellis: They shrunk it down to just 90%.

Lex: I don't know. I think there is something — this is another conversation about human nature and about the nature of governments — but there's something about — I think it's impossible to have an IRS-type organization that doesn't grow. I think maybe with good intentions, because you have to — like everybody always comes to you: "Well, I don't — here's my special case, I want my tax to be lower." And they kind of add another line: "All right, if you have purple hair, the tax decrease." And so you keep adding, and then of course the more you add, the more opaque the thing becomes, therefore corruption seeps in — or, you know, maybe not like official corruption but corruption light. And then the tax code grows, and then all of a sudden you have these big institutions that then encourage corruption, and then there's a bureaucracy and there's a momentum, and this is just how humans are.

Kaldellis: Yes. So this happens in East Roman society too. I'll give you some examples. So there's not a tax code as such. But the way it operates is through basically exemptions. So you petition for an exemption. You might be a monastery, you might be a wealthy land, you might be someone well-connected to the court, you might be a struggling provincial village. And you petition the emperor or some official and you say, "Hey, we know — we had bad season, or the barbarians came through and they took all of our — we can't pay the taxes. Could we have an exemption?"

And emperors often are granting these exemptions for very specific things — like maybe for this year or for that tax or whatever. The problem is that the emperors are discovering every once in a while that their officials are granting these exemptions sort of on their own. So every once in a while the emperors have to like clear house and they have to cancel all of these exemptions and start the whole process again. But later on, once we start to have documentation — like maybe in the 11th century — we see that every little (well, not every, but) we have all these monasteries, these little villages, these high officials, and they all have these documents that give them exemption from this or that or the other thing, and it gets so complicated. And that's how it works. But it is the engine of society that makes the whole thing work — cuz you have to fund — I mean there's infrastructure projects, but you have to fund the army, and the army is necessary to defend against all the military incursions.

Eunuchs: Parallel Tracks of Power

Lex: A good fraction of the bureaucracy seem to have been eunuchs — which are castrated men. Can you explain why this was a prominent thing?

Kaldellis: The eunuchs are part of the household staff of the emperor. So that would be like — what would it be? — the west wing of the White House or something like this. So they are the people who handle the imperial person, the bed chamber, the organization of palace life, things like banquets, you know, these kinds of things, the wardrobe.

And why are they important? So for starters, if you're physically with the emperor all day, right — like you're handling the meetings — people can come to you and say, "Hey, I need you to put in a good word for whatever." Right? So they can get things done. So you bribe them, they get things done. So they're very useful in this way. They're also kind of hated for that reason, right? But they're also kind of there to be hated. In other words, they soak up a lot of the frustration that people sometimes feel when they're not getting what they want from the court.

Okay, that's one thing. Here's the other thing that's more interesting. How do you as an emperor make sure that your bureaucracy doesn't displace you? Like if the bureaucracy is handling all the matters of running the empire, what do we need you for? Right? Like the bureaucracy can develop rules of its own for solving all the problems — for dealing with petitions and exemptions and whatever — like they can do it. So where's your power exactly?

So what the emperors did not want is to be displaced or overshadowed by these kinds of more impersonal institutions. And so they would often set up parallel tracks of power. So this could be a bit confusing.

So eunuchs were perfect for this reason, because often their origin was from outside the empire, or they were former slaves. They weren't connected to networks of powerful families. So you knew that they would generally be loyal to you because they depended entirely on you, the emperor, for their position and their power. And what you would sometimes do is you'd say, "Okay, I'm putting you in charge of like this army" — literally putting in charge of an army just so you can counterbalance the networks of established generals. And you'll have like, "No, a eunuch from my wardrobe, you know, I'm putting him in charge." And so this kind of shook things up a little bit so that people who were part of a system or a network could never fully count that that could get them all the power that they wanted. Now they have to answer to this eunuch from the wardrobe who's outside of their system.

And sometimes these eunuchs are extremely competent. Like Narses, Justinian's general who defeated the Goths in Italy in this massive battle. This guy was a total hard-ass. And this little old man — he was very old, he was tiny, and he was a eunuch. The Goths laughed at him, but he had the last laugh.

Lex: Is there some component to them being eunuchs — so they can't propagate the bloodline?

Kaldellis: Yeah. They can't have their own family, so they can't be like looking out for their own offspring. They're kind of isolated individuals who depend entirely on your favor.

Lex: Is it known whether the castration is almost always by force?

Kaldellis: There are eunuchs in society generally. They're often bishops. They're appointed patriarch of Constantinople. Sometimes they're in all kinds of positions. Many of them are children who are enslaved and castrated against their will. Sometimes, however, they are castrated very much according to the will of their families.

In some parts of — like in Caucasia — there were some regions that were famous for producing eunuchs. Also in Persia and so forth. But also we are pretty sure that this happened within the empire because there were some families that really wanted to place a eunuch at the court. So from Paphlagonia there were a number of — this is in northern Asia Minor — and so they would — it was almost like a family strategy.

Lex: I mean, it's fascinating that they had so much representation in the inner circles of the emperor and therefore had real influence.

Kaldellis: Yeah. But this was the case always, even in Rome — like in the Julio-Claudians under, you know, the first Caesars, there all these powerful eunuchs, and the senatorial class always hates them. I mean, it's just — it's the same dynamic.

There Are No Isolated Peasants

Lex: One of the interesting things you said as an aside in one of the many podcasts I listened with you is you said that there were no isolated people — you know, people that are disconnected from the empire, maybe who for example don't know about the taxes or just don't feel integrated into the system. I mean, that's really interesting to me — that there's so much connectivity, there's so much cohesion as the entire peoples.

Kaldellis: These people do not exist. These isolated peasants — this is a myth that I one day I'm going to write a chapter about this. Because it's this idea that you occasionally encounter, actually quite often, in the scholarship, that there are these rural communities out there somewhere that don't know what's going on in Constantinople. They might not even know who the emperor is. They're cut off. Maybe a tax official will show up every once in a while, maybe not. And they just live their lives on their own, unaffected by the state, because the medieval state we suspect is, you know, always powerless and can't affect people's lives.

There's no proof for these communities at all. At most, you might try to find some actual hermits — like actual Christian ascetic hermits — who try to go out and find this situation. And sometimes they're described in the text as like, "Oh, so-and-so went so far out into the mountains that not even the tax census people showed up there" — which tells you how they think about what it means to be isolated, right?

So this is a state in which every arable piece of arable land has been censused for centuries. It's not like you can hide a village, right? And the taxation process is not like they visit once a year, give us some coin and we'll leave. It's actually more like three times a year. And the taxes is not just coin and maybe payment in kind or something, but it's a whole bunch of things — like services and recruits and all. And once you start adding all of these things up, it creates a very dense institutional matrix in which all communities are enmeshed. They can't get out of it.

Also they have a church, don't they? And if they have a church, they have the calendar. And of course they have the calendar because — so like how do they organize time and space and boundaries and all of these things? No, this is all state instituted. How do they pay for anything? They have coins. Coins are everywhere. So I think that these institutions structured the way that everyone — even in village communities, even in the most isolated ones supposedly — how they thought about things like time and space and value and relations and power and kinship and so forth.

Lex: So what you're saying is: taxation is the thing that brings the empire together.

Kaldellis: It is, principally. Yes. I mean, it's true, right? This is how you know. But on top of that is layered things like the economy — coins, right? But also there's a church.

Lex: Yeah. And the church is important, just like you said.

Kaldellis: Yeah. And there's churches throughout the empire. Yes. And they have sermons and they pray for the emperor after this service. No, there are no isolated communities.

What Do We Even Call This Thing? The "Monarchical Republic"

Lex: So one of the things I briefly mentioned — it would be nice to elaborate on — is this term of monarchic republic. So we've been throwing around words like kingdom and republic and empire and monarchy. So what's a good applicable term to what this thing is — the East Roman Empire?

Kaldellis: That's an excellent question and I'm glad you flagged that, because we call it conventionally Byzantine Empire and it's neither of those things really. We talked about Byzantine.

Now, empire is a bit of a problem, because first of all, there's no term in Greek — in the Greek that they use at this period — that corresponds to our notion of empire. It's not part of the official terms. They didn't even have a term for it. You could have — there's some terms that might mean something like state, um, archē maybe, or — but for the most part it's either basileia, which we might translate as monarchy, or politeia, which is polity. And politeia in Greek — polity — is a Greek translation of Latin res publica.

Res publica doesn't mean republic the way we mean it. For the Romans, like, a non-monarchical regime, right? That's how we mean it — like the American republic, or, you know — that's a usage that came in later, like during the Enlightenment. In Latin, a res publica is the common affairs, the common interest, of a people who are constituted as a political community, regardless of the type of regime that they have.

Now, Cicero could imagine the Roman res publica governed by a king, or by a senate, or by a democracy — and they continue to use that term. We don't use it according to Roman norms. We use it according to modern norms — the term republic. So they continue to call it a res publica or a polity under the emperors as well, right? And it was understood that the role of the emperor — whether he's Caesar or basileus or whatever, whatever language — was to serve the polity. They keep saying this. So the terms that occur in the sources most commonly are basileia of the Romans, polity of the Romans, or Romania — which is the proper name of this state, not empire.

Is It Really an "Empire"?

Now, empire — in modern English, commonly an empire is a state that's created when one group conquers a bunch of others and rules them. It could be an ethnic group, it could be an ethnoreligious group, it could be a political group, whatever. But there's an act of conquest, and it results in a state where one group is ruling over others.

That's not how Romania is actually constituted. We've talked about this. It's for the most part all Roman citizens ruling themselves — like Roman subjects ruling themselves through their own government. So it's not an empire, strictly speaking.

Now occasionally they will conquer other people — like we talked about the warfare, sometimes it's not just defensive, sometimes it's offensive, right? In that case, you do establish an imperial relationship, like: we, the Romans, are ruling over this other group. Could be Slavs, could be Bulgarians for a while, could be whatever. Or even some Muslims in northern Syria for a while in the 11th century, who are not Romans. And that's an imperial relationship.

The question is: how many of these imperial relationships do you need to decide that, well, we've reached a tipping point and now we're just going to call the whole thing an empire? Personally, I don't think we ever reach that point. I mean, all states even today have minorities. Some of them are part of that state because they were conquered once upon a time, but we don't call those states empires necessarily. Even though — like look at the United Kingdom. Well, Wales was conquered and, you know, the Welsh have their own identity, their own language. I don't know that you would call that now an imperial relationship. Maybe. I don't know.

Lex: I think there's also a synonymous use of the word empire that refers to the power that the state has in the global context, geopolitically. So sometimes empire is used synonymously with superpower, meaning it's one of the primary nations in the world, and it doesn't necessarily have to do with the imperial nature of its history. Hence the sort of "American empire," "US empire."

Kaldellis: That's true. Though you would never characterize the American political regime as an imperial one, insofar as it does not have an emperor, for example. But anyway — yes, the problem is that in my field, we use the term empire conventionally even when what you just described is clearly not happening. Like even toward the end, there's not a major world power. It has a few islands and the Peloponnese, but we call it empire and the emperor and it's completely ridiculous. There's not even a term in their language for that sort of thing. Anyway, we can call things whatever we want, just so we understand what the reality is and what we are using those terms to mean. I think that's the important thing.

Why "Monarchical Republic" Fits

Lex: So then let's look at words which are both fascinating. Monarchic republic, right? Why is that a good term for the East Roman Empire?

Kaldellis: Because they understood it very much to be a polity — in the Roman sense of a res publica — that was governed by a regime that was monarchical. So those two elements: there's an emperor at the top, and it functioned politically like a republic. Also, the emperor was expected to be working for the republic — like they keep saying this. So this was their conception.

You don't find it, for example, in other kinds of dynastic empires — the ones that don't emerge from within the context of an ancient republic. So this is another reason why I find this civilization so fascinating. I mean, we mentioned the Greek and the Roman and the Christian elements that are kind of jostling together. This is fascinating to me in part because it's this weird combination of monarchical power — like in terms of executive institutions — and a republican kind of baseline ideology on which it operates.

Lex: Yeah. And they sometimes come to a head.

Was It a Military Dictatorship?

Lex: Is there at all a degree to which it's a military dictatorship? Because especially when it's a general in the emperor's seat — yes, there's a republic, yes, there's political discussion, but the army has a lot of power. So to what degree is it a military dictatorship, as is sometimes talked about when you talk about the Roman Empire?

Kaldellis: Yeah. So Augustus' position, for example, and that of his successors, is talked about as a military dictatorship. In other words, when push comes to shove and the basis of political power is the army, then I suppose it's legitimate for historians to talk about a military dictatorship.

But here's why I don't think the Eastern Roman Empire is a military dictatorship. Even though again, the emperors control the armies, and the armies themselves will often have a say in who becomes emperor — like through the civil wars that we mentioned. Okay.

The reason why I don't think it's a military dictatorship is because they almost never — very, very, very rarely — use the army as an instrument of social control. Isn't that strange to you? Because you see that being done through so much of human history.

Lex: Yeah. And the fact they don't resort to that…

Kaldellis: I mean, for a thousand years, that's just not a thing — as a way of keeping down the population. You just don't see it. And part of the reason for that is I don't think the population needs to be kept down. Like, this gets back to the consensus that we were talking about. The understanding is that the army is there to protect the Roman people, and is often recruited among them. And for most of the period that I study, it lives among them. So when the emperors have to call up the army, they're literally calling up soldiers from local villages.

Lex: It's just incredible that this worked. I guess the civil war thing, as a mechanism, is effective, cuz — you just, throughout human history, I mean, you look at even recent history — it's just a very common thing for authoritarian figure to use the army to suppress people.

Kaldellis: Yeah, but isn't that dangerous, though? Look at how many modern regimes tried to use their own armies against their people who were in a moment of rising up.

Lex: Well, it's a risk. Like — the Arab Spring.

Kaldellis: Especially in Egypt. And you could tell for those three weeks that Mubarak was trying to decide whether he could actually count on the army to suppress the protesters or not. Because you never know what they're going to do if you ask them to fire on a civilian population. It can backfire on you badly. So I think that is a very dangerous force to use, and even in the context of modern regimes can result in a regime change.

Lex: Absolutely. And there's huge wisdom behind that. But you know, leaders are not always wise — often not wise — and often maybe they start out wise, but power corrupts them over time. And so the fact that this does not happen just over and over and over and over on the East Roman Empire — there's just signal there that's fascinating. And I mean, this goes to the book that you want to be working on about why the thing lasts.

Kaldellis: So there's one emperor who does do that, and that's Justinian.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、税收不是"取走"，而是"写入"——一种被误读的国家操作系统
大多数人把税收理解为国家从社会**抽取**资源的行为。但 Kaldellis 在这里描绘的东罗马税制其实颠覆了这个直觉：一年三次上门、既收钱、又收实物、还征劳役和兵源的税吏，本质上并不是"收割者"，而是**帝国的写入头**——把国家的时间感（日历）、空间感（地籍）、价值感（货币）和身份感（罗马公民）一遍遍地刻进每一个村庄的意识里。
有一个非常反直觉的观察值得停下来品味：Kaldellis 说，"隔绝的农民"这种浪漫化的中世纪意象，在东罗马史料中**根本找不到证据**——甚至连隐修的圣人也是用"连人口普查员都到不了那里"来定义"深山"的。也就是说，这个帝国的偏远程度不是用距离度量的，**而是用税务能否触达来度量的**。这与我们通常想象的"中世纪松散、封建、割裂"完全相反。真相是：一个每四个月就派人下乡一次的国家，事实上比多数现代宗教都更频繁地"临在"于臣民生活之中。
## 二、宦官：一种解决"委托–代理问题"的生物学方案
宦官为什么长期在东罗马权力中枢扎堆？主流解释常常停在"防止君主戴绿帽"或"忠诚可靠"这类道

item.title: [Lex Clips] Murder of Roman Emperors - The crisis that almost destroyed the Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

帝国崩溃学的悖论：稳定与危机是同一枚硬币

大多数人读到"50年26位皇帝被杀"，第一反应是"帝国要完了"。但Kaldellis点破了一个让人脊背发凉的反转——这场危机恰恰是罗马"太成功"的副产品。当卡拉卡拉把公民权给了所有行省人，帝国的"股东"就从一小撮意大利精英扩张成了几十个行省军团。所有人都想当CEO——这不是分裂主义，而是过度整合。所谓"高卢帝国"从未想过独立，他们只是想用罗马模式统治整个罗马。这是政治想象力的彻底垄断：当一个系统连它的叛徒都只会重复它自己时，这个系统其实赢麻了。

埃及莎草纸与"皇帝死亡新闻"的时差——一个媒介考古学的启示

这里藏着一个跨领域的连接，值得当代人深思：顶层的极端动荡与基层的日常生活可以近乎完全脱耦。埃及莎草纸文书显示，当罗马街头正在处决第20位皇帝时，尼罗河边的农民该收租收租、该纳税纳税。这几乎是麦克卢汉"媒介即讯息"的前身——信息传播的带宽决定了危机的心理半径。今天我们把华尔街的一次盘中波动当成"末日"来体验，是因为算法把顶层噪音直接注射进了每个人的神经系统。3世纪的罗马人反而享有一种"信息带宽保护"，让他们在客观的政治地震中过着主观的平静生活。信息越通畅，社会未必越稳定，反而可能把局部危机放大成全民焦虑。

戴克里先的"深层国家"辩驳：任期长度如何塑造官僚文化

Lex Fridman提出"戴克里先是不是发明了深层国家"，Kaldellis的反驳非常克制，但你抛出的那个观察其实点破了一个跨越两千年的政治物理学定律：深层国家的形成，是任期不确定性的自然产物。民主制中领导人是"临时工"，官僚就必然把自己组织成"永久层"来对冲不连续性；而皇帝制下，官僚会嵌入皇帝，而非对抗皇帝。

这里还藏着一个更出人意料的连接——硅谷的创始人独裁模式（founder mode）与罗马帝制的行政哲学惊人相似。当CEO任期无限时，组织的中层不会形成对抗性的"深层公司"；当CEO频繁更换（比如受VC操控的初创企业），中层就会演化成阻碍性的官僚层。制度稳定性不是靠制衡塑造的，而是靠"任期时间长度"塑造的。

隐藏的系统性真相：伊利里亚人不是"救世英雄"，而是"下沉市场的兵源套利"

3世纪一连串把帝国从悬崖边拉回的皇帝，全部来自苏格兰大小的南斯拉夫地区。表面看是"军事天才辈出"，实际是经济结构性套利——伊利里亚经济落后，从军是最优出路，于是最强的年轻人被虹吸进军团，再从军团虹吸进最高权力。这是贫困地区通过垄断某个上升通道，反向殖民帝国核心的经典案例。类比在历史上反复出现：奥斯曼帝国的巴尔干新军、大英帝国的苏格兰军官团、美国军队里的南方白人和拉美裔——帝国的顶层总是被它最贫困的边缘所填补，因为核心地区的人已经"买不起"军旅生涯的机会成本了。

一个被忽略的终极观察：税收是文明的操作系统内核

对话最后收束在一个几乎让人意外的地方——所有政治剧变、内战、改革，最终都指向税收。戴克里先真正的革命不是四帝共治，而是给意大利征税。这是罗马历史上第一次"征服者向自己征税"，意味着"我们"和"他们"的边界从此消失。从税制角度看，所谓罗马帝国的"衰亡"其实从未发生——因为拜占庭延续了这套税收操作系统直到1204年。真正终结罗马的不是蛮族，是十字军摧毁了它的税基。这才是Kaldellis作为拜占庭史专家真正想让你听懂的话：帝国不死于剑，死于账本。

（见上方 insights 区块）

📝 转录文本

引子：从公民权敕令到3世纪危机

Lex： You said that one of the important aspects about this edict is that giving equal rights to provincials was one of the things that helped deal with the crisis of the 3rd century, and was the thing that — I think you said — {quote} sapped discontent from the relevant populations, which then minimized the probability of uprisings.

Kaldellis： Well, now, that's a very long-term process.

Lex： Yeah.

Kaldellis： Now, the 3rd century is a chaos, there's a crisis, it's a mess, right? So [clears throat] what follows the edict is quite a few decades of unrest.

3世纪危机的规模

Lex： Well, okay, let's put it in mildly. Let's talk about the crisis of the 3rd century, where 26 emperors were murdered in a period of 50 years.

Kaldellis： Yes, it's tremendous instability.

Lex： So there's civil wars nonstop. Almost all of these emperors were generals, and almost all were murdered by their men. So civil war was the norm, hyperinflation, economic crash, plus there's a plague —

Kaldellis： Yes.

Lex： — from 249 to 262 AD, which is a pandemic, possibly smallpox or Ebola. It killed up to 5,000 people a day in Rome alone, devastating the tax base and army recruitment pool. And the empire fractured into three pieces, but then emperor Aurelian miraculously reconquered them in a period of 5 years. So there's a lot of chaos. It deserves the name "the crisis of the 3rd century" —

Kaldellis： A perfect storm. Yeah. I mean, you have a crisis of imperial legitimacy. Basically, the army's proclaiming emperors left and right.
