item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] How Nuclear Will Unlock Energy Abundance with Valar Atomics Founder Isaiah Taylor
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、真正的悖论：核能不是"物理问题"，而是"心理问题"——一个被自己吓死的行业

最出人意料的观察是：这家公司做的事情，从物理上看毫无新意——高温气冷堆（HTGR）、三代裂变燃料（TRISO）、石墨慢化——每一项技术在 1960 年代都已经存在。Isaiah Taylor 真正的"发明"，是拒绝把核工业当作核工业来经营。

请注意他反复强调的那个隐蔽指控："大部分核能公司其实是建模与仿真公司"。这是一句杀人不见血的话——它意味着过去 40 年里，整个行业把自己伪装成工程组织，实际上运行的是学术论文生产流水线。他们的产品不是反应堆，而是关于反应堆的确定性叙事。三里岛之后，行业不是变得更安全，而是变得更擅长"证明自己是安全的"——两者是完全不同的技能树。

这暗合了 Nassim Taleb 的一个观察：当一个系统被要求"绝不能出错"时，它会停止学习，因为学习需要错误。核工业过去 50 年就是这样一个"反脆弱性归零"的系统。Valor 真正的赌注不是技术赌注，而是一个哲学赌注：用"降低后果"替代"降低概率"——这一句话背后，是从贝叶斯世界观切换到蒙特卡洛世界观的整个认识论转向。

二、跨领域连接：核反应堆、SpaceX 与 Toyota Camry 是同一台机器

Isaiah 说了一句意味深长的话："我们不想做兰博基尼，我们想做丰田凯美瑞。"这句话看似谦逊，实则暗藏一个非常深的制造业哲学：

  • 航天业（马斯克证明的）：迭代硬件 > 完美设计
  • 汽车业（丰田证明的）：可制造性 > 峰值性能
  • 半导体业（台积电证明的）：良率曲线 > 单片天才
  • 核能业（Valor 正在证明的）：Tick Rate（点击率）> 论文引用率

而这里出现了一个真正让我意外的隐藏结构："Tick Rate"这个词借自电子游戏——服务器每秒更新游戏状态的次数。Isaiah 把核工业的成败指标定义为"两次原子分裂之间的时间"，这是一种把工业进程当作实时系统状态机来管理的思维。

这意味着 Valor 本质上不是在建反应堆，它在建一台"元反应堆"——一个把反应堆当作输出的组织机器。真正被复利放大的不是核裂变的能量，而是学习的速度。他们的第一次到第二次临界间隔 7 个月，目标是压缩到几分钟——这就是"摩尔定律"在物理硬件上的重演。

三、最深的隐藏真相：能源不是商品，能源是"其他所有商品的第一因"

访谈末尾那段关于"铝曾是贵金属"的例子，藏着一个绝大多数听众会滑过的存在论洞察：

Isaiah 说："当我们搞定 AI 和机器人后，能源将成为所有事物的成本。" 让我把这句话推到它逻辑的尽头——

如果人类劳动可以被机器人+能源替代，如果原材料的开采可以被机器人+能源替代，如果机器人本身也可以被机器人+能源制造——那么整个经济体正在坍缩为一个单变量方程：一切价格 = f(能源价格)。

这在跨领域上形成了一个惊人的呼应：这与热力学第二定律直接对应。宇宙中唯一真正稀缺的、不可逆的资源是低熵能量（自由能）。Isaiah 说"能源是宇宙中唯一稀缺的东西"，这不是浪漫修辞——这是薛定谔在《生命是什么》里的原话的商业化版本。生命本身就是一台"以能量对抗熵增"的机器。文明只是这台机器的社会化外延。

那么真正的洞见浮现了：Valor Atomics 的商业模式，从物理学上讲，是在做"文明的负熵基础设施"。当能源足够便宜时，"制造"这个动词会消失——因为把一堆沙子重新组织成一台手机，本质上只是能量的付出。这就是为什么 Isaiah 说 "everything becoming free"——他不是在说共产主义，他是在说当能源成本趋近于零，所有物质产品的边际成本也趋近于零。

啊哈时刻：AI 芯片跑在核反应堆上，nuclearbite.com 这个网站只在反应堆运行时存在——这个演示看似是营销噱头，实际上是人类文明未来形态的微缩预演：信息（AI）与能量（核）直接耦合，中间的所有工业中介层被压缩到零。这个网页存在的每一毫秒，都由 10¹⁷ 个铀原子的裂变直接支撑。这是赛博朋克与实体经济的一次量子纠缠。

📝 转录文本

开场：一次历史性的演示

Welcome to War 250. First advanced reactor to ever make power by a startup.

We did something pretty awesome today. The first ever AI chip powered by a nuclear reactor.

First TRISO reactor to turn on in over 50 years in the United States.

How much of the premise of Valor is possible now comes from increasing demands for power in the United States largely driven by AI compute. Energy being a commodity, the demand is set by the price. If you can figure out how to make energy cheaper, you will have demand.

When SpaceX started going, people were like, there's no way they're going to hit the numbers that they're hitting today.

Most of the nuclear industry is a modeling and simulation industry. They're not focused on hardware iteration, hardware execution, building the simplest and safest reactor that allows them to scale. And Valor just demonstrated that we are.

主持人开场

Hi listeners, welcome back to No Priors. Today I'm here with Isaiah Taylor, the founder and CEO of Valor Atomics. We're here in their Utah nuclear facility where we're going to do a tour, talk about why speed and scale are the only path to making nuclear reactors in the United States again.

Um, why energy is the only input to our hyper-techno-industrial future and why there's an open regulatory environment for progress in energy R&D for the first time in years.

Welcome, Isaiah. Thanks so much for doing this.

Of course. Welcome to Valor Atomics at San Rafael. Thanks for coming.

Valor 在做什么？

So what are you doing here at Valor?

We are making nuclear reactors built for planetary scale. So nuclear fission is kind of an old technology. It's around the 1940s that we really started working on it. Um, and we've built a lot of nuclear reactors, but we've never built them for mass scale. Nuclear's never had its Ford moment or its Tesla moment, if you want to put it that way. And that's what Valor Atomics is working on doing.

We have nuclear reactors that are more manufactured than constructed, and uh they're extremely safe, and we think the combination of these two things is going to allow us to scale nuclear fission and make energy 10 times cheaper for humanity. So that's what we're working on.

为什么是 Isaiah？个人起点

We're going to dig into why you think this is possible and why you're making it happen after decades of like no real activity in this space. But first for you personally — you don't have the you know credentialed decades of nuclear research background that is often required to work in the space — like why you and what triggered it?

I have been uh reluctant to start this company for a long time, um, and eventually did out of sheer frustration that uh nobody else was working on it with the pace required and understanding the scale that would be required.

Um, and uh, you know, I I think any problem that uh you want to work on that's as hard as this — you have to really really believe in it. And after a certain amount of time, I just realized that I was never going to stop thinking about this. And so I I might as well build it.

It started for me — my great-grandfather was a nuclear physicist on the Manhattan Project. Um, my great-grandfather and great-grandmother were both in the secret city um during uh World War II. And I just grew up thinking about nuclear as this incredible technology.

I've always wanted to build thousands of machines. Um, I didn't know which ones I was going to build. It was just very clear to me since I was like six that I was supposed to build thousands of machines.

Um, and at some point um I went from thinking about nuclear as like a totally solved problem, right, which was my first um you know outside view of the industry. It was like, okay, people know how to build reactors. They're doing fine at it. To suddenly realizing that this was an entirely unsolved problem. Um, that we we sort of thought we solved in the '60s and there was like a good run into the 70s and then we suddenly stopped.

So I came to that realization. It was uh very disorienting for me actually. I I suddenly realized — it actually remember the afternoon. I was like, wait a minute, did we actually stop building reactors in the 70s? Like who — why did nobody tell me that?

Um, and really since that moment I had been like obsessively trying to understand why. Um, and I came to some conclusions about why uh when I was in high school, and I still hold those conclusions today.

And then it was a, you know, 10-year journey of like waiting for someone to solve the problem, watching every nuclear startup that popped up, trying to figure out if they were going to solve the problem. And eventually realizing that no one was going to solve it at the pace and scale that was necessary. Uh, and so I started Valor.

为什么美国停止建反应堆？

So for people who come from outside the nuclear industry, like why did we stop building reactors in this country?

Yeah, we stopped for a pretty simple reason, which is Three Mile Island. So, we were building a lot of reactors. We were doing really well. Um, we had a nuclear incident in the Three Mile Island reactor. And in that incident, we essentially lost the ability to uh cool the reactor.

And in traditional uh light-water reactors, you have to always be able to cool them, right? So, if you shut a reactor down in traditional nuclear, you have to keep cooling it even after shutdown. And in this case, the cooling system failed and so you had core meltdown.

Now, nobody died, right? Uh, in fact, not only did nobody die, nobody was injured and there was no radiation dose to the public. But, uh, it was sort of a optics and PR situation um that was mismanaged in many many different ways and the public uh became fearful and lost interest in nuclear.

Now I think that actually turned around even like 15–20 years ago where suddenly uh people looked back on that and they said, okay, actually we do know how to build reactors really safe — even learnings from that uh we've taken and incorporated into new designs.

But then there was a second problem, which was: once you stop an industry it's very hard to start it again. And in the intervening time between Three Mile Island and when people gained interest in nuclear again, we also really changed how we build things in the United States.

We went from a country that was very good at large-scale civil infrastructure, right? Bridges, roads, um huge power plants, dams, highway systems, right? We were really good at large-scale civil infrastructure. Um, and we kind of atrophied at that skill set and we got much better at advanced manufacturing.

And so my realization — one of the first realizations I had about this — was: if you're going to reboot nuclear and you actually believe in the atomic energy century, which I absolutely do, it's probably not going to look like what it did in the '60s. It's not going to look like civil infrastructure because we're not that good at that anymore. And what we're way better at is producing, you know, more complicated and uh more functional pieces of manufactured equipment.

And so you're going to shift from the civil infrastructure way of building to a manufacturing way of building. And I think even though some people did realize that — and this is generally what you'd call SMRs, right, small modular reactors — what was missing is that people did not realize that you're going to have to pursue that model through hardware iteration.

And that's really where Valor is unique — that yes, we believe in SMRs, we believe in reactors that are manufactured, but we also know that in order to get there, you have to do it through hardware iteration. You have to turn some plants on. You have to turn on a 100-kilowatt reactor like the one behind us. You have to go cold critical like we did in Project Nova. And then you have to take the next steps after that. And it's going to be driven by hardware iteration. And I think that's what nobody else realized before us.

监管路径：一个被遗忘的法律通道

So that's a whole company philosophy for you, but it has to interact with a regulatory environment where I think the assumed status quo is: you can't iterate because you can't turn anything on because it's going to take you 15 years to go through a permitting process and several billion dollars to get a no or try again. So why isn't that true?

There's this chicken and egg that existed for nuclear in the last 20 years where you need data in order to go to the regulator, but in order to go to the regulator with data, you have to have run a plant, right?

And so there's this like, okay, how do you get the empirical data? And the way that people tried to solve that was with modeling and simulation, right? So if you look at the nuclear industry outside of Valor, it's mostly a modeling and simulation uh industry. Like when you think of a nuclear company, right, there's like nuclear companies out there that everyone knows the name of. And you kind of look under the cover — it's actually a modeling and simulation company, right? They produce um very very precise what we call paper reactors, which have really good predictions of how a theoretical thing might behave.

And that was sort of the hack to try to get around this regulatory chicken and egg.

But the alternative is that you actually just do what Congress originally intended. Um, if you look back at the early days of nuclear legislation um there were actually two pathways for nuclear in the United States. There was the um testing pathway and the commercial pathway.

So the commercial pathway everyone knows is called the NRC — the Nuclear Regulatory Commission. The NRC is solving for large-scale commercial deployment of mature systems. So yeah, if you try to do the iteration under the regulator that's concerned with large-scale commercial deployment of mature systems, that's going to be tough, right? You're going to be asked commercial deployment questions when you're just trying to figure out how to bend some steel and get some atoms splitting.

Um, but there's another pathway and that pathway is the Department of Energy.

So, most people don't know this, but the Department of Energy was originally called the ERDA — the Energy Research and Development Agency. And people don't know that because it was only called the ERDA for about 2 years. There was a little 2-year period where uh it was renamed shortly after, but its origin is actually a spin-out of the Atomic Energy Commission.

So where the DOE comes from originally is Congress taking the Atomic Energy Commission, which was one agency responsible for both commercial deployment and for testing. And they said, "Actually, let's split these out. Let's make the NRC in charge of commercial deployment. Let's make the ERDA in charge of testing, and we'll have these just two separate agencies."

And then two years later, uh Congress said, "Actually, we're just going to call the ERDA the Department of Energy," right? The DOE. But the origin of this agency is testing nuclear reactors. That's what it's for.

Um, and everyone sort of forgot this. Um, it's just been sitting there in legislation for like 40 years and no one's really taken advantage of it except for one government program here or there where they want to, you know, do a NASA space reactor or something like that.

But it has from a legal perspective been waiting for an administration to say, "Hey, this might be a way to accelerate nuclear R&D." Uh, and that's exactly what the Trump administration has done.

So, the reactor that's uh behind us here um was turned on under an executive order. It was EO 14301, which called for three advanced reactors to go critical on American soil by July 4th. Um, and we built it under Department of Energy uh authority under that executive order.

So, I mean, this is an incredibly exciting time in nuclear. This is really breaking that chicken and egg, right? It's the first time where we've been able to shortcut that, you know, problem of how do you get data — and uh we have data now. We're currently, as we're sitting here, making 100 kW uh splitting something like 10^17 atoms per second uh in this reactor behind us.

控制室

So, this is the control room. There's two sections. This is the uh control half. This door's closed. We have to have senior reactor operator's permission to enter here, which we just got. Thank you, Brent. And uh out here is sort of our uh observation room.

It looks like the control room in Hawthorne.

It is the exact control room in Hawthorne. We literally picked this up, put it on an airplane, and flew it here. So the entirety of the plant, including a control room, flew with us on the C-17s. Uh, first time that's ever been done, which is pretty cool.

How many people um operate?

We got one operator at the controls who's the reactor operator who's controlling all this. And then you got a senior reactor operator who provides oversight and kind of ensures that we're staying within our safety limits and stuff. But we always have to have one person that controls maintaining safety, maintaining the reactor.

计划中的 SCRAM 演示：从"低概率"到"低后果"

So, in a couple of days here, we're going to do a scram. So, we're going to press that big red button right there. Uh, big red button scrams the reactor. So, basically drops the rods, right? Dropping the control rods will introduce a lot of boron carbide into the core. When we scram that, rods will drop. Boron carbide enters the core. Boron is a strong neutron absorber and so there will not be enough neutrons to maintain criticality because the neutron population gets absorbed into the rods. So that'll cause the reaction to stop.

Now, in a nuclear reactor, this is getting to the core of like what is Valor doing differently? Why do we really believe that the technology that we have here is going to scale?

Traditionally, um, when you scram the reactor in a light-water reactor, your work is nowhere near done yet, right? The next really day of operations is incredibly important because when you turn the reactor off in a traditional nuclear reactor um you still have a lot of heat produced. And you have a lot of heat produced because in a nuclear core you still have about 5% uh heat production from recently split atoms.

So when a reactor is running you have a bunch of atoms being split. They're producing a ton of energy. And even after the reaction shuts down, you have recently split atoms that are continuing down their decay chains. So, this is called decay heat. Um, and decay heat is about 5 to 6% of what your continuous power was before you shut down.

And in a traditional reactor, the reactor would melt down if you didn't continue to run the cooling pumps after turning the reactor off. So, there's no more fission occurring. You don't have a chain reaction anymore, but you do have this decay heat. And if you were just to let it sit there, that heat would build up and you would have meltdown. That's exactly what happened in Fukushima and Three Mile Island.

Um, and so the way that they deal with this in traditional plants is they have very very carefully built cooling systems that they continue to run for about 24 hours after shutdown. Um, and they try to build in all these redundancies and make sure that in any circumstance the cooling systems never ever ever fail. Um, and that works pretty well. And nuclear is the safest form of energy ever made.

Um, even with sort of that caveat — Three Mile Island, you know, even though it melted down, nobody died, right? Fukushima, uh, arguably maybe one person died. It's sort of a debate among scientists whether that person died from radiation or not.

Um, so even as traditional nuclear is, it's very safe. But we want to do even better. And we want to do better because we are interested in scale. We want to make thousands of reactors, tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands. And when you're doing that, you really just want it to never ever melt down for any reason, right?

And so the best way to do that is actually just to make active cooling systems unnecessary altogether. And that's actually what we're going to demonstrate on Friday. We're going to scram this reactor. Immediately after we scram, we're actually going to turn off the entire electrical supply to the plant. We're going to turn off the circulator. We're going to turn off the RCCS pump. Every safety system in this plant, we're going to shut off and we're going to watch what happens.

Now, we have a cheat code. We know what's going to happen uh because we did this in Hawthorne before we had uh neutrons in it. Uh we ran the plant at full power uh with electrical simulators. So, we basically have these electrical resistors in the plant in Hawthorne. We put about 15 city blocks worth of LA power into this and got it up to full nuclear temperatures, pressures.

Right. I remember seeing the sim.

Yeah. Uh, and so we held it there and then we did exactly what we're about to do, which is we turned off all safety systems at once and we watched the plant for 2 days.

Um, and exactly what we had hoped uh happened — which is that the uh RCCS panels, which are these water jackets around the core, um went into passive circulation mode, meaning the water boils, the steam leaves, it condenses, that removes heat and you have natural circulation with no moving parts.

Um, so zero input from the operators, no moving parts, no electrical control. It's just the geometry of that system that slowly just removes that decay heat over a period of two days.

So we're about to demonstrate that uh right here starting on Friday after 72 hours of at full power. So that'll be really exciting demonstration and it's really the basis of scale, right? If we're going to do this uh many times, we want to make sure that just from the basic physics this reactor doesn't melt down. Not because of operator control, not because of really good engineering, but the physics of the plant make it safe from meltdown.

安全的哲学：赔率 vs. 后果

One of the huge reasons for um the slowdown in investment in real R&D in this space has been concerns about the safety profile. You and your team are you know very respectful of but not particularly concerned that this is an insurmountable problem. What do people outside nuclear misunderstand about safety of these SMRs and your design in particular?

There's two big misunderstandings.

One is that people, you know, generally perceive nuclear as like stable, cheap base load but kind of dangerous with downsides. I think that's just a complete misperception. Nuclear energy is the safest form of energy empirically. If you look at the amount of power generated versus the amount of deaths, um, it's even safer than solar — which is very strange because how does someone die from solar? Well, actually most solar is installed on roofs and people sometimes die falling off of a roof installing solar. And if you add those deaths up, it is actually a higher death per energy than the totality of nuclear energy in the world.

Um, so that's actually kind of crazy. Nuclear as it is today is already the safest form of energy.

Now the only problem with existing nuclear is that — I think the existing philosophy, again, it's the safest in the world, but we can do better. And where we'd like to improve is: the existing philosophy says okay, there's — if you look at risk, it's actually composed of two different things, right? It's composed of the odds of the thing happening and the consequence if it does happen.

And traditional nuclear has focused on risk reduction by reducing the odds. Right? So they say, okay, yeah, a meltdown could have some bad consequence. So let's make sure that it never ever ever happens. And all of the effort into risk reduction goes into low odds of anything ever happening.

The alternative way that you can reduce risk is actually just reducing consequence, right? And we would argue that that's a much better way to reduce risk. Odds are stochastic, right? Even if you do an enormous amount of risk reduction on odds and you do all this engineering, something's still going to happen, right? Like just something's going to come out of left field and you can't predict everything.

Um, and so advanced reactors tend to focus on consequence more than odds. Now, we still don't — you know, we still try to protect against things like — um, you know, we have site security here for example, right? So someone can't come and compromise the control room. But from an engineering perspective we also say, well, what if someone did compromise the control room? How do we still make sure that it's perfectly safe?

Um, and so advanced reactors really focus on that second thing. Um, our safety basis when we go to the regulator is: everything in the plant has failed. Absolutely everything. And we know what happens.

Yeah. In fact, it is safe. Right. So we have regulatory limits where we say, okay, in an accident scenario you can't dose the public with radiation. Right? That's really ultimately what a nuclear regulator is trying to prevent. You can't dose public and workers with radiation.

And so our safety basis says, "All right, let's look at the reactor design and pretend that everything in it has failed. Are we dosing the workers and the public with radiation?" And the answer is no.

Um, and that's super super important because even though we do still try to reduce the odds — we have site security, we make sure that our operators are well trained — we start with the worst case possible scenario where everything has failed and we say, "How do we make that safe?" And that's really a physics problem. It's really about the geometry of the core. It's about the materials that you use. TRISO is a huge part of that. Um, and that's how we pursue safety.

So, I think that people miss that (a) nuclear is already very safe, but (b) the new breed of nuclear is much more focused on just making sure that these plants are intrinsically and inherently safe regardless of odds, right? Regardless of what crazy thing comes out of left field. Um, and that's a much better way to scale.

可靠性问题的主要来源

What are the major causes of um like reliability issues for a reactor of this design?

Let's see. Helium circulator is one area that people have had trouble with in the past. Um, the heat exchanger between the main helium loop and the secondary power conversion loop is something that Fort St. Vrain has had issues with. Uh, I don't know Jess, what do you think?

Moisture as well. Fort Vrain had problems with moisture. So graphite is hydrophilic, meaning that it loves to take moisture — even in the hot desert air, hot dry desert air, it still sucks up moisture.

Um, so we have mitigated that risk by having the helium purification system, and part of that is taking the moisture and trying to condense that moist air out of the system and then put it into some supply tanks — or excuse me, some storage tanks — outside of that.

We know it's a known fact that for several hundred hours of operation, we'll be slowly leeching that moisture out of the graphite blocks as we keep them to maintain the temperature. So, as you know, it's graphite ceramic on the block on the way in. Um, so it's kind of difficult to migrate that moisture from the center of the block all the way out. That's why it takes so long is just keeping up to temperature and it slowly migrates its way out into blocks.

But if you don't capture that moisture and remove them from the system, then you can imagine any carbon steel which is in the system will be in the rust. Then the rust particles will migrate and either cause a shortage in the motor or a blockage in the system somewhere. Um, so anyway, that's one of the lessons learned from Fort Vrain — need to.

I will say though, helium graphite systems have a lot of natural advantages. Um, and yeah, there's basically no chemistry going on other than the bit of moisture that we need to pull out of the graphite blocks. Helium is inert, right? And unlike a water-based reactor where you have — you're very very carefully trying to make sure you have dry steam if it's dry steam plant — and wet steam is going to start just destroying all of your thermal machinery and everything.

Um, helium is a very very inert simple chemical uh to work as a working fluid. Now there are downsides to it. It's not very dense. You have to have more pumping power, these sorts of things. Um, but the fundamentals are in our favor in terms of building a very simple plant.

SMR 全景：硬件执行派 vs. 设计派

When we think about you know an abundance of nuclear power, I think a lot of interested listeners will have heard of a number of SMR startups and people building larger scale reactors. How do you break out the different use cases and the different designs here? Are people aiming at different power levels and efficiencies? Is it just a completely different philosophy of company? How do you look at that landscape?

Yeah, I would make one big division in nuclear companies, and it is: people who believe that nuclear is essentially a hardware execution problem, and people who believe that it's a design problem.

Um, Valor is solidly in the camp that we believe this is a hardware and execution problem. This is really an issue of: can you build reactors, can you turn them on? Can you get them running? Can you operate them well? Can you produce them at scale? Can you go from making one to 10 to 100 to a thousand? Um, that is the problem of nuclear.

Um, the second class of nuclear companies, I think, are mistaken — think that this is a problem of like making the most beautiful design, making the most sophisticated design. The design will have you know the most perfect efficiency. Um, you know, you look inside some of these design documents and there's so much complexity and you know the materials that go into it are super rare and you have to set up entire supply chains that don't exist just to get the thing running.

And I look at that and I say, okay, maybe over time the reactor grows in complexity and um you start to use some more rare materials to try to get higher and higher performance. But the problem of nuclear today is like the Toyota Camry problem, right? Like we don't want to make Lamborghinis. We want to make a very simple, very cheap, very safe reactor that we can make literally tens of thousands of.

Um, and actually that is going to make the cheapest energy in the world, right? Um, you maybe will get a little bit better performance out of a more complicated reactor, but I'm going to beat you on cost because I'm making a thousand of them, right? And that's really what we care about.

Valor is in the business of making energy 10 times cheaper for humanity. Um, and we want to continue doing that forever. There will always be ways to make energy cheaper and cheaper. Um, and that's very much our goal.

And I think the big difference in philosophy is: do you tackle that through building reactors and through iteration, or do you tackle that through design? And we believe it's entirely a hardware problem.

Tick Rate：借自电子游戏的进度指标

And so the company is less than 3 years old — just about to turn three.

Yep.

Where in the timeline of you know hundreds of reactors are we?

Yeah, we just turned on number one. So we went critical back in November. That was our first criticality. Um, that was what you'd call a cold criticality or critical pile. Um, and just a few days ago we made power for the first time in our first reactor. So that's the W-250 reactor.

Um, obviously the next goal is to go turn on another one. Um, but there's a sort of a success metric here that we talk about in the company, and we call it tick rate. So tick rate is how we judge ourselves and how we judge other people in the nuclear field.

Um, tick rate — you know — comes from video games, right? And it's sort of the time in between changes in the game state, right? And um for us, what this means is how long from the founding of our company to the first time we split an atom to the second time to the third time to the fourth time.

And the goal of the company is to get that tick rate as low as possible. Right? So our first ever mark from filing in Delaware to first atom split was 2 years and 4 months. From uh Project Nova first atom split to the second time here in this reactor was about 7 months. And the goal is to get that window as short as possible.

Um, and we will get it down to minutes. Um, this company will get to the point where we have a new reactor turning on every few minutes.

And when we do that — if you look at the economics of nuclear — it really is driven by how quickly and cheaply can you produce plants, right? The uranium is very cheap, right? The fuel is very cheap. The question is: can you produce plants quickly and cheaply? And that is going to be driven by iteration and by scale, which ultimately is driven by tick rate.

So, I'm very proud of the team. We've done some enormous, incredible things that no other nuclear startup has been able to do yet. But we are nowhere near done. We're very hungry. Um, and we're going to make that tick rate, you know, 6 months, then four, and then one, and then into the minutes.

参观 W-250：Modular Citadel（模块化生物屏蔽墙）

Welcome to War 250. Uh, so where you are standing right now uh is the first advanced reactor to ever make power by a startup. The first advanced reactor ever built outside of the national laboratory. This is the fifth new nuclear device to make power in the United States since the year 2000.

Valor is the only private company founded since the discovery of nuclear fission that has ever made nuclear power. Um, and this is really the thing that is unique about what we're doing — that we're a new team. We're a new company and we're attacking nuclear fission from first principles.

All the other companies in the space who've actually made power before, you know, are 100-year-old or 150-year-old utilities, defense contractors, large engineering corporations, and they haven't really done fundamental R&D. What they've done is they've picked up sort of government work, commercialized it a little bit, done some supply chain integration.

Uh, but this is like startup mindset applied to nuclear fission. And there's a lot of things about this plant that's different that you'll be able to see.

The first one is what we're looking at right here. This big awesome structure is called the Modular Citadel. I'm going to talk to you for a few minutes about concrete. And it's going to sound like I'm a big concrete nerd, and I guess that's okay. But uh, this is actually some of the more innovative stuff that we've done in this reactor.

The purpose of this concrete is a bio-shield. The reason we're able to stand here and not be receiving unhealthy doses of radiation right now is because there's 78 inches of concrete uh in front of this reactor. If this concrete were not here, uh we would not be able to stand here.

But it's actually very special concrete. The Modular Citadel is a series of pre-cast blocks. So each of these blocks was actually created in a factory. It's our factory. We have a uh what we call the Citadel factory in Salt Lake City. Um, and we have a production line where we could create 3,000 blocks a year that are identical to these, and they arrive here on a truck and then they get placed by this crane up here. So that's why we have this crane. They can place these blocks.

And what's really awesome about this is people have thought about doing this before in nuclear. They thought about pre-cast blocks, but the problem is like, well, if you're going to put two concrete blocks together, if you were to zoom in on a microscopic level, those blocks are actually not touching, right? Nothing's actually touching on a microscopic level. You have a little gap between there. And gamma rays can slip between the gaps and neutrons can slip between the gaps.

So, what you can't see here, and I'll show you some photos afterward, is that if you were to look with an X-ray in that seam right there, you would actually see that the concrete itself follows what we call a tortuous path. So, it's a sine wave. So, the concrete on every seam follows a sine wave curve — and actually on the vertical edges as well. So, if you were looking at that vertical seam, you would also see a sine wave. If you were to look in the corners, you would see a sine wave. There is no straight path from the inside of the reactor to the outside through the Modular Citadel for a ray to follow straight through concrete.

Um, and what's even more interesting is that traditionally when people have thought of this — because it's really hard to get that casting shape right and to get it to actually be solid enough to bear all of this weight — um, the way that they get around this is they would grout it. So they would grout it with more concrete. You'd have like sort of a wet concrete mix that you use to seal them together. This has no grout. These blocks are literally just stacked.

In fact, there's no mechanical lock between them either. There are no bolts. There are no screws. We do have this um frame around it. This is called a seismic frame. Um, we did this — we actually don't need it according to our engineering calculations. Uh the Utah State building code — there was a disagreement about whether or not we needed a seismic frame — and you're like, "Oh, let's just put it on just to be safe."

Um, but the entire Citadel has no fastening of any kind uh in the blocks. And what that means is we went from 3 months, which is what it would normally take to build a bio-shield like this, to we stack this in about 42 hours.

为什么 Valor 比别人快？

How do you explain the pace of what you're capable of doing here versus other people that are trying and have been trying?

Yeah, the pace is extraordinary. Uh, and you know, of course I'm proud of my team. Of course I think Valor is the best nuclear company out there. But I would argue empirically we have the highest pace of any nuclear company in the world and it's not close.

Um, and the reason for that is two things.

One is we are obsessed with simplicity. Um, we want our designs to be as simple as possible. Um, we would rather have a simpler machine that's easier to build, that we can build at higher scale, than a machine that's more efficient, that's more performant. Um, we have ruthlessly deleted complexity, deleted parts, deleted systems where they're not necessary.

The other thing is we picked a super safe architecture. And this is I think a point that it's hard to tell from the outside because every reactor looks the same from the outside and every CEO says that the reactor is safe. Um, and to some extent they all are, right? Because they're highly regulated and we make sure that they're safe. But there are levels to this, right? And it goes back to the previous conversation we had about odds versus consequence.

Um, a TRISO-fueled, graphite-moderated, helium-cooled reactor of the geometry that we've built it here is just about as safe as humanity knows how to build a nuclear reactor. And that actually allows us to move faster, right? Um, and that's going to allow us to scale as well. The lower that we can keep that consequence, the greater we're going to be able to scale and the faster we're going to be able to move.

The last thing is just the way that we have shaped this team. I have built this team very very carefully. Um, I am deeply involved in every hiring decision. And uh we have filtered nuclear as an industry for people with extreme agency. Um, people with extreme bias to action. And actually we have a lot of people from outside nuclear, right?

A lot of the people here now have more nuclear experience than you know any other startup because they've actually turned a plant on. But when they walked in, they had never seen a nuclear reactor before. But what they had done is built hard things in the real world, right? And so we try to find those people who built hard things.

And then within nuclear, I particularly love to find people that started in nuclear because they loved it and they realized it was really slow and then they left and they're like, "Screw this, I'm going to go do something interesting." And then they, you know, built a ton of hardware somewhere else. And then I convinced them to come back um because I know that they love it, and I'm like, if you come back here you're going to be able to actually build and actually execute, but in something that I know you love.

Um, so also lots of people from the traditional nuclear world, but but those people we filter very very hard for: do you want to do science, do you want to write papers, or do you want to build reactors and turn them on? And um the entire Valor team is laser focused on that.

AI 需求与能源作为商品

How much of the premise of Valor is possible now comes from there are increasing demands for power you know in the United States largely driven by AI compute?

Yeah. So I'm super grateful for you know all our partners in the AI space. Um, NVIDIA of course — we've got a big event that we're talking about today. Um, and we'll talk about that more in a minute what we did here with NVIDIA.

But I would like to point out that energy being a commodity, the demand is set by the price, right? So if you can reduce the price, you increase the demand. And that will be true about energy forever, right? From now until the end of time, we know that energy is sort of the only scarce thing in the universe, right? It's the only irreversible thing in the universe in terms of a resource.

And so if you can figure out how to make energy cheaper, you will have demand. Um, and so I view Valor as having a fundamentally infinite market — because if we can figure out how to make, you know, energy at 1 cent, you induce your own demand, right? We begin to imagine ways to do things better with cheaper energy. If you can make it a tenth of a cent, you've induced even more.

And so, yes, it's awesome that we have these, you know, tailwinds of customers who are really hungry for power. I think it's also great just from a public awareness perspective where suddenly everyone remembers that power is super important, right?

But I also know that fundamentally making energy cheaper is what induces demand for more energy, and that's really going to be our strategy for the next century.

Can you talk about what you are doing with NVIDIA?

Yes. So uh we did something pretty awesome today. Um, we powered uh the first ever AI chip powered by a nuclear reactor.

Consume some nuclear power. Nice.

That's right. You have to tune in while the reactor is running. We'll keep it running for a few days uh as we continue our final testing um and we'll go through various tests over the next few months.

反驳"2031 前不可能规模化"的行业共识

When you go talk to the big buyers of compute and thus power today, they will say nobody can deliver at scale until like '31, '32, and until then it's going to be um diesel gen sets and maybe solar and batteries and you know we got to figure out how to make it until that period of time. What do you believe that they don't believe about the next 5 years?

Yeah, I think that they've looked at all of these nuclear companies of the past and again most of the nuclear industry is a modeling and simulation industry. That's really what it is. If you — you know — companies are what they do, right? And so I actually did not allow Valor Atomics to call ourselves a nuclear startup until we —

You're a concrete pouring and graphite —

We are legitimately a nuclear company because we split atoms, right? Um, but you know, I'm serious. We didn't allow ourselves to call ourselves a nuclear startup until we'd split the first atom, right? Because companies are what they do. Before that, we were a company that was planning to split an atom. Now, we're a company that has done it. Uh, you know, 10^20 or so atoms so far. Um, that number will keep going up.

Um, but yeah, so I think there's just a big gap in mindset between: are you serious about scale?

And I would also point out that exponential curves are very counterintuitive, right? If you look at the predictions of how many satellites would be in orbit um when SpaceX started going, people were like there's no way they're going to hit the numbers that they're hitting today. And exponentials are just really really hard for humans to understand.

Um, so I would say yeah, if you're looking at the nuclear space today, you don't expect people to have this problem solved in 2031. In fact, I would say even 2035, a lot of these companies are still not going to get there because they have the wrong mindset. They're not focused on hardware iteration, hardware execution, building the simplest and safest reactor that allows them to scale. And Valor just demonstrated that we are.

自己发明混凝土：Rock Hunt 的故事

We actually had to invent our own grade of concrete in order to do this. Um, and the reason is we need a high enough density to block gamma rays. The concrete also needs to be very strong because these are self-stacking blocks. And we also can't use rebar because if we use rebar, um, rebar becomes activated by neutrons. Uh, so you'll actually be producing nuclear waste.

We took two engineers on our team. One of them is a nuclear physics — uh, sorry, one of mechanical engineer, one of nuclear physics — and we had them tag team this, who said, "Go invent a grade of concrete which has the density to block gamma rays, which has the strength to actually be a concrete block that's self-cast, that has no rebar, and that has the right atomic mix that it will not become nuclear waste when it becomes irradiated."

And they literally flew all around the country collecting rock samples from all around the United States. Uh we called it the rock hunt. It was about a 3-week period from plane to plane, literally collecting bags of rocks. And they would come back on a Friday night and they'd set up the conference room with a bunch of buckets of acid and they would dissolve the rocks and then they would take the rocks through a spectroscopy analysis to figure out the exact PPM. And then they'd go back out the next week and collect the next sample.

Uh, by the way, the two team members are 23 years old and 21 years old, respectively, and they've done things that the nuclear industry has been dreaming of for literally 30 years. When we unveiled the system for the first time, we got DMs from people who have been in nuclear literally their entire career saying, "I've been writing about how someone should do something like this since the 1980s, and I've never seen anyone be able to actually pull it off."

And that's a really awesome thing about working at Valor. That's also why we get the best talent in the world — because if you are a really really talented person who cares about nuclear, you want to see things actually get built and you need to go where you can actually build things.

垂直整合：Valor 的秘密武器

You are not a paper and simulation company, you're a nuclear company, but you also seem to be a road building company and a building building company and a concrete pouring company and a rock finding company. So how do you think about — you're very opinionated that you know if we must for speed we will verticalize. How do you think about having those capabilities and why you make that decision?

Yeah, I mean this is the secret weapon of Valor and I'm going to give you the secret weapon because I fully believe that no one will be able to copy us in this.

Um, we are willing to verticalize anything that is necessary on our path to scale. Um, and we've proven that a couple times in this reactor so far uh in areas that we were told repeatedly was utterly impossible.

Um, and the fact is like when a problem is important enough, you can go solve it and you can verticalize it, because when a problem is as important as making energy cheap, um, you can go get the most talented people in the world to come and help you solve that problem, right? You can assemble the brightest minds to tackle it. You can get the best capital formation who helps you go and acquire the resources necessary.

Um, and it's really a mindset thing. You know, we've talked to and know many of the most talented nuclear engineers in the world. And what we keep finding over and over is: yes, there are amazing designs, um, but not much has actually been physically built. And if you have the muscle as a company to build things in the physical world, if you know how to do fundamental clean sheet design all the way through to prototyping, testing, and manufacturing, um, you have an edge that nobody else has in nuclear today.

Um, and so we look at really anything in the stack. And it's not that we want to verticalize everything. We'd prefer that most of the plants commoditize, right? In theory, it'd be great if we could just buy a kit, right? Like if anyone is selling a nuclear reactor kit, I'm a buyer, right? I'm going to go staple it together and I'm going to make energy and that's great.

You're just going to make lots of plants.

Exactly. But, you know, that's not how it works. In actuality, um, there are a lot of these components that are wildly overpriced that have super constrained supplies. And a fundamental edge that Valor has is that we know we are competent to verticalize any individual piece of that that prevents scale. Um, and once we identify that um we attack it ruthlessly.

And you don't think any piece of it is like you know intersecting with more regulatory pain, too complicated for you to figure out? Like when I think about your fuel supply chain —

It's definitely intersecting with regulatory pain. Um, and like we run at that pain. Like we just run toward gunfire on the most complicated things every time. We're two and a half years, three years into this company and we've done something that no other nuclear startup has ever done, which is make nuclear power.

Um, and it comes from this fundamental mindset that the team has, which is: yes, there are complexities in regulation. Yes, this is hard equipment to manufacture. Um, yes, it's difficult to do both of those at the same time where you have complex equipment and regulation and construction and rock hunting and pouring concrete and building buildings.

But the company that figures out how to do all of that complexity at scale and at pace is going to win. And, you know, we're sitting here right now, you know, less than 3 years from the founding of the company. And I think if you look back at 10 years — in 10 years from now — and you know Valor's worth $500 billion, something like that, um, you will be able to see that throughline where we took the hardest parts of nuclear, right — which is the site, right, and the regulatory and the fuel supply chain and the instrumentation and the shielding and all these things that everyone tries to outsource — and we said, actually, we're going to become masters of that, right, and we're going to go hire the best people in the world to tackle that. And that becomes our edge.

一个具体案例：45 万美金的电子盒 vs. 500 万美金的 RPS

Um, and in the actual manufacturing — this is another really good example of what makes Valor unique. This is our control skid.

Uh, these three boxes right there are basically an analog to digital electronics box. Uh, so if you know anything about electronic circuitry, it's pretty simple stuff. You've got some signal amplifiers. You got some basic rectifiers in there. Uh, and basically we take some analog signals from our detectors and we send them to the control room.

Each one of those boxes costs $450,000.

This is when anyone talks to you about like cost and nuclear like, "Oh, nuclear is expensive." Uh, they are talking about ridiculous things like this, right? And this is really where Valor's going to win. That we accepted the $450,000 cost on this one because we're moving really fast, you know, and spending a million and a half dollars for these three boxes was more worthwhile to us than taking a couple months of delay.

But this box over here — totally different story. This one, it was bid out at $5 million. They told us that it was going to cost $5 million to buy that box right there. That is our um what's called reactor protection system, RPS. This is basically the brain of the reactor.

Uh, this system right here is super unique. It has no human input. It's basically a self-deterministic machine that decides whether or not the plant is in a safe state. So it has three different sections that vote against itself to say is the plant in a safe state or not. And if two of them vote that the plant is not in a safe state, it shuts down.

And we were quoted about $5 million. Uh, and we were told it would take about two and a half years to build. Um, and so we said, "Well, all right. Maybe I'd be willing to pay $5 million, but I'm certainly not going to wait two and a half years."

And so after having haggled with this vendor for about two months trying to convince him to go faster, uh, we eventually got the team together and said, "Guys, we're going to have to build our own RPS." And, uh, we sat down with Joe who runs instrumentation and control — awesome guy, Brown dropout, uh really really good with electronics — and he brought together five team members and uh locked ourselves in the conference room and uh six weeks later we had a working RPS and we spent about $400,000 on it.

Um, everywhere in nuclear is like this. There are totally fake costs from an industry that is just totally anemic and doesn't know how to build anything anymore.

By the way, the conclusion to that story is kind of unfortunate — which is that when the vendor realized that we were going to do it ourselves because we just could not, you know, get our heads around a two-year timeline, uh they freaked out and started telling everybody in the industry that we're an unsafe company that's going to kill people. Uh, because we would dare to do something ourselves that, you know, is their special sauce that they're charging the market $5 million for.

Uh, and if you go to — I don't know if you guys use any of these like investor search platforms where you can like go look up a company and people have done like expert calls. Um, they contacted all of those expert calls companies and volunteered to do an expert call on Valor where their CTO —

Shitty.

Yeah. Their CTO basically went and like ranted about how we're the worst company on earth. Uh, because this is a massive massive threat to them — the fact that their $5 million system we can just make for a couple hundred grand with a team of 5 in — years.

Uh, but the more that you look at nuclear the more it's like this — everywhere you look it's a fake industry that has not been building anything for 40 years, and the little bit that they have been building they're charging a 100x margin on. But if you just take it from first principles and you have smart people in the room and you're willing to build it yourself, you end up with this and the plant is running.

融资哲学：股权而非项目融资

There is something very inspiring about the idea of like — given the physics work, it should be possible, and if it is possible to do it faster here, the best people will come. Yes. And the capital will come.

Exactly.

Um, this is an expensive project as far as I can tell. Uh how do you think about the fact that — the uh you're an equity financed venture-backed company today. How do you think about the fact that the like debt vehicles, project financing, operational proof of this doesn't really exist in the ecosystem to you know perhaps support as much scale as you'd like as quickly as you'd like?

Yeah. So this is actually one of the things that I noticed um years before starting the company. The sort of traditional plan for a nuclear startup is you try to do some engineering and some design and some customer LOIs and you try to assemble this package, and it's essentially paper, right? But it's a package that's attractive enough to get somebody to come and fund the project, right? That's the essential plan of all nuclear startups — they want to convince a debt financer or project financer or something like that to fund their nice paper package of designs and partnerships.

Um, I went into this company believing that — and by the way, I believe that that was possible like 10 years ago. And after watching this fail over and over and over in other startups, um, I came into this company knowing we're not going to do that.

Um, one of the unique advantages that the United States has is a risk-on equity capital environment, right? And the type of risk that we are asking investors to take is: um, we know the physics works. We know that there's infinite demand. And how we go from here to there is technology execution.

And guess what? Venture capital in the United States is the best at underwriting tech risk of anywhere in the world. Right? That is how Silicon Valley has come to be what it is. That is how venture capital has come to, you know, eat as much of finance as it has — is that venture capitalists know how to underwrite technical risk.

Um, and it's execution and it's, you know, when we say tech, we're not talking about physics. We're not talking about science. We're talking about mechanical engineering. We're talking about thermal hydraulics. We're talking about instrumentation, right? Manufacturing methods. These are — um it's complicated. It's nuclear, but it's like significantly less complicated than a rocket engine, for example.

Um, so, you know, I think this is one of our fundamental edges and advantages — is that um we're risk-on about this and we have a cap table that's risk-on about this. We are willing to go build reactors on equity balance sheet because that's going to allow us to prove it years ahead of anybody else, right?

Where our competitors are still trying to convince, you know, uh fundamentally risk-averse financers to finance a project, we will be on our fifth reactor, right? And after five reactors, well actually now project finance is an option, right? Now debt is an option. But it's only an option once those early backers have put the capital in the ground, demonstrated it, and that becomes an enormous moat that's frankly going to be impossible to beat.

Gigasite 策略：自建电厂，让负载自己来

Uh, one of the more different things that you've decided to do here um is you know try to build your own plant independently.

Yes.

And bring customers to it as you know at much scale as possible in a gigasite. Right. Um, and you know part of the belief there is like: well if I can provide power at scale and especially cheap power, the load will come to me for many things. Um, you know, can you describe like the rationale behind that strategy?

Yeah. So it comes from a very similar argument that I was making before — where I'd watched these startups try to get that deal right for a very long time. And frankly there's just too many parties, right? There are like too many people and too many parties —

Touch your speed. Like, "Well, let's negotiate about this site and this permit —"

Exactly. Like, yes, in theory could we go to you know a hyperscaler who wants a gigawatt of power in a place like — yes, and certainly we will at some point. Um, but we could also just put a gigawatt on the ground, right? And we can do that on our own timing, um at our own pace, and that pace is going to be faster than anyone else in the world.

Um, and then the question flips around, which is: if I have you know a gigawatt of power with land and fiber, is someone going to put a data center there? It's like — yes, absolutely. Right?

Um, and so yeah, I think like in the long term we'll maybe start to do some of these deals where a dedicated customer wants their own gigasite for their hypercluster or something like that. Um, but it'll be driven by speed even in that case, right? We'll go to the customers who say um you know, "we want it delivered in a year," right? Not the customers who say, "oh we're thinking about this in 3 to 4 years."

So really speed and scale. If you honestly — if you ask me any question and you keep digging back, you will find speed and scale. Like that really is what nuclear is missing. It's missing its Ford moment, its Toyota moment, its SpaceX moment — where you have a company that realizes that the actual thing that nuclear is missing is scale deployment. And that's really what we care about.

And so the gigasite — we've got a site here. We're going to build more reactors here. We have a planned gigasite nearby. We get to go with conviction, build power. Um, and yes, I'm absolutely confident that load will follow.

作为 CEO 如何驱动速度

How do you um — if you just think of your primary objectives as speed and scale, given safety, right — how do you like drive that personally as CEO? What do you wake up every day and like think about — problems to solve or what to focus on?

Yeah, this is one of those things that I think is probably the most irreplaceable attribute of a company is its internal pace. We call it tick rate.

That's a sort of a metric that we've designed around going critical in new systems, but you could probably translate that to any industry. You know, in software, it's probably something like, you know, new continuous integration builds per hour or something like that.

But um I think it's the hardest thing to replicate. You have to start it from the first day of the company. Um, it has to come from the CEO, and the first five team members have to have it deep in their bones. The first 20 team members have to have it deep in their bones. They have to be trained that when they hire people, they're looking for that in those people's bones.

Um, and that's also not enough. Also, once you're in that setup, I have to constantly — and this is really what I do — go around the organization and speed things up, right? You know, continuously ask — um, my chief of staff's laughing over here because she's seen this happen many times.

Um, you know, I go war mode on things, right? So, like look around and like, okay, that's going slow. And we will set up a literal war room — and I guess not literal war room, but we'll set up a physical war room — um, and we will say, how do we turn a six-month timeline into a four-week timeline, right? And maybe we'll hit 8 weeks, right? But it's not six months.

Um, and I think I've realized like there is no autopilot for that. There's no automatic mode that makes a company faster inherently. There are lots of things you can do to set it up for success. Um, but you have to push.

You will always — I will always have to push. And I will always push. And it's because um I know that the physics are here, right? I know that energy will be a thousand times cheaper than it is today. I know that nuclear fission will make energy a thousand times cheaper than it is today.

Somebody will do that. And it's really up to us as a team whether or not that's going to be in the future decades or the future centuries. Because that time is up to us, right? It's up to us how quickly we go down that path, how quickly we make energy more abundant. Um, and that feeds into everything else. So it's highly motivating for us. Um, and we are tackling it as fast as we can.

尾声：便宜的能源意味着什么？

People in their daily lives thinking about what happens for them if this works and you get very cheap abundant nuclear energy — like imagine that future for us.

Energy is the fundamental input to the quality of human life. If you look backward into time and say, "How did humanity go from one standard of living to the next?" — it's always unlocked by cheaper energy, right?

So when we went from essentially a solar-powered world, which is what we were in agrarian age — um like literally human muscles were solar-powered because you were either eating plants or eating animals and both of those things were essentially chemical energy that is built up by the energy of the sun through photosynthesis. Um, life was hard, right? Life was really, really hard.

Um, when we got into underground hydrocarbons, you had this massive explosion of quality of life, right? Where we have, you know, heat in the winter and we have AC in the summer. Um, where we're able to drive cars to get to the hospital.

Um, even really simple things — you know, we're in an aluminum strut building right now. Um, every one of these struts that you could see behind us are made of aluminum. There was a time when aluminum was considered a precious metal. Um, where kings would literally make jewelry out of aluminum because it was that rare.

And the thing that changed it to a literally structural metal that we make buildings out of is energy becoming cheaper, right? We figured out — you know, Charles Hall here in the United States figured out aluminum electrolysis. Um, and cheap enough energy means that you can actually just separate that oxide from the aluminum. Uh, and you can have pure aluminum metal. Um, and that is entirely downstream of energy.

So I would say that the standard of living that we enjoy today is entirely downstream of the fact that we figured out you can make pretty cheap energy with hydrocarbons. Now our goal is not pretty cheap energy. It's insanely cheap energy, and that's going to be unlocked by nuclear.

Um, what it will look like I think is fundamentally unpredictable, but I have a few ideas.

Um, first of all, I'm very very excited about transportation. Um, like if you think about the fundamental limits of why is it that I can't go see grandma every week, you know, on a plane — like why is it just so hard to do? Why is it so expensive? Why is it so complicated? It really just comes down to the fact that we've kind of squeezed as much juice out of jet fuel as we can, right? It's just hard to imagine jet fuel getting 10 times cheaper than it is today. Uh, and it's polluting the environment and all of these different things.

Um, if you could make energy 10 times cheaper, um, then you probably could go visit grandma every other day, you know, just on a — on a Saturday it takes 30 minutes and, you know, she lives across the country, but you go and visit her on the weekend.

I'm very excited about what I would call uh hyper-techno-industrialism. This is a term I invented for —

Okay, haven't heard it.

Yeah. Um, for basically everything becoming free.

Uh, this sounds like I'm talking sci-fi here, but like an approximation of this will happen. Um, let me put out a thought experiment for you.

So uh let's take a a a physical good that's manufactured today, right? Let's take this microphone. So this microphone came from a factory, and the factory had um sort of three inputs, right? It had people in the factory that are working on it, assembling things. It had input materials, right? And I would include in those input materials, you know, the plastic on here and this foam thing — also the machines, right, that you know, maybe injection molded that plastic. And then the last thing it consumed was energy, right? So energy is one of the three things that made this.

What's interesting is um with the introduction of AI, we're actually converting the human input element to energy, right? So instead of a person having to pick something up and put it onto the next tray, right? You're going to have a robot pick some up, put it onto the next tray and consume energy, right? Um, and instead that human will be able to coordinate hundreds of robots, right? So, their personal output will go from picking up an object and putting onto a tray to essentially picking up 100 objects and putting it onto 100 trays. And the trade-off we're making there is that we're increasing the consumption of energy.

Now, you could still say, "Okay, but you are consuming physical inputs." What's interesting about that though is let's dig into that. All right. So, there were some physical inputs and some machines that went into that factory. Well, where did those come from? Right? Those don't grow on trees. Those also came from factories. And you can ask the same question. What went into that factory? It was energy. It was machines and it was people, right?

Apply the same rubric and you realize, okay, actually, it's really just machines and energy. Well, where did those machines come from? If you keep asking this question, what you realize is energy is the fundamental input.

When we figure out AI and robotics that allows us to do semi-autonomous manufacturing, energy will become the cost of all things, right? The cost of buying a thing will become the cost of energy used to make it. And to the extent that we can make energy 10 times cheaper and then 10 times cheaper, uh, that is the extent to which we will be able to make basically everything free.

Um, and that's very exciting to me. Uh, because if we're going to go explore the stars and we're going to set up, you know, a place for us to live on Mars and explore the universe, we're going to need a lot of stuff, and it's going to need to be a lot cheaper than it is right now. So that's very exciting to me.

Exciting to me too. That's a great place to end. Thanks, Isaiah.

Thanks so much for having me.

收尾

Find us on Twitter at @NoPriorsPod. Subscribe to our YouTube channel if you want to see our faces. Follow the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you listen. That way you get a new episode every week. And sign up for emails or find transcripts for every episode at no-priors.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、真正的悖论：核能不是"物理问题"，而是"心理问题"——一个被自己吓死的行业
最出人意料的观察是：**这家公司做的事情，从物理上看毫无新意——高温气冷堆（HTGR）、三代裂变燃料（TRISO）、石墨慢化——每一项技术在 1960 年代都已经存在**。Isaiah Taylor 真正的"发明"，是拒绝把核工业当作核工业来经营。
请注意他反复强调的那个隐蔽指控："大部分核能公司其实是**建模与仿真公司**"。这是一句杀人不见血的话——它意味着过去 40 年里，整个行业把自己伪装成工程组织，实际上运行的是学术论文生产流水线。他们的产品不是反应堆，而是**关于反应堆的确定性叙事**。三里岛之后，行业不是变得更安全，而是变得更擅长"证明自己是安全的"——两者是完全不同的技能树。
这暗合了 Nassim Taleb 的一个观察：**当一个系统被要求"绝不能出错"时，它会停止学习**，因为学习需要错误。核工业过去 50 年就是这样一个"反脆弱性归零"的系统。Valor 真正的赌注不是技术赌注，而是一个哲学赌注：**用"降低后果"替代"降低概率"**——这一句话背后，是从贝叶斯世界观切换

item.title: [Lex Clips] The greatest emperor of the Byzantine Empire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最响亮的沉默：君士坦丁"不解释"背后的元信号

Kaldellis 抛出的一个细节值得反复咀嚼——当皇帝干掉自己人时，只要有一丁点儿可用的借口，都会通过"所有媒体"大肆宣传（"Crispus 谋反"之类）。但君士坦丁处死亲儿子 Crispus 与妻子 Fausta，却连一句敷衍的辩护都没有留下。

这里藏着一个反直觉的认识论工具：宣传的缺席本身就是证据。在信息学上，这类似于"负空间信号"——不是看模型说了什么，而是看它在应该说话时的沉默。这套推理逻辑今天依然可用：一家公司裁掉高管后连套话公告都懒得发；一个政府对某件事全网屏蔽却不给出任何叙事版本——沉默的形状勾勒出了真相不可言说的轮廓。历史学家在 1700 年前的宫廷里读出的东西，和现代情报分析员在企业公关中读出的东西，是同一种语言。

二、"家族"是政治的伪装词：血缘作为可回收耗材

对话里那句轻描淡写的话其实是一颗炸弹：君士坦丁的"家族"本质上是"通往顶峰路上的便利联盟"。岳父、妻子、儿子——这些词在拜占庭政治词典里更接近"股东"、"合伙人"、"接班候选"，血缘只是包装。

跨领域的意外连接来了：这与现代硅谷创始人叙事里的"我们是一个家庭"（we're family）是同一种修辞欺骗，只是方向相反。君士坦丁把政治联盟称为家族以便动员忠诚；现代 CEO 把雇佣关系称为家族以便榨取忠诚。两者都利用了人类对血缘的原始信任回路，作为一种廉价的治理协议。真正的家族在裁员/清洗到来时，都是第一个消失的。Crispus 之死不是"家庭悲剧"，而是一次股权重组。

三、"要不要跟他喝一杯"vs"他做了什么"：历史评价的两套操作系统

Lex 追问的"喝啤酒测试"和 Kaldellis 的"决策后果测试"其实是两种根本不同的价值操作系统在打架：一个是德性伦理（他是什么样的人？），一个是结果主义（他改变了什么？）。

这里有个让人不太舒服的观察：Kaldellis 承认历史学家通常不做排名列表——这不只是学术清高，而是因为做排名会强迫你在这两套操作系统里公开站队。一旦你把君士坦丁排在第一，你就在承认：迁都、皈依基督教这种"世界级决策"的历史权重，压过了杀妻杀子的道德失败。这是一个大多数学者不愿在公开场合做的道德计算。

更耐人寻味的跨领域联系：这与今天所有"影响力排行榜"、"改变世界的 100 人"、乃至算法推荐系统面临的困境完全同构——排名的暴力在于它必须把不可通约的价值压扁成一维。Kaldellis 说做这件事"很好玩"，但这个"好玩"里藏着一丝学者的越界快感：他在做一件历史学纪律禁止他做、却是每个普通读者最想让他做的事情。通俗史学的能量，也许恰恰来自专业史学不敢碰的这条禁忌线。

📝 转录文本

Byzantium and Friends, and Ranking the Emperors

Lex: So you do a podcast, Byzantium and Friends, but you're also a guest on podcasts — and I should mention you being a guest multiple times on the excellent The History of Byzantium podcast hosted by Robin Pierson. I highly recommend people listen to it, to all of your appearances. You have a lot of really fascinating ones.

But the reason I bring all that up is: one of the episodes, you do the fun thing of selecting the top 10 emperors of Byzantium.

Kaldellis: Yeah.

Lex: And spoiler alert, everybody should go listen to the full list, cuz you justify all the different options. But Constantine ends up in the number one spot.

Kaldellis: Yeah.

Lex: And so, can you just add a little bit more depth — why he would be in your number one spot, now that we're talking about him?

Kaldellis: Yes. It's not because I sort of like him as a person, right? I mean, as you said, he was pretty murderous.

Lex: Yeah.

Kaldellis: Right? All those wars — so clearly ambitious, murderous, ruthless basically.

The Murderous Side: Family as Alliance

Lex: Can you actually speak to the murderous aspect? He seemed to have murdered a lot of people in his family.

Kaldellis: Yes. That's because what we call his family were also alliances of convenience that he made on the way to the top. And when those alliances — or, you know, those wives or those fathers-in-law — were no longer convenient, or turned against him, he had no hesitation in removing them.

But he's also, you know, he gets a little extra bonus, because he also murdered his own son, Crispus, who was apparently a very competent and popular kind of general — and we don't know why.

Lex: Mhm.

The Silence Around Crispus

Kaldellis: He executes Crispus soon after defeating Licinius, and then follows it up with the murder of his wife — not Crispus's mother — and we don't know why.

The funny thing is that when emperors do this sort of thing, if there's any kind of remotely like a good reason, they'll blast it through all of the media, right? Like, "Crispus was rebelling against me," or whatever. There's nothing. He doesn't even try to justify it, which means it must have been something unjustifi— like, I don't know, I can't process it.

Lex: And also I read that the historians of the time would just ignore that that happened.

Kaldellis: Yes. Well, sort of. So, when Crispus is alive, it's all like praising Crispus as a — "oh, you Constantine, you great emperor, and your wonderful son who will…" whatever. And then Crispus is executed, and he just disappears from the — like, they never talk about him.

Lex: Yeah.

Character vs. Consequence: The "Beer Test"

Lex: Actually, there's another side to a — you, I think, mentioned, whether it's on your podcast or elsewhere, that if you had to have a beer with any of the emperors, that you probably wouldn't. So it's not like — some of the most impactful, some of the most influential and great emperors that we talk about, they might not be very pleasant people.

Kaldellis: Right. So, let's set aside whether you'd like to have a beer with these people.

The reason I put Constantine at the top of the list is because the decisions that he made were so consequential. Just important. And for the most part, not bad decisions. So I couldn't not put him at the top of the list.

The, you know, the creating Constantinople and converting to Christianity and setting the empire on a path to conversion — these are major, like, world-history-level decisions. Right? Now, not many emperors do that. In fact, Constantine is in like his own league when it comes to those kinds of decisions.

Criteria for the Ranking

Kaldellis: My other criteria for top emperors were basically those who did the job well. Right? Not making, you know, these kinds of earth — you know, shattering decisions, or, you know, world-history-changing decisions, but just who performed the role competently, with results that impacted the lives of their subjects for the better.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: So those are the other criteria that I used. It's difficult to juggle these different criteria, right? In order to create a systematic list. And by the way, we historians generally don't do these kinds of things.

Lex: (clears throat) You said it's fun.

Kaldellis: It is fun. It was fun. And it allows you to talk about certain things that you might not otherwise do.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: So, yeah — that's why Constantine was at the top.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最响亮的沉默：君士坦丁"不解释"背后的元信号
Kaldellis 抛出的一个细节值得反复咀嚼——**当皇帝干掉自己人时，只要有一丁点儿可用的借口，都会通过"所有媒体"大肆宣传**（"Crispus 谋反"之类）。但君士坦丁处死亲儿子 Crispus 与妻子 Fausta，却连一句敷衍的辩护都没有留下。
这里藏着一个反直觉的认识论工具：**宣传的缺席本身就是证据**。在信息学上，这类似于"负空间信号"——不是看模型说了什么，而是看它在应该说话时的沉默。这套推理逻辑今天依然可用：一家公司裁掉高管后连套话公告都懒得发；一个政府对某件事全网屏蔽却不给出任何叙事版本——沉默的形状勾勒出了真相不可言说的轮廓。历史学家在 1700 年前的宫廷里读出的东西，和现代情报分析员在企业公关中读出的东西，是同一种语言。
## 二、"家族"是政治的伪装词：血缘作为可回收耗材
对话里那句轻描淡写的话其实是一颗炸弹：君士坦丁的"家族"本质上是**"通往顶峰路上的便利联盟"**。岳父、妻子、儿子——这些词在拜占庭政治词典里更接近"股东"、"合伙人"、"接班候选"，血缘只是包装。
跨领域的意外连接来

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Rahul Sagar on the Birth of Indian Nationalism
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Parmanand 的"匿名"不是谦逊，而是一种精密的知识论工程

大多数读者会把 Mama Parmanand 的隐身理解为维多利亚式的自我谦抑，或者印度传统里"无我"的道德姿态。但如果把他放在与"美国联邦党人文集"的 Publius、19 世纪英国匿名评论杂志（Edinburgh Review、Quarterly Review）同一条脉络里看，会发现一个更冷峻的判断：他在设计一种"防止论证被人格劫持"的公共讨论协议。

这里有个出人意料的现代回响——他的匿名逻辑，几乎精确对应了当代密码学里"零知识证明"的伦理直觉：让命题在不暴露命题人身份的前提下被检验其真值。Sagar 明确划出一条界线——匿名可以用于"陈述原则"，但不能用于"指控事实"——这条界线，恰好是今天 AI 时代所有 deepfake、匿名爆料、社交媒体人格分身问题的核心分水岭。也就是说，一个 1890 年代躺在床上、患帕金森的印度人，比大多数 21 世纪的平台政策制定者更早想清楚了"匿名的合法边界在哪里"。

二、印度自由主义之所以"看不像自由主义"，是因为它的敌人不是国家，而是国家的"缺席"

这是全篇最颠覆的洞见，也是我读完最想标红的一句：霍布斯到密尔面对的是一个已经存在、需要被驯服的利维坦；而 Parmanand 面对的是一片"利维坦真空"——盗匪、雇佣兵、旧王室、失控的英军和种姓 panchayat 塞满了权力的空隙。

这里可以做一个跨领域的连接：印度 19 世纪自由主义者面临的问题，本质上是"经济学里的科斯问题"，而不是"政治哲学里的洛克问题"。洛克问"国家凭什么征税"，Parmanand 问"如何先造出一个能收得起税的科层制"。这就解释了为什么他们把改革希望寄托在 maharaja 而非议会——不是因为反民主，而是因为在制度真空里，你需要一个"制度企业家"来承担初始的固定成本，就像 Alchian-Demsetz 意义上的"团队生产的剩余索取者"。maharaja 在这里扮演的不是君主，而是"制度的初始股东"。

这一视角也解释了一个更大的谜团：为什么 1919 年后英属印度的自由主义走向了"群体代表制"（分立选区、种姓保留），而土邦里的自由主义走向了"个体解放"？因为前者是在已建成的殖民国家里争夺分配，后者是在空白的制度画布上进行奠基。同一个词"自由主义"，指向的是两种完全不同的生产函数。

三、Sagar 声称"印度自由主义没认真对待宗教，才为印度教民族主义腾出了空间"——这句话的意思远比它听起来激进

表面读，这像是一个保守派对世俗主义的老生常谈。但把 Parmanand 的第一封信（关于宗教）与 Bankim 的 Dharmatattva、与今天的印度政治并置，会浮现出一个令人不安的结构性洞见：

19 世纪的印度自由主义者本来试图完成一场"印度教的宗教改革"——一场类似路德/加尔文的、把宗教从祭司手中夺回来交给个人的运动。Parmanand 那段对"祭司比信众还无知"的猛烈批判，不是启蒙式的反宗教，而是一种为宗教辩护的、要求宗教配得上现代个体的呼吁。这与马丁·路德"人人皆祭司"的神学几乎同构。

而这里的跨领域连接更让人震动：印度错过的不是"宗教改革"本身，而是"宗教改革所配套的经济-法律基础设施"。韦伯说新教伦理催生了资本主义，但更精确地说，是宗教改革需要一整套支持个体脱嵌于共同体的东西——私法、劳动力市场、识字率、独立司法——而这些恰恰是 Parmanand 反复要求 maharaja 去建设的四件事（约束自我、行政训练、教育、公民社会）。

于是有了这个惊人的推论：20 世纪的印度独立运动，选择了"用民族主义替代宗教改革"的捷径——因为民族主义能提供宗教改革所能提供的"超越个人的动员力"，却不需要付出建设配套制度的漫长成本。而今天的印度教民族主义，某种意义上是那场未完成的宗教改革，以一种被政治化、被扁平化的形式回来讨债。Sagar 说自由主义"没认真对待宗教"其实是在说：你们把 Parmanand 那套"缓慢的、教育性的、结构性的宗教现代化方案"扔掉了，于是就只剩下"快速的、情感性的、动员性的"版本可用。

这不是保守派命题，这是一个关于制度演化中"跳过步骤的代价"的命题。它和 Sagar 提到的新加坡例子、"排序论"（sequencing argument）在深层结构上是同一件事：你不能省略中间阶段而期望终点相同。

📝 转录文本

引言：一次久违的重逢

SHRUTI RAJAGOPALAN: Hi, Rahul. Welcome to the show. It's such a pleasure to have you on again.

RAHUL SAGAR: Thanks so much for having me, Shruti. Absolute joy.

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes. I feel like you introduced me to the men that I call my 19th-century soulmates, all these classical liberal gentlemen who believe in rule of law and binding the hands of the monarch or the ruler. They're against arbitrariness. They like good fiscal policy and public finance. I feel like you introduced me to these soulmates I didn't even know I had.

The latest one is in this book. It's called The Birth of Indian Liberalism. The latest protagonist you've introduced me to is Narayan Mahadev Parmanand, or Mama Parmanand, as he's known. In this instance, it is letters that he's written to the Gaekwad of Baroda. The letters are extraordinary. Your very detailed introduction, placing these letters in context and really telling us why these are not just letters but the birth of Indian liberalism, but also India's own endogenous or indigenous political theory, is really remarkable. So, one, thank you for being here, and two, thank you for the book. It's been a joy to read it.

SAGAR: Thank you so much, Shruti. Writers dream of readers like you.

RAJAGOPALAN: [chuckles] I have lots of questions on why you call this The Birth of Indian Liberalism and also why you call this the "first work of political theory" as opposed to just ideas or ideology or letters or just discourse.

SAGAR: Yes.

RAJAGOPALAN: But before we get to that, most people have not heard of Mama Parmanand, so maybe it would be good if you started with who he is, how you found him, and what made you put together this book.

一、谁是 Mama Parmanand：一位刻意隐身的思想家

SAGAR: Thanks. Yes. He is a bit of a mystery figure. Almost no one in India or really anywhere in the world will have heard of him. His book has not been cited at all. In all these years of academic work and research, the text has been cited once by Chris Bayly on his famous book on Indian liberalism, but he didn't actually look at the book. It was seen to be just by some random figure. It's not attributed to Parmanand. It's written with a pseudonym.

And so, he's a complete unknown. Really, the only two hints that he even existed to most Indians—one is there's a road named after him in Mumbai, and there's a scholarship named after him in the University of Bombay, and that's it. Most people will see that, and it'll be one of those things that they drive past or look over, and they just assume, "Must have been someone." It turns out Parmanand was actually probably one of the most important intellectuals, not just because he wrote this book, or this work of political theory, but he was just one of the most important Indian intellectuals of the 19th century.

He made it a point to be always in the shadows. He never wanted to attract attention. There's a whole story behind that. That's the reason why it's a deliberate choice on his part to step away. Most people would not have heard of him, and it's not a surprise. It was his intention, which I've somewhat destroyed now by making his life incredibly public. [laughter]

从 Sawantwadi 到 Bombay：一位神童的诞生

He's born in 1838 to a middle-caste trading family in Sawantwadi, a small principality. It's in the midst of turmoil. There's chaos. There's confusion. His father passes away in the midst of all these stresses, when he's two, and his mother decides to send him off to Bombay, where his sister is.
