item.title: [Mercatus Center] Laura K. Field on the Making of the MAGA New Right
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：MAGA新右派其实是"最像左派"的当代右派

最令人意外的洞见藏在这场对话的方法论层面，而非内容层面。Laura 反复提到这些右派知识分子的"ideas-first"（观念优先）方法——认为"政治是文化的下游"，只要重塑观念就能重塑政治。这句口号出自 Bannon，但其思想血统真正的祖父，其实是 葛兰西（Gramsci） 的"文化霸权"理论。

换句话说，Claremont、后自由主义者、国家保守派——这批人在"如何改变社会"的操作系统层面，运行的是新左派 1960s-70s 的代码：长征穿越体制、占领机构（Heritage、ISI）、培养干部、构建反霸权的意识形态叙事。他们指控进步派"通过教育与文化摧毁美国"，同时逐字复制了进步派的策略手册。这就是为什么 Patrick Deneen 读起来像倒过来的 Marcuse，Yoram Hazony 的"民族"话语在结构上极像早期认同政治——只是把阶级/种族换成了"heritage Americans"。

这构成一个隐藏的系统性真相：当代美国政治光谱的两极，在"元政治"（meta-politics）层面正在收敛。他们争夺的是同一套武器——观念工程、身份塑造、通过学院与媒体的缓慢渗透——只是弹药方向相反。真正的"古典自由派"（procedural liberalism）反而成了双方共同的"过时对手"，因为它假设制度可以中立，而两极都不再相信中立。

跨领域连接：杰斐逊悖论 = 硅谷悖论 = 蒸汽机悖论

Laura 用了一个极精彩的转折——她把林肯在 Dred Scott 之后关于"繁荣易生暴君"的话，投射到了硅谷科技寡头身上。这个类比乍看勉强（她自己也承认），但如果拉远镜头，会发现一个跨越三个世纪的同构模式：

  • 1800s 的杰斐逊：写下"人人生而平等"，同时其经济基础（种植园）绑定他于奴隶制。他不是虚伪，他是结构性被困——理念与利益的物质基础发生了绞合。
  • 19 世纪的 King Cotton：一种技术—经济复合体（轧棉机 + 全球棉纺工业）把道德恶行变成"不可撼动的经济现实"，任何废奴主张都被斥为"不切实际"。
  • 今天的硅谷—AI 复合体：技术精英一边宣扬解放个体的话语（"民主化知识"），一边其商业模式（数据攫取、内容训练、劳动力替代）在系统性地剥夺普通人的自主性与经济地位。

三者的共同结构是：当一种技术—经济系统积累到足够规模，它就开始生产为自己辩护的意识形态，而这套意识形态会俘获甚至那些真诚的自由主义者。杰斐逊为奴隶制找理由（"联邦强制解放的威胁"）；今天科技领袖为算法权力找理由（"进步不可逆转"）。Lincoln 说 Declaration 是留给后世的"一颗难啃的坚果"（hard nut to crack），恰恰是因为他预见到：财富积累会周期性地生产新的贵族制。这不是巧合，这是共和主义传统中一个被反复重发现的定理。

打破常规的判断：这场对话的"盲点"就是它自己

带一点冒犯地说——Laura 与 Kristen 在结尾拥抱"公民教育 + 阅读经典 + 多元对话"作为解药，本身就落入了她们批判的 MAGA 新右派的同一个认识论陷阱：认为读对了书就能治好政治。

这不是说公民教育无用，而是这里有一个未被察觉的系统性讽刺：一群相信"观念优先"的右派知识分子，让两位相信"观念不够"的左倾政治理论家，最终提出的对策是……更好的观念教育。两边都在假设 "the arena of ideas" 是政治病症的手术台。

真正被系统性回避的问题是 Laura 自己在中段一闪而过的洞见：Trump 根本不听这些知识分子。这意味着 MAGA 新右派的知识分子们，可能既不是 Trumpism 的"设计师"，也不是它的"下游解释者"，而是寄生的合理化机器——一种事后为既定权力提供合法性外衣的学术工业。如果这个诊断成立，那么用更好的公民教育对抗它，就像用更好的营养学论文对抗糖业游说：理论战场根本不是决胜战场。

Sam Bagg 关于"state capture"的框架（Laura 在结尾提到但没深入）才是被压抑的真正答案——问题不是美国人想什么，而是美国的制度允许谁把想法变成政策。这条线索被"回到经典与对话"的温柔结论盖住了，这本身就是当代自由派知识分子的一个"啊哈时刻"应该指向的地方：我们最擅长的工具，可能正是我们最不该过度信任的工具。

📝 转录文本

Virtual Sentiments 播客 · Laura K. Field 访谈

开场与嘉宾介绍

Welcome to Virtual Sentiments. I'm your host, Kristen Collins. On today's episode, I talk to Dr. Laura Field. Laura is an associate with the Illiberalism Studies Program at George Washington University, a non-resident fellow at the Brookings Institution, and visiting scholar in residence at American University. She is a political theorist and author of the book, Furious Minds: The Making of a MAGA New Right, published last year by Princeton University Press. In addition to her academic work, she has written for The New Republic, Politico, The Bulwark, Le Monde, The New York Times, and The Atlantic.

本期背景：两个 250 周年

As I noted in past episodes, this year commemorates the semi-quincentennial anniversaries of two momentous events: the publication of Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations and the American founding, as marked by the signing of the Declaration of Independence. Laura and I discuss the latter of these two anniversaries.

Whenever we commemorate, we reflect on the past from our own present circumstances. Laura's new book analyzes the arguments of different intellectual figures on the American right who, despite disagreement amongst all of them, do all share deep criticisms of liberalism. And that's not just more progressive approaches to liberalism, but also basic principles of classical liberalism, including often commitments to value pluralism and even individual rights. Many of these intellectual figures also use the American founding to justify their criticisms of basic principles of liberal democracy.

So this conversation with Laura served as a primer to the different groups now comprising the pro-Trump coalition on the American right and how they differ from past conservative coalitions, such as what is popularly known as fusionism — the alliance between more traditional, often Christian conservatives, and libertarianism, which largely shaped the American right in the second half of the 20th century.

In response to these challenges, Laura and I reflect on pluralism, disagreement, and the political compromises that marked the American founding in the early years of the republic. Laura and I talk about how, as political theorists, we do think ideas matter, but they are not the only things that matter. And even the best ideas can be warped beyond recognition or beyond intention when they are tested in that arena that is real-world politics. Still, we reflect on how the great books and civic education, if pursued in an environments that actually treasure pluralism, disagreement, and critique rather than simply as partisan projects, can help us engage and develop the intellectual and emotional skills and habits required of citizens in a free society.

访谈正式开始

On today's episode of Virtual Sentiments, I'm talking to Dr. Laura Field. Laura is a political theorist at the Illiberalism Studies Program at George Washington University, and she is the author of the recently published book, Furious Minds: The Making of the Mega-New Right.

Kristen: I wanted to pretend that I'm a late-night host. Should I walk in?

Laura: Yes.

Kristen: So, Laura, I'm so excited to talk to you today. Thank you so much for joining us.

Laura: Thank you, Kristen. I'm very happy to be speaking with you, too.

纪念与"当下性"

Kristen: Great. Well, you know, this year, as you know, is the 250th anniversary of the American founding. And so every special year that we commemorate anything like this — this year is also the anniversary of the Wealth of Nations. So we on the podcast have been doing some commemoration of that text as well from Adam Smith.

It's always a project that is twofold. There's a lot of ways in which we commemorate history and we're trying to recapture that political moment, that past moment, understanding the thinkers, the text on their own terms, in their own context. But there's also a way in which we can never escape ourselves. Right. So every past centennial, bicentennial of the American anniversary is always going to be kind of embedded within that political moment.

One of the ways that I wanted to frame this conversation with you, Laura, is to get a good sense of how your book illuminates a particular set of people and how they are wrestling with and putting forth their own visions of American identity, of the legacy of the founding, and how should we, who might disagree with a lot of the claims that they make, wrestle with their arguments — and especially kind of the broad tent of liberals, classical liberals, people who were also more of the conservative tradition but do not agree with these sets of people, progressive liberals, people on the left. How do we all negotiate this moment in American politics while looking back on the history from the founding to today?

Laura: Thank you, Kristen. I just — I think that's a wonderful way to introduce the book, but also especially this act of commemoration, you know, and just how we're always situated in the present and the past at the same time. And I just love the way you put that, that every time we look back on things in these special moments, it is always going to be a reflection of what we're currently thinking through and acting upon in our own politics.

And I think when we look through history — even just, if we look at the controversies in 2020 over some of these old Confederate statues — it's a reflection of that moment, right? If we could look back to the erection of some of those statues, those took place in particular moments, right? And were acts of commemoration or of, like, you know, memory building and identity formation for us. And so I just think it's a magnificent thing to keep in mind.

本书的定位：一部关于"当下"的历史

And my book, yeah, I mean, it's obviously a very sort of presentist book — is the way that historians talk about this, right? It is a kind of history of the present spanning 2016 through 2024. You know, it was very intentionally bracketed in that way. And so, I mean, of course, I'm reaching back into the past to try to explain how current actors understand themselves, but basically it's a story of ideological radicalization, right?

And I think what's happened is an ideological radicalization away from the principles of liberalism and the commitment to liberal democracy. And so there's a lot of different things going on. But I think there's a kind of unity in that illiberalism or rejection of liberal internationalism, of an orientation in liberal rights, that really has been dominant since especially the end of the Second World War — where you see the kind of defeat of Nazism and, you know, after the aftermath of the Holocaust, you see this proliferation of liberal democratic constitutions that were in many respects inspired by the American founding. You see that all around the world. They didn't copy the American founding exactly or anything like that. But, you know, there was a kind of continuity there.

And so this movement is a pretty explicit rejection of a lot of that. But of course, it's also complicated. And some of the actors I write about — and I can go through the different groupings — but some of them see themselves as the ultimate defenders of that project. So I think we have a lot to talk about.

严肃对待知识分子：不再把 Trumpism 简化为"民粹起义"

Kristen: Yes. No, for sure. So let's get into that, because I think what I really enjoyed about the book — and I think everyone's going to have a different perspective on it — is that I really read you as actually having a lot of empathy at a lot of moments in the book, even as you're quite critical, right? I think you are trying to understand the people that you write about from their perspective.

And also just in the very basics of this book, for people who maybe even have heard of it, is that you talk about the fact that a lot of times people in academia, people — you know, the sort of maybe let's say like educated East Coast elites — kind of dismiss Trump or Trumpism as kind of like a libidinal mess of instinct and also, you know, corruption and self-interest and all this. Or if anything, maybe if they take it more, you know, taking it as like a mass uprising of some sorts of, you know, Trump as this populist figure for all these people who feel resentful of the establishment. Right.

But you show that there is this whole very wide variety of intellectuals who have kind of justified and tried to create and understand a critique of and a rejection of the very liberal principles that were taken for granted. Right. And so could you maybe start us off by just giving us the basics of maybe some of these different camps? Because I know somebody like me who's not familiar can sometimes forget that there's such differences between these groups the way that you've kind of categorized them, so that we know that we're not just painting this as like a — you know, the Trumpist right one big glom.

Laura: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, I think — I mean, I was writing the book partly with the skeptics in mind, and it is really focused on the intellectuals. So I don't deny that a lot of this is a kind of populist uprising fueled by Trump and his obviously uncanny ability to harness these grievances and articulate things that people are angry about. And that's obviously a big part of it.

But there's also over the course of the last 10 years been a quite deliberate, quite obvious — if you're paying attention to this, which, you know, I did for various personal reasons — there has been this pretty deliberate intellectual project that's come into formation. And so I'll tell you a little bit about how I've been thinking about it.

I mean, it's basically the first half of the book covers the sort of first inklings of this movement, the different people who — like Michael Anton at the Claremont Institute, who were some of the first to defend Trump from this intellectualized perspective, and then kind of getting together in different organizations and bringing different types of people together. And then the second half is more about the consolidation of the movement and its kind of takeover of the GOP.

But just to kind of set us up, I think it's a pretty narrow slice of the overall MAGA movement, but I think it's had a huge impact in Washington and in the thinking, right? I talk in the book — and I'm sure we'll get to this — about the ideas first. Like, they really are people who think about, you know, if you have the right ideas, you can kind of use that: if you build it, they will come. And you're using ideas and ideology to sort of make a change in our politics. Trump isn't so invested in that, but they've been very effective in building a cultural milieu that is open to a kind of ideological version of Trumpism.

Michael Anton 与《93 号航班选举》

So there are different people involved. And I think especially Michael Anton is very useful here. And so Michael Anton is a figure who was a fellow at the Claremont Institute out west — these sort of West Coast Straussians, defenders of the Constitution as they see it, right, or the American founding. The whole mission of the Claremont Institute is to defend the principles of the American — restore the principles of the American founding. So the assumption being that these things have been lost, they're fragile. And I'll get into sort of how that works.

But Michael Anton wrote this piece in 2016 called The Flight 93 Election, which was an intellectual, pretty extremist defense of Trump based on this idea that things have gone so badly with the old establishment that it's time for taking really dramatic action. And so voting for Trump is risky, but it's better than a vote for Hillary — because Hillary, for him, he uses this rhetoric, you know, it's like playing Russian roulette with a semi-auto, right? With Trump, you take your chances, but with Hillary, it's certain death to the republic. So this is how he's thinking about it.

But his enemy and target there — and this essay went viral because it was read by Rush Limbaugh in 2016 in September when it was published. And so it did probably have some impact on the vote. Who knows? Right. But Rush Limbaugh said this is an attack on the establishment, on the never-Trumpers. And I think that that's accurate. And you can sort of see it in how he lays out what Trumpism stands for.

从 Reagan-Buckley 融合主义到 Trumpism 的四条腿

I mean, the Anton argument was that the old establishment — and in the book, I call it the Reagan-Buckley establishment. So it's fusionism, it's free market economics, social conservatism and traditionalism, and also that are kind of brought together by this anti-communism originally, and then that sort of morphed into liberal internationalism or — I mean, adventurism. I don't mean to defend all of this, right? But it's a kind of bundle of things that from Reagan onwards really unified and brought the Republican Party together.

That also coincided historically with the departure of a lot of Black people from the Republican Party and the moderates from the Republican Party, which is, you know, important historically when we're talking about some of these themes that are going to come up, I'm sure.

So Anton says that's all been a failure. Social conservatism hasn't been maintained at all. It's been a complete, you know, from the civil rights movement, and but more feminism and immigration — they haven't managed to conserve anything. The economics have been a failure. Yeah. And so has the international adventurism. And so he says, well, our version of this and Trumpism — and he saw clearly, I think, that Trumpism did stand for these things, and he's drawing on other traditions like paleoconservatism and even Goldwaterism and the old right — but he says, no, what we stand for, what Trump stands for, that I recognize as something I stand for, is:

  • Nationalist economics — a rejection of that free market stuff.
  • Secure borders — or rejection, obviously, of this demographic shift. And he's very replacement theory. I mean, it's very explicit, conspiratorial.

I have encountered a lot of people who I'm surprised don't know about this theory. I mean, it's a conspiracy theory and it's very dark.

"大替换"阴谋论与背景

Well, it's based on — there has been a big demographic shift. I don't think that's debatable, right? The Civil Rights Act in 1964 changed the standards by which people were judged admissible or not. And so we have had a big change and an opening and less discriminatory by race in its sort of outcomes. And then there's been, I think, a problem of illegal immigration that a lot of people have been trying to fix for a long time. And so there has been this change.

But the great replacement theory is more specifically this idea that — and he doesn't use that term because that wasn't in use yet in 2016, I don't think. Maybe it was by scholars, but — or I don't really — maybe it was. I'm not sure about the actual origins of that. I've forgotten. But basically, it's the idea that the Democratic Party is welcoming this demographic shift and all of its policy is oriented towards that because they are trying to get these people to come and vote for them, to secure power forever.

And so that's where it's kind of like, well, A, these people aren't voting. They're not legal here. Right. That's just a lie. And B, the idea that there's a kind of mechanism here or, you know, that the Democratic Party is organized behind this is, I think, just totally ignorant. So, I mean, it's a lie. So that's the conspiracy. But he's very much on board with that. He says it very explicitly in the Flight 93 election that, you know, the Democrats have been — everything's against the right because of these forces on the Democratic side that the establishment has gone along with. Yeah, Republican establishment.

So his — so anyway — so we've got nationalist economics, secure borders, and what he calls America First foreign policy. And it's also of course still a doubling down on conservative values. So that's sort of what I — I sort of talk about the new — I don't think there's like a new stool, it's got these four legs. And because the old Reaganism, there's this famous image of the Gipper's stool, right, which is the three-legged fusionist stool.

So Michael Anton delineates and defines Trumpism already back in 2016 as this like totally new policy agenda. And I think it's accurate. And I think that a lot of what we've seen in terms of policy and what they're actually trying to accomplish does line up pretty well with some of that, although obviously there are complications here.

So that's the broad schema. But again, that's — and I think they all pretty much stand behind that policy agenda. Yeah. Although — I mean, I guess I just — we have to have a caveat here from the beginning, because of the America First foreign policy is often tied to a kind of isolationism. And they were arguing that Trump alone recognized that these Middle Eastern wars were a failure. And now, obviously, where we are sitting in 2026, that looks pretty preposterous, the idea — because of what's going on in Iran.

So, but that's Michael Anton. And then that's sort of the foundation of what they were trying to do and why they stood with Trump. But then you get this proliferation of other ideas. You get these different people getting involved. They're drawing on different traditions. And there's different schools of thought that are kind of building around the movement. And they're happy to cooperate with each other because of their shared purposes and because of that shared vision that I think that Anton got at. But they also do have differences. And I think that's where we can kind of start to parse their different understandings of the founding, too.

意识形态与现实的落差：为何"观念优先"这么难谈

Kristen: Yes. Yes. Excellent. That's exactly what I was thinking about. If you'd like to bring us into that direction. But maybe before we even get into those differences between the groups —

If you might also, as long as the timing works out for you, as you just said, just to take an example: so much of the motivation — and I don't think the right was alone in this — in being frustrated with American foreign policy in the past, you know, since 9/11, really, the reaction to 9/11 and longer, you know. I know there's this Onion article that has this joke and it's like, "boyfriend is going on about something the U.S. government and CIA did in 1960 again." So I think everybody — some people, they know that that brings to mind.

So I don't want to limit ourselves, but I do want to say that, you know, so we have that thread of argument — which is that push away from American adventurism, or if it's a push away from the neoconservatism or a push away from liberal internationalism one way or another, it's a reaction against the idea that America has done too much, has expanded its military might abroad and in the Middle East and made things worse in a lot of ways.

And yet, even though that was this sort of intellectual argument or reaction, in practice, we're getting mired in the Middle East again. We have all these issues around oil. Point being that we haven't — you have those ideas that are circulating, but don't actually seem to be making a dent in terms of the actual foreign policy. America's still doing similar policies, still doing similar military spending. In terms of also, you know, now you have people in the tech world like Alex Karp mobilizing around: how do we make Silicon Valley more patriotic? How do we get more government contracts and kind of strengthen the surveillance state?

So we have this disjuncture, which I think I wanted to foreground to ask you to explain a little bit more about this concept that you have in the book about ideas first — that this other, you know, that with some of the disagreements in terms of the precise way that these thinkers make their arguments, the sort of extremity of those arguments in their politics, the degree to which they are friendly to more extreme conspiratorial thinking versus much more — I don't know how to put it, but just not engaging in that kind of work, at least directly.

There is, though, this maybe cohesive methodology or intellectual approach to political theory or politics and ideas, which is this idea of focusing on ideas in this specific way of thinking about the role of ideas and politics in a specific way.

"Ideas First"：观念如何 —— 以及不如何 —— 塑造政治

Laura: Yeah, I think that's a very useful thing to just put on the table because it's also the question of how much does this all matter when you've got someone like Trump leading — like who's actually in charge. He's the one actually in charge. And so we've got all these people with all their big ideas, and some of those — and they've thought about this, they've been talking about it for a long time. It's got some roots, right? It's got some ideological heft. And again, a big part of the book is to try to take that seriously.

And just relating back to your prior question about, you know, why don't we take this seriously? I mean, this is also kind of the nub of that. You've got Trump not listening to these guys. But I think the trouble is the ideas can matter in some ways and not others. And they're still kind of a necessary — I mean, I think they do matter in important ways. So I'll delineate some of that.

And I also just want to say that in writing the book, it was hard to navigate that problem, because I do think that people were not seeing these ideologues, were not seeing this intellectual project, and that that was a weakness, right? And so I wanted to shine a light on that, obviously. But also, I also didn't want to stain-wash it, right? — or launder it. I was sort of walking this fine line. And so my answer to that was to be very transparent and to be kind of sympathetic when I could be, because that's what I think we need to do. Because I think that there's a lot here that's, you know, interesting and useful and drawing on real problems in our society, but also things that are dangerous, and that the liberal imagination almost ignores — can't quite wrap its head around, and we need some help with that.

观念在哪些方面确实重要

So this problem though of how the ideas work, of how this — the people I write about connect to Trump and his actual actions and the actual political world that they're situated in — is sort of a massive question. And I think that there's a clear answer in terms of how and why they matter. And I can just list some ways in which they matter:

  • They've sort of taken over a lot of conservative institutions — like the Heritage Foundation, the Intercollegiate Studies Institute here in D.C. And there's been a proliferation of new institutions that have formed around MAGA by this point, right? We're almost a decade into it. And there's like new blogs and all of this. So it matters for that.
  • It matters for personnel, right? Obviously, a lot of the people who are — but again, we're still talking about Washington. So we've got a lot of people in Washington who, being sort of educated and formed in these milieus, work as staffers on Capitol Hill.
  • It matters for policy. Obviously, you've got something like Project 2025 that's been heavily inspired by these ways of thinking.
  • And the broader political culture that we're in is being shaped by the ideas that these guys are promoting — from highbrow ideas about the American founding and the need to restore those principles, to new ways of understanding constitutionalism and originalism and legal thought as it relates to the founding, and the ideas of nationalism — all the way down to the real gutter stuff, right? Where you've got Groypers and Bronze Age Pervert and raw egg nationalists trying to completely change the way in which we think about, you know, gender and voting rights and all kinds of things.

So there's a lot there that matters for the culture. And then there's also just this other thing, which is like the global and international dimensions of all this. So I think it matters for that reason.

一场高度意识形态化、"观念优先"的运动

But OK, to actually come back to your question now: they have built a movement that's highly ideological, that is not just sort of an elite phenomenon, insofar as some of these people I've mentioned are pretty — you know, they're kind of influencers. They're on Twitter. You've got Bronze Age Pervert with his podcast and his sort of influencer shtick and pretty big following. But it's still generally the people I focus on — it's kind of a narrow ideological thing that's happening. I'm really focused on PhDs, people writing articles in magazines, right? They're writing mission statements and signing letters together.

And so I call that the "ideas first" sort of method — or I don't think I call it their method, but that sort of characterizes how they think about change in politics, sort of their theory of change. And this has overshadowed old roots going back to Buckley and his project of developing an intellectually respectable conservatism, and somebody like Richard Weaver and this phrase ideas have consequences. But even to more recent phrases like — I think it's Bannon — politics is downstream of culture. So if you can change the culture, you're going to have a big impact on politics — and that that's going to be sort of a tectonic shift in how politics works just by working these ideological channels.

Yeah. And I think there's a lot to that. I think I'm very sympathetic to that as, like, a good old Straussian who's been trained in how ideas shape history. But there's also limits to that.

观念之力的限度

So they're testing the limits of this theory. And they've been very successful — they've got the vice president kind of in the palm of their hands, right? Or, like, he's their big vehicle, poster child. So they've been incredibly successful in a way. And they're very ambitious and deliberate about this. And that's incredible.

But also it's kind of detached from reality in a lot of ways. And so I try, throughout the book and in my own thinking, to just think about: well, if you've got all these ideas, does it matter that they don't actually map onto reality and in some ways they're not true? Does it matter that a lot of other people think about things totally differently and don't ascribe to the same way of thinking? What do we do about that in a liberal democracy?

And then sometimes it's just completely — I mean, I've already said, what about when it doesn't match reality — but what about when they're just lying about things like January 6th, or about the security of our elections, or about the nature of the working class, like just all kinds of things? And they're also pretending to speak for everybody else.

So I think there's just a lot of problems with this approach — not necessarily inherently. I mean, there's some tensions here in my thinking, too. How much do we need ideas? How much do we need to agree about ideas? How much do we need to share a common understanding? Those are real questions for politics always.

And their answer is typically: we need to have agreement. And part of the problem of modernity is that we have such a scattered and fissured and fragmented politics that we're losing the capacity to live together. And, you know, there's something persuasive about that. But then their answer often is: so we're going to come and restore the truth with a capital T. We're going to teach the young people the truth with a capital T. And then everything's going to be okay. And I don't think that's in the spirit of the Declaration of Independence — is where maybe that could be our transition.

自由主义、多元主义与分歧的政治

Kristen: Yeah, yeah, yeah. No, I think that's great. Well, I do think it is important to think about — what is maybe generally the unifying theme of the thinkers you talk about is rejection of a liberal pluralist society. And I think there's a lot of good reason to see the American legacy and the founding as one of trying to create what we have now taken for granted in terms of a pluralist society with vast political disagreements without imposing a single kind of unified way of life or political system.

I mean, in the sense that — you know, we don't need to get into this fully and we'll talk about this, I think, in future episodes — but the idea that the Declaration of Independence had this kind of all this emphasis on universal principle, those on anti-monarchism. And then eventually with the Constitution, there was a lot more of a complicated political bargaining process that was also trying to create a federal government — a powerful one, in order to do what a state at that time historically needed to be able to do in order to raise money and to protect itself through a military.

But within that period of time and in those political instances, we have huge disagreements among the actors involved in doing that. And so disagreement and negotiating disagreement has always been a core part of that project. And so I do think there is this — how do you deal with, and moving forward — and I'm not talking historically, but more about the way that liberals today conceive of liberalism — is there is this tension of: we need to admit that we do have a positive vision for society. We do have a vision of a society of people who are different, who disagree, but are still able to enjoy equality under the rule of law, for example.

That is, you know, I think a very basic liberal ideal that a lot of people, even if they're more right-leaning, more left-leaning, will agree with. But then how do you deal with the fact that that's something that's supposed to then be capacious enough to allow for varying degrees of community and varying degrees of other sorts of visions of the common good, visions of living well together — if you're always going to then be keeping in mind, we need to have negotiations around disagreement here. We need to be able to have people who are still treated as members of a common society, even as they pursue various forms of living and various organizational forms and family forms and things like that.

Patrick Deneen 与后自由主义的挑战

Laura: Yeah. And I think that maybe a useful place to kind of begin getting into some of the weeds of the MAGA New Right is to think about, you know, how do they see that project?

Free press, the separation of powers — I would include freedom of inquiry and freedom of conscience. And so there's all these things bundled up in when we speak about liberalism, or when someone like Patrick Deneen speaks about why liberalism failed.

And I think that the most sort of dramatic critique of this — or the maybe the hardest one to refute — is the idea that bundled up in that bundle is a normative project. That Patrick Deneen says: not only is this a smuggled-in normative project, it's actually not just a set of neutral procedures — it's actually a very thick moral way of thinking that destroys other forms of thinking, right?

He says it also just eats away at politics itself. So liberal individualism and our particular conception of freedom as free agency and autonomy — that that destroys the social fabric, destroys all these subsidiary institutions that people talk about, especially from the Catholic tradition. And so there's that critique, but also I think there's a recognition, not just from the post-liberals, but many on the right now. They say, you know, liberalism pretends to be this neutral thing that doesn't impose a thick conception or a comprehensive worldview, but it does. And liberals don't even know this is happening. And it makes it very hard to be a conservative because we don't fit into some of the norms that are being formed. We don't share some of these assumptions, and because we have these deep disagreements, we're not welcome in these spaces and we're not valued.

新右派的分类图谱

Kristen: So why don't we, keeping in mind the anniversary of the founding, walk us through these different groups, their different approaches, and then the degree to which any of them have particular visions of the founding, what they're trying to retrieve, and how they situate their own projects within this grander narrative of America.

Laura: Okay, so I do this classification of the different groups on the new right, and I talk about:

  • The Claremont people — the Claremonters, we could call them Claremonsters. They seem quite happy with that; they call themselves that sometimes.
  • The post-liberals — who tend to be sort of socially conservative Catholics.
  • The nationalists.
  • The hard right.
  • And there's also kind of the tech world we could get into.

So that's the general schema. And they think pretty differently about — they're drawing on different intellectual sources, and I think they think differently about the founding era and about liberal democracy.

Claremont：作为"建国真正守护者"的自我理解

So I'll start with the Claremont Institute. They see themselves as the true defenders of the founding. And the mission of the Claremont Institute was to restore the principles of the American founding to their proper place in American society. And they were inspired by Harry Jaffa and his work on Lincoln primarily, and especially Crisis of the House Divided. It was a really important book that Jaffa wrote all about the Lincoln-Douglas debates.

And this really is — you know, I think we're going to have to get into some of that, right? If we're going to be talking about — we're already pretty well into this conversation, but we've got to talk about Lincoln and his understanding that the Declaration and what might be understood as a second founding, right? And this idea that the Declaration was this promise and that needed to be fulfilled — that was unfulfilled but was possible after the Civil War. And so the Claremont scholars sort of embrace that history.

But Harry Jaffa did write this important book about the moral core of Lincoln's project and his deep belief in equality and his understanding of just the logic of equality, and how that actually worked with the future of America and the problem of slavery. Yeah. And so he wrote about that, and the book is pretty fascinating.

But then later in life, he also decided that, of course, you needed Lincoln, but that the founders had it pretty good at the beginning — that the American founding was so impressive that he talks about it being the best possible regime. I mean, he caveats it just enough to say that that's possible in the real world. But he holds it up as a very high version of politics that is almost insurmountable in terms of its perfection and sort of represents this accommodation of Athens and Jerusalem, reason and revelation.

And I think that's where there's this sort of — it's extremely idealistic, which is appealing. I mean, that's nice, but is it true, right? And so we have this problem with the Claremont Institute, which is that they hold these ideals so highly, and they have a very rigid understanding of it, in my view.

So they take something like the Declaration of Independence and they want people to understand it their way. And so the Declaration is sort of one thing — it means something specific. Or, I mean, maybe I'm exaggerating a little bit, but I think they've got a pretty rigid understanding of the Declaration and of the constitutional system, such that any departure from that — whether it's the New Deal, whether it's the Civil Rights Act and then the demographic changes that have happened because of the immigration changes, or even Roe v. Wade — I mean, you can get into all these different things. And the growth of the administrative state, they've been very anti that for a very long time.

So you have any sort of changes — they're not very well equipped to deal with that. And they're very explicit about that because they think that they're a violation of the true constitutional order. And so it becomes an existential threat. Those changes, those historical changes, become catastrophes and moments of crisis. And on par with the crisis of the two constitutions and the House Divided. And so that warrants radical action, right? And counter-revolutionary measures, whether it's January 6th or the election of Donald Trump or, you know, who knows what's in the future this year.

I mean, you get this kind of justificatory schema for really dramatic and violent reactions. And so there's a real vehemence to the Claremont world, and I think they legitimate that using arguments associated with the founding fathers and with their own pretty limited way of understanding what that moment meant.

Clarence Thomas 与"自然权利"的党派化

And I guess a good example here — which I haven't had the time to fully — at full disclosure, I haven't watched the whole speech yet, right? So I've been reading around this — but Clarence Thomas comes in, and it's a very classic — he gave a speech in Texas last week where he's appealing, where he's talking about natural rights and the principles of the Declaration. And he's also criticizing progressivism. And bringing up Woodrow Wilson and the progressive abandonment of natural rights.

And the idea here being that progressives don't believe in the principles that ground our political order. And you can find — he's not making this up. You can find places where Woodrow Wilson and John Dewey criticized a certain way of understanding natural rights and conceptualizing the ideas that informed the Declaration and the ways in which the founders thought about politics. Absolutely.

But then to project that onto a deep critique of that whole history and to say that that's also causally connected to Nazism and Stalinism — I mean, that's a big claim. And it's highly partisan against today's progressives. And the idea that so many — I think that the Claremont people are quite comfortable saying that people on the left undermine the political order because they don't believe in natural rights and because they don't believe in freedom and equality in the way that they do.

And I just — I think that's a big fudge and kind of a lie. Because I think that then, what are we going to do? Go examine every American's civic concept? So, how do you understand natural rights? Are you based on a natural law? You know, there's something really perverse about that — about this assumption that we should all be understanding the Declaration in the same way. So maybe I'm getting too quickly into the critical mode instead of exploring what the actual —

But that's sort of the Claremont — how I think about the Claremont's take. And, as you can see, you get into pretty complicated territory philosophically once you start unpacking it.

谁能垄断"自然权利"的解释权？

Kristen: Yeah. Yeah, I mean — you've done excellent podcast episodes before on these topics too. So I know for a fact we could spend hours talking about the specifics of any one group, including the Claremont thinkers.

In terms of, he writes the Declaration of Independence, this very vehemently powerful vision of natural rights, and also has a paragraph critical of slavery originally in his first draft of the Declaration that gets cut for various reasons. He's clearly aware of the way in which slavery is a violation of natural rights. But then he —

Laura: But he was using it sort of to take a cheap shot at the British, right?

Kristen: Yes.

Laura: Which was a little, I think, is my understanding.

Kristen: Yes, that's right. So Tom talks about how the issues with this paragraph is that on the one hand, it's critical. On the other hand, it's also blaming everything on the king, and is very much embedded in Jefferson's own skepticism and criticism of the British system and of the attempt to create an agrarian, Republican, virtuous community in America — that actually also requires expanding slavery, requires expansion of land, all these various things.

And that is to say — so they have this figure who has an incredibly powerful authorship of the Declaration that later generations will draw on to expand freedom for people who are oppressed. Right. And yet he himself has these contradictions.

And what Tom talks about is that there is this tendency for people to make sense of this as just a matter of personal hypocrisy. But he argues that instead we should think of Jefferson as a tragic figure, as somebody who has these grand self-contradictions, who also is maybe deluding himself, wants to not be aware of what is even happening just in the course of the early 19th century — in early America, and the kind of Missouri crisis and the debates around expanding slavery in the territories.

And what Tom talks about is this idea that we have Jefferson's broader political coalition of these kind of agrarian Republicans — they were concerned with a variety of issues. And after a career of mostly public silence on the slavery question, has this letter that is published — you know, we don't have to get into it, he knows it's going to be public — where he is essentially justifying the fact that slave owners don't want to — if slavery isn't allowed to be expanded in territories, then people in the southern states are going to be afraid that the federal government is going to come and forcibly emancipate the enslaved people, and as a result they will be unsafe, right?

And so this fear of this threat of federal power ends up being justifying for why it should be okay to allow the expansion of slavery, even when people know — and Jefferson was critical of people who were offering more positive visions of slavery. So it's not — again, it's a complicated coalition, and a thinker who is not necessarily endorsing it as somehow consistent with the natural rights. But it is also something that is — there's this inherent tension here.

And it gets us into a position where we have to reckon with this legacy and we have to reckon with the fact that we have the principles of the Declaration of Independence that we draw on for this idea of liberty, while being compatible for decades in America with a horrific, oppressive practice that can be —

Laura: Understood as nothing other than a flagrant abuse of human beings. Barbarism.

Kristen: Of humanity. Yes. And Smith has incredible passages on this, too. So we have to reckon with this history. We can't just wish it away or imagine it didn't exist, especially if we're even just trying to think of a figure like Jefferson.

So I guess I want to ask, how do you think of those moments? How do you think of the idea of how do we approach this as understanding the promises of the founding, the ideals that we want to draw on, this idea that we're celebrating the anniversary of the Declaration — while also doing justice to the fact that I think people who get frustrated by the simple idealization, or the sort of claims that this was this brilliant moment that was not — when in reality it was marred with all sorts of compromise, moral and political?

Laura: Yeah, I mean, I think it's a very difficult thing. And again, it brings us into what our moment is and where we're sitting and what conversation are we having?

And I think we see that problem again and again in politics. I mean, that is politics: it's always going to be a problem of living up to one's ideals.

And where we are sitting now, in the book, I try to — I have some sympathy with the people who want to lift up the founders. Right. And we haven't been living up to the principles of the founding, and that there's been terrible injustice that needs excavation and change.

And a lot of what happened at the founding is worthy of admiration and worthy of celebration.

Kesler 与 Lincoln 对 Dred Scott 的回应

But I think what happens in a lot of instances is that this desire to elevate and celebrate — you know, we have blinkers on. And in the case of just thinking about the Claremont group and some of what happens in their scholarship, it's a pretty consistent pattern.

I'm thinking of Charles Kesler's The Crisis of the Two Constitutions, which is a book that sort of borrows the valence of Jaffa's title, right, and of course of Lincoln's speech, to write about the Founders' Constitution versus the Progressive Constitution, and how these things are in massive tension, and the Progressive Constitution is a betrayal of the Founders' Constitution — so all these familiar things.

But he looks at that history between the founding and the Civil War, and he writes as though there had been massive progress. And it's not true. And there was a massive expansion of slavery. There were all these efforts, obviously, to continue to expand slavery. There have been some limited legal improvements.

But even Lincoln — I'm thinking of this wonderful speech, well, I mean, wonderful but very upsetting speech he gave after the Dred Scott decision — where he's very explicit that the quality of life of American Black — like enslaved peoples and Black people — has only deteriorated. And it's delusional. And, you know, Taney, the author of this decision — this is the famous decision that says that Black people did not have standing in the court and that they did not have equality, that the Declaration of Independence was not speaking to them because they are inferior, right? And that they had no rights that the white man was obliged to uphold. And so that's the ruling from the Supreme Court.

And Lincoln says in response — he offers a reading of the Declaration of Independence that says: that's not true. Black men in some states were, in our history, free men and were allowed to vote. And there's been a kind of transformation, the growth of slavery.

Now this — as you know, there's a whole, you know, Lincoln built up this idea that there was a — a little conspiratorial, right — that there was this agenda, that slave power to expand slavery to all the states in the West. And, I mean, that's all complicated history, but it's also true, right? Some of that was happening, and that was a big part of his argument against Douglass, and the amount of money that was being made. It was the anchor — you know, the slave system was the anchor of the American economy in a lot of ways.

And so Lincoln, though, offers a beautiful defense of the Declaration as this expansive liberationist sort of document. And I don't think that Kesler — I mean, but a lot of that history gets fudged by the Claremont Institute, and the ugly parts get kind of disappeared. And it's kind of — I think it's part of that desire to elevate — is means not encountering the other side.

And when you do that, you're excluding — I think it kind of comes up in the response to the 1619 Project. If you can't even hear the arguments that are being posed by critics of the founders or critics of the current elite powers or the current ways in which we're taught this history — then it's not just blinkers, it's claiming a monopoly on knowledge. And I don't think that's in keeping with the sort of liberatory spirit of what the Declaration means, which is that we get to be free people who have our own ideas and who stand for the rights of people, right? And who get to act on that.

And so there's a lot here, obviously, but that's kind of how I think about the Claremont people. It's very complicated. They're not all the same. They don't all think in the same ways. And some of it gets really ugly, and some of it's just kind of blinkered and, I think, incoherent — or ahistorical, I should say.

批判理论也能带来形塑体验

So we have to be clear about that. But we also have to be clear about — and the critical theorists could be clear about this too — about what it is we're loving and what we're appreciating and what we're not just debunking, but valuing and deriving meaning from.

So that experience is so profound and important, and I'm sure it happens in religious contexts too. I mean, we're talking about the formation of our souls, right, in some ways — or our characters. So this can happen in a number of different contexts.

This is part of what the reading experience does for us. And we have a kind of vicarious experience of these things.

And so then, you know, you do that enough in grad school — you sit around for four years, writing things and reading this way, and then you know being pumped up — yeah, it's — you feel like you're on top of the world and that you might be that kind of world-historical actor too. And maybe that's true for you, and maybe — but the problem is that — and this is, you know, you get someone like Nietzsche is so powerful on these themes, right, of the great man and of the ugliness of the person who would take that away from you, right? Because it's just envy and it's just like tearing down and who wants to be the naysayer and say that.

But on the other hand, Nietzsche, like any historical figure, including yourself, mister, is like has some problems, is living in a real world, is living in a body.

对新右派"过度自我严肃"的诊断

其他阵营：后自由主义者、Vermeule 与 Deneen

Kristen: Well, and so I wanted to give you an opportunity if you wanted to expand on some of that. So we talked about Claremont a lot. I don't know if there are other figures you want to talk about. Laura: Yeah, I mean, let me sort of — I should go through the other groups and how they think about the Declaration, because I haven't fully done that. And I can go more quickly, because I think the Claremont people are the one who really — that's their identity, right? And the others, it's not. And so it's much more explicit rejection of some of that.

Patrick Deneen 与后自由主义者

So you have someone like Patrick Deneen. Here I classify him as a post-liberal, and I bundle him with Sohrab Ahmari and Adrian Vermeule at Harvard. And there's these different figures in that world and Gladden Pappin, who was until recently in Hungary. But these are figures who have a pretty deep critique of liberal democracy that I've kind of — I've already said something about that. So they have this critique of liberal democracy as individualizing, and so forth.

And for Patrick Deneen, that is what the American founding represents. He's very explicit: you know, this was a liberal founding. This was an Enlightenment project. And his idea is like — and that's bound to fail. That's going to eat away at itself. That's going to have this cratering effect. And so we need to — it's kind of an anti-modern inflection that he offers, which is like: we've got to shift gears completely and have — now he's talking about regime change. And so that's a pretty radical.

You know, I don't know what he's saying these days about the Declaration, but he's pretty explicit, I think, that he doesn't like this stuff. He doesn't like individual rights. He doesn't like the individualizing dimensions of it. And he thinks it's too weak and thin a foundation for a healthy political order.

Adrian Vermeule 与"共同善宪法学"

And Adrian Vermeule, though, does something very different, which I think is interesting because he is offering — I think he agrees with a lot of what Deneen says, but his argument as well is — and he doesn't say much about the founding, but he talks about jurisprudence and modes of interpretation and constitutionalism.

And so he's got this book called Common Good Constitutionalism, where his argument is against originalism. And so it's against this thin way of understanding the founding documents as a text or a kind of sort of understanding that we can recover and then base our judgments on in politics. He says, well, no, there's a thicker background here that we've forgotten.

And he's also against progressive living constitutionalism. I mean, both — like Kesler's founding and those are both out. He says there's actually beyond this, there's a truer and deeper foundation to all of this.

I think — he doesn't explicitly say it's not an Enlightenment document, but he kind of does. He says this is the old English tradition of common law plus natural law, classical legal tradition brought in from the Christian world. There's a kind of natural law philosophy here. This is the background against which the Constitution was built up.

There are principles that are more important than liberty and freedom, and there's — you know, there's like the common good — and he gives this six-fold definition of that — and he says those are the true orienting principles. That's the true forgotten foundation for our law. And we need to recover that.

And that gives basically — it's a very short book, so he's trying to do a lot with it — but it's like that basically gives this whole new way of understanding how judges might make rulings on cases, how politicians might organize their thoughts in terms of new legislation and what it aims for. And it's a rejection of liberal individualism. But that's claiming that the original Constitution also didn't ascribe to those things in nearly the way that our tradition tells us it has.

So that's very different. And I think it's a little — I think it's fudging some things. I'll just put my cards on the table. So I don't think that's true of the American tradition. I think Deneen's closer to the truth. It's a mixed bag. Again, it was contentious. There's different traditions being embraced. But I think the idea that it's just this one thing that Vermeule says it is is not accurate historically.

Yoram Hazony 与国家保守主义

And then you have somebody like Yoram Hazony of the NatCon tradition. Okay. So the NatCons is National Conservatism, and in my thinking this is really the organizing body for the MAGA New Right. This is a person who is a great organizer. He's also a political theorist, right? But he's an Israeli-American. He's built institutions. He was someone who founded the Princeton Tory or something — one of these journals when he's young — just like Peter Thiel did at Stanford. I mean, you've got these guys who are political actors.

And he's been very involved in trying to organize this ideological side of MAGA since, you know, 2017, 2019 — I guess some of the first conferences were. So he's been hosting these conferences. People from the different camps will come to talk about policy.

But he published a book in 2018 called The Virtue of Nationalism. And this book — you know, it's trying to restore the idea of nationalism, like capital-N Nationalism, as opposed to patriotism or what have you. And it's the idea being that a robust politics needs — you need people with a shared history, shared language, shared religion, and sort of monolithic, and against, again, liberal pluralism.

But he grounds this in the Hebrew Bible, in a totally different sort of — I mean, he doesn't invent, but he sort of really exaggerates or tries to recover this Protestant — well, this older liberal — this not liberal — this older tradition of political philosophy that's kind of more grounded and rooted in cultural things and particulars and nationalistic particulars. And it's a rejection of modern individual rights theory. So this is like the anti-Locke doctrine. It's like modern liberal theory completely underestimates the extent to which you need all these background features.

And so it's kind of similar to Vermeule and Deneen in a way. It's like: we need something much more robust, built and founded in actual communities that have grown up organically. But I mean, that tips pretty fast into blood and soil. I mean, he denies that. But then you also have the book kind of chronicles how that has tipped into inviting bizarre figures to their conferences and a kind of ethno-nationalist — ethno-nationalists are very explicit about being very attracted to the NatCon sensibility. And this movement he's trying to build is connected to the far right around the world.

So you have that. But that's a very clear rejection of liberal individualism and of sort of anything that we think about — if we agree that the Declaration was inspired at least in part by early modern political thought and then these ideas of consent of the governed, which I don't think you can read the Declaration and not — you know, that's not a projection, right? Consent of the governed, individual rights to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. I mean, that stuff — you can't take Locke out of that.

And so they're really trying to muffle that part of the American tradition and reorient things in a new direction. And so that bleeds into these debates we're hearing where you have JD Vance talking about "heritage Americans." You have somebody like Neil Gorsuch finding that he needs to go defend the idea of America being a creedal nation.

Yeah, that's surprising, I think. I wouldn't have 10 years ago thought that that would have been something that a conservative Supreme Court justice would have had to do. So that can maybe give us some insight into actually what's happening in our world right now around some of these issues.

And it's striking to me. Like I said, I have encountered a few people who don't even seem to really be aware that that's where we're at — that you have suddenly a need to defend the idea of America as a creedal nation. And you suddenly have so much of this really imminent danger and threat to birthright citizenship that is core to American history. I mean, there really is no way around that.

And I think the institutional decisions at the founding, the deliberate decisions and more political documents and institutional decisions that were — you know, the separation of church and state and other features of the —

Kristen: Right. And also just the background here of like religious warfare in Europe, right? Like that matters too.

Laura: There's just a lot that matters that they kind of underplay or try to draw out other things.

Kristen: Those arguments, not necessarily having to rely on the authority of the founding of it when the history of it does not fully point in that direction.

Laura: Well, right. I mean, the founding matters as a historical thing that we understand in its own time. I'm going to use the tech right thing as a moment to pivot to Lincoln, because I've been thinking quite a bit about Lincoln and this Dred Scott ruling. And it's a little — I think it's a little tendentious what I'm about to do — but I think I have a good point here.

Lincoln 论《独立宣言》

So he says about the Declaration — he says:

"I think the authors of that notable instrument intended to include all men, but they did not mean to declare all men equal in all respects. They did not mean to say that all men were created equal in size, color, intellect, moral development, or social capacity. They defined with tolerable distinctness in what they did consider all men created equal — equal in certain inalienable rights, among which are life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. This they said, and this they meant."

Okay. Now it sounds here like he's defending this idea of some kind of equality but not full equality, for even maybe even with regards to color and stuff. Like it sounds like a pretty limited defense here, right? Very thin defense. And Michael Anton uses this in one of his essays, right, to defend the idea that Lincoln didn't really believe in equality the way we think he did.

But then he goes on. And this is the part that Michael Anton consistently avoids, I think. So here he says:

"They did not mean to assert the obvious untruth that all were then actually enjoying that equality, or yet that they were about to confer it immediately upon them."

Again, a note of realism, right? Like they weren't saying this had actually arrived. He says:

"In fact, they had no power to confer such a boon."

Okay. But this is then he says this:

"They meant simply to declare the right, so that the enforcement of it might follow as fast as circumstances should permit."

So, I mean, that's a pretty — I mean, it's a little vague, maybe abstract, but I think it's beautiful. And I think I first read that in Harry Jaffa's book, because he also prizes this statement. And that's a very expansive understanding of what the Declaration is doing.

And then he goes on even further. He says — let me say — this is where I think he fudges something. Okay, I think we can all appreciate that was beautiful. He says:

"The assertion that all men are created equal was of no practical use in affecting our separation from Great Britain."

Which is, I think, shocking, right? And he says:

"And it was placed in the Declaration not for that, but for future use."

Here he says:

"Its authors meant it to be — thank God, it is now proving itself — a stumbling block to those who in aftertimes might seek to turn a free people back into the hateful paths of despotism."

And here's the part — this is my favorite part, which will allow me to pivot to the tech bros. Okay, he says:

"They knew the proneness of prosperity to breed tyrants. And they meant when such should reappear in this fair land and commence their vocation, they should find left for them at least one hard nut to crack."

Again, he's talking there about — I mean, he's talking about what Lincoln later has called the slave power and King Cotton. The next summer there will be — or like the next year, there will be this essay about King Cotton that basically argues that the economy of cotton and slavery is so powerful that nothing will break it. And that it's also sort of embedded in nature — that Black people are sort of meant to toil, that some parts of humanity are just meant to toil. It's really awful stuff.

But then, so Lincoln is concentrating on this sheer amount of wealth and money that is behind the slave movement.

类比不完美，但结构相似

And I am not trying to say that the tech bros are like slaveholders or that there's a clear analogy in any way. But I think that there's a lesson here about the accumulation of money and power and how that works in politics. And that Lincoln has some things to say about that. It's not straightforward. I'm not saying that the founders were all arguing for economic equality or equality of outcomes, but that there's a problem with an incredible amount of accumulation of wealth because it turns into a kind of despotism and it attracts tyrants.

So — in terms of the tech side of things, it's not really — I realized in writing the book that to do justice to all of that was not going to be in my capacity in the time frame I had to write it. So it's in there a little bit.

Kristen: Yeah, oh yeah, yeah, yeah.

And they, in the process, set aside the tricky problem of slavery. AI、硅谷与个人自主性的丧失

And so I agree with you. We should not oversimplify or, you know, obviously I don't mean to malign people in Silicon Valley by comparing them to people who own slaves, by any means. I really again just really want to emphasize that's not what I think is useful about this analogy.

It's still — it's an analogy of what do you do with entrenched special interest groups, especially when it's not just wealth — which I agree with that kind of framing there — but it's also wealth that is dependent on a particular kind of institutional political-economic policies, and also creating those policies, also lobbying for the government to have certain policies that benefit — that may be impinging upon the autonomy of individuals, right? So you could also put it in this way.

I mean, people feel very frustrated with AI, just as an example, for a lot of reasons. I mean, honestly, I think a lot of the issues we take with it come from — I think they resemble problems we were already having in society with privacy and data collection on social media platforms and the internet more broadly. But AI, I think, brings it to a whole other level, where people feel that they're — material, anything that they post online, things that they've published, authors suing tech companies because their copyrighted materials are being used to make incredible returns for these companies. And the companies at the same time, and maybe this will not be the case, but at the same time are kind of pitching their products as a way of replacing or kind of substituting for human-generated knowledge. Right.

And so how is it that people — if they're being told that there is this very frightening economy that's coming to play and also presumably this does reduce the incentives for people to generate knowledge on their own originally. And there's all these issues in the publishing world with people. You know, you can't — if you use an LLM to generate your book, you can't copyright that. So what are you going to do? Right. We have these incredible problems. Yeah.

And an ordinary person is going to look at major tech companies that are valued at enormous amounts of money and also seem to be getting closed-door meetings at the White House. And all this wrapped up — you know, power on the social kind of economic side, getting wrapped up and having more access to the political side of things, especially at the executive level, especially as power is getting wielded mostly by the executive rather than by Congress.

For the ordinary person, how are you supposed to look at that and not feel as though you are being pushed aside?
