Evan: A note for the wider AI audience that probably resonates both with practitioners and users: obviously something that's blossomed in the past six months is agents. And we love agents.

RJ: Who doesn't?

Evan: Necessary but not sufficient conditions. We all remember what agents were like, let's say, mid last year. Let's just say there is positive value and there is negative value. And both can be amplified by agents. Why? Agents are only as useful as the underlying models they're orchestrating. Let's think about coding. If your coding model even makes subtle but real bugs, your agents are just going to amplify those issues, and you're going to end up with not only slop but something that may be anti-useful that gives the user incorrect information. We all remember what that age was like mid last year — that made a lot of people lose patience with claims about LLMs for agent engineering. Something changed — clearly a threshold was met. And even though these models are still not perfect, the utility of LLMs for software engineering are so obvious now.

You can draw a direct analogy from that to what we're talking about here. We're working on an agentic platform for 24/7 drug discovery. You can imagine fleets of hundreds of med-chemists and CAD scientists working nights and weekends all the time for your drug targets. The codename for that gem is Sapphire. The prerequisite for that was: we needed the underlying models for pose, 3D complex prediction, potency, ADMET to all be good enough for an agent using these models 24/7 to create molecules that medicinal chemists would actually want to make and not laugh at.

If your model is sitting at 1.8, 1.9 RMSD — that's slop. Let me be really direct about it. You're hearing a bimodal distribution from people of the utility of a 3D pose and how real it is. The reality is that for a highly potent ligand, almost certainly there is a large portion of that molecule with a very well-defined 3D position, down to even half an angstrom. If you don't believe me, you can open up the electron density diagram in the PDB. It's all available online and you can literally see in some cases aromatic rings with missing density in the middle. So it's not just a construct on a blackboard that your organic chemistry professor showed you. You can literally see a donut, a torus of electron density for an aromatic ring.

However, there's going to be solvent-exposed areas oftentimes that will be less important for binding affinity but maybe important for solubility or other properties. Those sometimes are less defined because in reality they're more dynamic — they're flopping around in solvent. But the critical piece as an upstream indicator that's valuable for predicting the free energy of binding is to get the core of your molecule that specifically interacts with the protein to be correct to sub-angstrom resolution.

Why does this matter intuitively? A hydrogen bond — everyone in the audience has probably heard about a hydrogen bond. It's the most critical form of non-covalent interaction. It's how nucleic acids are held together. It's how most ligands and proteins interact. It's how proteins form secondary structures. Hydrogen bonds have a very specific angle and distance. The distance from the donor to the acceptor heavy atom is 2.7 angstroms to 3.3 angstroms. If I do my math right, that's a 0.6-angstrom gap. Outside of that, it's not a hydrogen bond. If it's less than that, it's a clash. If it's more than that, the interaction is much weaker pretty quickly. An angstrom, for those who don't know, is one-tenth of a nanometer.

Drug discovery really is a science of resolution. If your accuracy is not sufficient, it will therefore not be useful for the downstream things that you care about — which is both potency prediction but also prospective design: what molecule do I make next? The history of innovation in startups is that the ones that do best are ones that focus on one well-defined but very important problem. We think our ability to get higher-resolution predictions stems from our judicious focus on small/medium-sized molecule design rather than boiling the ocean.

怎么做到 1Å？一个无聊到刺眼的答案

Brandon: So that's the what and the why. What about the how — how did you get to one angstrom and sub-one-angstrom?

Sergey: I'm going to give you an extremely boring answer. Which I think is actually true for the entire AI field. Three things matter in AI: data, infrastructure, and evals. You can only improve what you measure. Once you are very careful about measuring what matters and you have really talented people on the team, we're going to figure out how to hill-climb that measure.

From the start, we actually focused on less than one-angstrom precision, and that led to a bunch of small decisions in the process that compound. If your team never looks into this metric at all, then you will never train a model that is good at it. And if you're constantly looking into that, then you're going to achieve that.

RJ: So it's the right objective plus good science.

Sergey: And it propagates everywhere through the whole stack. It propagates to how you look at the data — how do we filter out data? Some data is more noisy, so maybe we don't need to see it, or maybe you don't need to see it later in the training. It propagates through your modeling architecture, propagates through your loss.

Brandon: How long have you been working towards this specific goal? This is not something that you broadly hear in the community talk about wanting. People have decided that two angstroms is the canonical benchmark, and I've never heard someone say one before is the cutoff until reading things coming out of Genesis. How direct has this focus been in the evolution of the company?

Sergey: I think one of our most important secrets is that we are working on real drug programs — either with partners or in-house. When you actually work on real drug programs, you see the failure modes — what works and what doesn't work. It's pretty obvious when you see the outputs of real programs, what kind of failure modes are happening. It becomes very obvious that two-angstrom is just not working out.

Brandon: But there are a lot of really smart med-chemists who also think very carefully about benchmarks and who have prosecuted actual drug discovery programs. Why hasn't this become part of the community? Is this literally just that the community has never been able to succeed at winning a benchmark — that it kind of settled at "okay, two-angstrom is something we can aspire to"?

In my experience with pharma, there are plenty of problems where there's kind of these known things amongst the technical experts in a sub-domain, but it doesn't get out of pharma. Some of the information is proprietary and gets passed from company to company but never really released into the public. Is this your estimation of what's going on?

SWE-Bench 类比与评估危机

Evan: Have you ever heard of SWE-Bench? Gemini does pretty well in SWE-Bench. Sometimes Gemini publishes models that win on some of those software benchmarks. Raise your hand if you're using Gemini to write code right now instead of, you know, the obvious other-name competitors.

RJ: Not me.

Evan: No one. Why would you do that? It's obviously worse in practice.

In this case, if you look at the provenance of how that happened: RMSD-less-than-two came originally from — and I've been in the space forever — from docking studies, long before AI models for pose prediction, when physics-based docking studies from academic institutions [were dominant]. Because usually large proprietary software makers didn't want to benchmark their methods against other methods, so academics had to get licenses and try it. Then they'd introduce "RMSD less than two," which is not surprising — they're academics, they're not using these things to make drugs, they're using it to write papers. That got repurposed by the AI community.

But the actual trend is much more in the direction we're saying. The first big innovation there was PoseBusters, put out by a lab at University of Oxford, which pointed out that RMSD itself is insufficient and we need to look at physical validity as well. So Oxford improved that. In the latest release from OpenBind — we just published our benchmarks at Genesis on OpenBind, we'll talk about that in a bit — if you look at their original publication, their default metrics are using RMSD, PoseBusters validity, and LDDT. There's clearly an acknowledgement already made that RMSD less than two is insufficient, and the field is now rapidly evolving to acknowledge that.

Brandon: You think that the academic literature is going to establish some benchmark — maybe it's one angstrom, maybe it's some LDDT or some other metric — that will converge, and then hopefully as a community drive forward more accurate modeling strategies?

Evan: I would say there was an eval crisis in our field that is now in transition right now. Our field was previously a lot more quiet, but if you go to NeurIPS and ICML every year, the workshops get a lot more crowded. Those evals are in transition now that we realize the flaws of what came before.

公司的演化：从 PotentialNet 到生成式时代

Brandon: Pick the right evals and then just do good machine learning. But I picked up on something Evan said earlier — you started out with PotentialNet, this graph-based network, and then you did a lot of computational simulations, and now you're sort of going back into generative modeling. I'm curious about how that evolution worked. Did computational lead back into generative? Did you start collecting data to build a generative computational data as a data augmentation? What's the history?

Evan: I will say that the last line of PyTorch I've written is much further back in history than the most recent line of PyTorch that Sergey has committed. I want to make sure he gives his opinion as well.

I want to make sure I address something you said a little bit of time ago that I think kind of got lost — you asked about how we do other things including ADMET prediction. I can spend all day diving into details about Pearl and its evolution. I just want to give a shout-out to the fact that it is one important pillar but not the only pillar that matters in the trajectory of a drug discovery campaign.

You mentioned that PotentialNet paper, which we're happy to see had become influential since you and I were working in the space when it was very much in the future. Now it is the future. Another paper we published around the same time was on neural networks for ADMET prediction. We published two, actually — ADMET, can you —

Brandon: Oh sorry, thank you — absorption, distribution, metabolism, elimination, and toxicity are the five prongs that comprise ADMET. These are all the properties you talked about before that you have to get right to make a drug successful.

Evan: Correct. There's over 30 or so assays, each of which — if you're a neural net person, like a multitask neural network or multi-head — has to predict over three-dozenish properties. Each of which, if it's in the wrong range, means your molecule is not a drug, it's just a tool. Things like solubility (which Sergey mentioned, important for formulation — as in, can it be made into a pill you can take orally), oral bioavailability, whether or not you're inhibiting certain enzymes called the cytochrome P450s and its different variants, hERG — a critical channel which if you inhibit too much can cause cardiotoxicity. These are an alphabet soup of things that most people here haven't heard of.

Brandon: And a lot of these are extremely hard specifically because it's not a single causal effect. There are often many processes, many pathways which are involved in defining this one endpoint.

Evan: Correct.

Brandon: And data sets are oftentimes comically small, unless you have pharma to help you out. At least in open source, it's a hard problem.

Evan: It's sparse in the public domain. There's actually a range from really directly predictable properties to ones that, as you point out, are actually amalgams of other signaling events. Whether or not you're inhibiting CYP3A4 — that's really specific, it's a certain protein that you're inhibiting. So that's something that Pearl, for example, could actually model and expect to see useful prediction.

We've been doing 3D work on ADMET prediction before anyone else was. There was a bunch of work that ended up launching the company back in the day when AI could still be in peer-reviewed journals — not just random white papers on arXiv. We published MoleculeNet, which has been cited a few thousand times at some point. We also published this paper showing that multitask graph neural networks were the best at the time for doing ADMET prediction on large pharma data sets. That paper is one of the most cited papers on AI for ADMET ever now.

Our history from the beginning has been to work on not just one problem but to focus on drug discovery, and in so doing being able to have the bandwidth to focus on building all of the ML models that are needed for drug discovery — and not get distracted by the biological discovery processes that are needed (target ID side, or the clinical trial side) but really focused on all those tasks required for drug discovery, and also molecular generation. These are all important.

湿实验闭环与 RL：与 Incyte 的天作之合

Brandon: I want to talk about kind of the feedback between how your computation, your ML, and how wet-lab data went into developing this model. We talked about priors. It's been typically hard to scale protein–ligand models well in a way which meaningfully generalizes, and this recent Pearl paper has shown true generalization. How do these different aspects feed together to give you this power?

Evan: There's this classic concept of the narrative fallacy where in retrospect everything seems obvious and you can draw really linear processes of how we got from point A to point Z, but the reality is always more interesting and much messier and much more nonlinear. For Sergey and I, who've been training or taming neural nets for over a decade, we remember a lot of different eras. We would have been so excited if we could tell ourselves the capabilities of our technologies 10 years ago.

The concept of using generative AI for the molecular space is not a new concept, but the reduction-to-practice would have been very difficult a decade ago. I used to co-run the Stanford AI Salon — we had fun organizing, you know, it's Stanford in the Gates Computer Science Building, and we'd get some interesting speakers and like 25 people having wine and cheese, and they're all now like just straight-up famous people. Back then it was a much smaller field.

I remember very clearly in like 2017–2018 talking about GANs, and how they're clearly the future of image generation. There was a lot of work then on "well, can we just apply GANs to produce conformations of proteins, or to produce protein–ligand poses?" For all the same reasons those models were really tricky to train for images — mode collapse was the most famous problem — they didn't work very well for proteins or protein–ligand systems. We had to wait for the right primitive to be created, and that turned out to be diffusion.

Interestingly, a lot of image and video models — some are using diffusion, but some of those have actually gone autoregressive. What's kind of cool is right now for people interested in really core fundamental AI research, actually some of the most innovative diffusion research is happening in our field, in 3D structure prediction. No one would have predicted that then, but now that's a pillar of diffusion, I'd say. That was unpredictable 10 years ago.

In parallel to that, long before the advent of 3D diffusion models for generative tasks in chemistry, we were building a variety of tools for the problems of drug discovery at hand. Some were using physics-based methods for predicting potency or even for helping predict certain ADMET properties. Same with molecular generation — using different techniques to generate new molecular ideas. As Sergey pointed out, there are 10^60 drug-like molecules. Searching that space efficiently is hard.

Those things happened to be available when we wanted to take what was then the very nascent area of co-folding — which was clearly exciting but not at all ready for prime time, not at all ready for what a medicinal chemist would want to use in their day-to-day — and take it to the realm of useful, and in some cases irreplaceable, like clearly superior to a non-co-folding method. We had been building those other primitives, so they were available to us. We could put them together to build out, for example, the synthetic data pipeline or to use some of the inference-time techniques. We were ready to do those rapidly because we had been working on orthogonal techniques.

It's like almost any discovery — like the discovery of penicillin. I'm not saying what we're doing is on the same order of magnitude of benefit to humanity as penicillin, but there's always this element of: if you're laser-focused on a problem for long enough and spend enough time in the lab — or in our case on the computer banging your head against the wall in these problems — you can create the luck that will enable some of the developments.

RL 的雏形与 Incyte 合作

RJ: How does the lab interact with the development process?

Sergey: As I mentioned before, we train other models — not just structure prediction models — and for those specific models, lab outputs are extremely useful right away, like potency prediction. Those outputs can be used directly for model training. But what I'm most excited about going forward is reinforcement learning. I think that's coming in our field, and we have seen early signs of it working already with our models.

Initially, maybe physics-based feedback to improve your models through RL — typical RL loop. But eventually you can go all the way down to lab-in-the-loop setups where your model is not only producing predictions, but you synthesize based on those predictions and then you measure downstream properties and feed back those predictions into that model.

RJ: So how do you get enough volume? Do you have automation to do that, or do you have large campaigns that aren't necessarily automated but generate a lot of data?

Sergey: One thing I'm super proud of is our partnership with Incyte. They're such an amazing company. They are extremely good at producing data — basically taking the compounds, generating them, creating them, and then measuring the downstream properties and sending those results back. This is such a partnership born in heaven for us between Genesis and Incyte, where we are able to train models, give predictions, and have results back from Incyte super quickly.

RJ: So your rollouts include a lab iteration essentially.

Sergey: Yeah.

RJ: That's amazing.

Sergey: And diffusion is slow, so it's about the same speed sometimes. Give it a week.

Evan: It is true. I'm a big believer that companies are typically really good at one or two things and do best when they can focus on it. In the same way we've been just doggedly focused on developing the best AI models for drug discovery, Incyte has that level of maniacal focus on optimizing drug discovery and development.

Another trend people like to talk about is the rise of China in biotech. It's the elephant in the room. So many western companies have come to rely more on CROs to do a lot of their wet lab work — Incyte became state-of-the-art in terms of the experimental capabilities they had in-house. Their productivity is extremely high. It's a match made in heaven for us because what we thrive on is continuous learning of the models. We want to have design-make-test-analyze cycles that are as rapid as possible, and continuously fine-tune (in some cases retrain) the models based on what we see in the lab.

That partnership is one of, if not the first ever, that enables that sort of true joint foundation model training on historical and also prospective data. As ML practitioners, we all know data is just such a critical input — the critical ingredient. It's extremely exciting for us, and that range, reflecting our conversation so far, goes from structure, potency, and a variety of ADMET properties as well. It immediately will improve the strength of our models and be really powerful for generally accelerating drug discovery.

自动化湿实验的真实限制

RJ: We were joking around about the time that it takes to do diffusion, but how long does it take? You email them with "here's 10, 100 compounds" — and they get back to you with measurements of those realized compounds in a day, a week, a month, an hour?

Evan: It depends. Some compounds are really easy to knock out — really well-characterized reactions where the usual conditions just work. Sometimes it's "oh, this coupling actually didn't work like the literature said it would, and we have to try different conditions." I'm saying this so the audience understands that for all the claims out there about robotic labs automating synthesis, the reality is just a lot more complicated than that.

RJ: Sorry, what are some of the complications there? They all do some form of automation. What goes wrong with automated labs?

Evan: This is also one of the reasons, as Sergey mentioned, we're really excited about reinforcement learning, because it circumvents the problems of having to do very fast design-make-test-analyze cycles from the lab to feed back into the model. Ideally we can throw more and more GPUs at the problem and have the model self-train, in the same way we once did for board games in the late 2010s or most recently for coding. That's a key North Star for us.

However, before anyone tells you that there will be a country of geniuses in a data room just solving drug discovery — there are some problems in our space that don't lend themselves to RL as naturally and that will require experimental intervention. I myself spent a fair bit of time in the wet lab as a very mediocre experimental scientist before I found my true joy back in graduate school when I could just focus on coding all day.

In order to make a new molecule you have to synthesize it. That means different chemical reagents and catalysts and temperatures and solvents coming together in the right way in the right protocol to try to make your new molecule. Once you do that, you need to purify it. If you don't, your positive or negative assay readout could be an artifact. You need to characterize it — use NMR and other techniques like mass spec to indicate that what you made in your vial is what you actually sought to make in the first place. That's not as trivial as it sounds either. That's not only true for us, by the way — for anyone doing material science for AI, "did I make what I actually made?" is actually not a trivial question. It's easier in small molecule drug discovery than in materials, but still non-trivial and takes time.

We've seen so many cases where screenings of large molecules sounds great on paper because you can test millions, billions of compounds in one go, so you have more shots on goal — and as a bonus you have data for your model. Okay, the reality is that the translation of high-throughput screens, whether it's DEL (DNA-encoded libraries) or more traditional screens, to the actual business of resynthesizing a molecule de novo and doing a low-throughput high-fidelity experiment — that R² is shockingly low. That false positive rate is enormous for a variety of reasons. That's one of the many reasons why people are so excited about AI making those predictions, because in theory they can be even cleaner than a lot of the wet lab work, especially the high-throughput work.

RJ: Why can it be more? Is it just because the experiments go wrong a lot? Even with a very precise robot doing it, are there just too many variables, too much slop in the system?

Evan: It's a few things. One is that drug discovery frequently involves finding the outliers. One of the many aspects that's so wild about our space is that the properties one is seeking in a molecule very often anti-correlate. Binding tends to improve the more hydrophobic, the more greasy your compound is — because protein binding pockets are greasy. Guess what? That makes your solubility worse. Then you want to improve your solubility often by adding polarity to your compound — and suddenly, for those that remember high school biology, cells are surrounded by lipid bilayers. Your molecule might not get through the cell membrane because you made it too polar.

The properties you want often anti-correlate, and that's where multi-parameter optimization of molecules ends up feeling like playing whack-a-mole. You're often searching for real outliers, and that often requires making molecules that are fundamentally novel. The kinds of chemistries that today can be automated are fairly constrained. Your ability to search chemical space broadly for those really top-Pareto-optimal compounds is very limited. The benefits you get in speed have a very harsh trade-off with the actual quality and novelty of molecules that you can make.

RJ: So you can do a lot of boring stuff very quickly.

Evan: If you're lucky.

Sergey: Yeah, if you're lucky.

RJ: But to actually get to the cutting edge questions, it's hard to get a robotic system to do those kinds of things.

Evan: Yes. We would love for that to happen. It'd be a huge tailwind for what we do, but it's not available today.

Incyte 的纵向整合

RJ: What is Incyte's approach that makes them so fast and effective?

Evan: If one is willing to focus on one problem and say no to distractions, it's amazing what can happen. Their talent density is very very high, and they're willing to — what is not universally done now in large or medium-sized pharma — build so many experimental capabilities in-house. I actually think that it's possible for more companies to try to do that, given the global dynamic of pharmaceutical development that's changed so dramatically.

Brandon: Right — so CROs taking over everything has now suddenly become commoditized, and your capability to do the cutting-edge experiments goes down.

Evan: It's been a debate for hundreds of years about the benefits of vertical integration. It comes with all the areas of risk and challenges and upfront cost, but has all the benefits of controlling the process end-to-end. When it works, you can do amazing things when you do things end-to-end. It comes with greater challenges and an appetite needed to do it.

从 Genesis Therapeutics 到 Genesis Molecular AI：身份的澄清

Brandon: This brings me to another question. You guys — so maybe now's a good time to talk about your pivot. There's a name change from Genesis Therapeutics to Genesis Molecular AI, and that goes hand-in-hand with maybe a pivot in the strategy. What it sounds like to me is "we're going to focus on the AI portion and sell that to pharma, rather than be a drug company that also sells the tool." Is that accurate?

Evan: When we started the company, the origin of the company was fundamental deep learning research methods we had developed at Stanford. The objective we were trying to solve for was: how can we have the greatest impact for patients? At the time, there was no real precedent for a pure model company really making it in the realm of just creating value for partners in pharma, or really most legacy industries. The other component was: the company being founded on AI research, we really wanted to show people that we were serious about the biotech domain.

Brandon: You seem a lot more serious to all the tried-and-true folks in pharma — the dyed-in-the-wool med chemists, drug developers — who we really wanted to work with.

Evan: There was an element of an attempt at nominative determinism there. But also an acknowledgement that I don't think anyone had really shown at that time that you could be a pure AI model company and really make it in the space. That was obviously — this is 2019 — we had the benefit of being real forebears, pioneers in the space. We had the downside of being pretty early.

As the company grew and evolved, we really were structured kind of like a double helix in that we had extremely strong, focused AI research, and we were fortunate to be able to recruit some of the most experienced and accomplished drug hunters — people on our management team and board who have discovered, co-invented, developed many FDA-approved drugs. You talk to most med-chemists, most feel very fortunate if they're able to work on one molecule that gets FDA approval in their whole career. We were really fortunate to convince these accomplished drug hunters to come join our management team and board at the beginning and steer the show.

The outcome has always been the same: everything we're talking about, it only matters if we end up helping patients and their families at the end of the day. We have felt from the beginning that the way to do that at scale is to primarily partner with large and medium-sized pharma companies and biotech companies. So they can do what they're best at — novel biology, clinical development — and we can do what we're best at — AI — and bring those two together.

In addition to that, as Sergey has said, there are numerous advantages to having in-house wet lab data generation. Clearly dogfooding the models, getting really direct human feedback from real med chemists on what we need to build. And the third thing is that the single most valuable thing in the universe — what I like to joke is the Mirror of Erised — if you ask people what is the one thing you most want, and you really thought about it, most people would say a medicine for themselves or a family member. There is just immense intrinsic value to having one's own pipeline programs.

When we interact with pharmaceutical partners, on average they love that we're working on molecules ourselves, because it means we're not just a bunch of keyboard jockeys — we live the dream that we are pitching, and we need to use these models in practice for ourselves too. So they're battle-tested in an additional way, and we're sort of domesticated in that we know what's really difficult about the space. We're not just selling some pipe dream.

RJ: It seems to me there's been a shift in the last year to two years where suddenly pharma has become interested in buying tools off the shelf. Whereas previously, a lot of companies started trying to build AI and then had trouble and became pharma companies because that's the only way they could make money — you raise a bunch of money and hope that in 10 years you have a drug. And now that's changing — you have a lot of recent AI acquisitions in drug discovery and pathology. Have you seen this shift, and is that part of why you're shifting toward selling models? What's going on?

Evan: To finish that narrative arc: the name change was just to reflect what we were really doing in practice — which is we are an AI company, as we have been from day one when we were coding in PyTorch 0.17, building, you know — when Sergey was scaling transformers on PyTorch, the first one to do that — we were scaling graph neural nets in PyTorch, one of the first ones to do that. It's reflecting what our identity has been from day one. But also alluding to the fact that we are very serious molecule makers ourselves. We are full-stack in that way.

Our mission is to create as many medicines as possible as would otherwise not have been created. I think the way to get there is to put our technology directly into the hands of as many drug developers as possible — not just our own, but in big pharma, midcap pharma, and biotech. That's partially why we've been building our agents, so that our models — which have become so much more powerful — can be easily used and orchestrated in the hands of med chemists and CAD scientists who might have never written a line of code in their life.

智能体的崛起：从工具到代理

Brandon: I find it interesting to hear that you have now started bringing in agents into your development process. From the very get-go, your philosophy was much more "these are tools for scientists." Now you are switching and saying "we are actually going to have automated systems which make decisions." You did allude to maybe hitting the threshold like last November where it just magically became useful. Are there other reasons for going in this direction, and why now?

Sergey: Well, let me ask you a question in return. How many tools do you think a human med-chemist or CAD scientist can realistically learn and be proficient in?

Brandon: Oh, how many can they learn and how many do they use? Because I've seen that they like to use lots of them, but maybe they're not necessarily good with —

Sergey: But it's really hard, right? A lot of the tools are extremely complex. They have a lot of parameters that you need to set properly and configure them. This is where agents are extremely good at. They actually know how to use all of the tools and how to orchestrate them well.

Brandon: I wouldn't interrupt you, but when you say "agent" — some people like using the term agent to just mean something automated, but other people use agent to specifically mean you have an LLM which is responsible for orchestrating decisions. Outside of biotech, it's almost universally the latter. But sometimes in the space of bio, people are still using "agents" to refer to any decision-based system which is automated out of humans, and maybe some of them are more like traditional rules-based systems.

Sergey: In our case, it's the AI definition of agents — an LLM which is able to use all of the tools that we have been painstakingly building over the many years of existence of this company. That's a very powerful concept. Now as a CAD scientist or med chemist, you don't need to deeply understand all the details and all the hyperparameters you need to set — you can just let this thing go and figure out and solve the problems.

This is where all the previous questions we discussed come back as very important. Agents need to be able to understand what these models are producing. Why do we care about crystal structure? Well, the agent can actually look at your predicted crystal structure and make decisions based on that.

RJ: You're saying you can take a crystal structure — which I guess in this case is framed as a graph or like a three-dimensional image that Mol* is interpreting — and it actually can make decisions upon what is binding to what and make hypotheses?

Sergey: There are definitely multiple layers to this question. You can start with basic image understanding, or you can use additional tools to understand what is happening in the crystal structure, or you can even train a model which is able to natively understand crystal structure representation. Image modality is a modality for LLMs these days; crystal structure can be a modality for LLMs as well. I can fine-tune it on sequences that are somehow capturing tokens that are 3D crystal structures.

Cursor 的轨迹是预言

RJ: If you historically have treated humans like you were making tools for humans, now you have agents. How do you reimagine the interaction of humans and agents and the decision-making process? Do you hope to essentially automate humans out? And how do you make sure that your agents are not making weird decisions where they tweaked the wrong hyperparameter and now you give the human a bunch of —

Sergey: It's a great question. The answer here you can see in how coding tools have evolved. You remember Cursor when it just appeared and it was top autocomplete. It was still useful but not to the extent it is useful right now, where I can fire up Cursor and it just goes and does everything for me. We're going to see a very similar trajectory here.

Now those agents are already useful, but humans need to provide consistent feedback and basically steer and guide this agent along the way. Over time we're going to become more independent. Still, I don't believe in full automation, replacing humans — I believe humans are going to become way more efficient in the job that they're doing using the tools. A human will be providing strategic direction to what this agent needs to do, and the agent is going to be able to go and execute on behalf of a human.

文明的弧线与医学的意义

Evan: I think this is the best time ever to be a creative human being, or really to be any human. The long arc of history, starting from when we decided — "I don't know about that hunting and gathering, I think I'm going to settle down and do this whole agriculture thing" — I think that started thousands of years of drudgery. Sometimes you wonder why did we actually do agriculture, because everything I've seen seems like agriculture was probably a net loss in the short term.

The only benefit I see in the long run is that if you were a hunter-gatherer and you got sick or were born with a genetic illness or got injured, you were in trouble. There's not much we could do for you. Medicine just didn't really exist. Ultimately I think the crowning achievement in my mind of civilization is where the sick people among us in society are not seen as burdens, but people we want to help. That isn't true in the history of the animal kingdom, including humans. It's a sad reality but it's true. Now, because of medicine, we see people who are sick as people we want to help, who we can help, who could become even more productive members of society.

If you fast-forward that to now: so much of creative work, day-to-day work — if you look at it as a pie chart, time is our most precious resource — so much of it was being taken up by repetitive tasks that actually didn't require much thought or creativity. Now we've created this additional headspace for deep thinking. For us, you're solving some of the most technically challenging but important problems. We can use these tools to become wildly more productive and creative. That goes to the med chemists, drug hunters, CAD scientists who are our direct users, that they can be grand strategists for a drug discovery campaign. When you have hundreds of drug discovery scientists working 24/7 on large data centers, just the output you get in terms of discovery is just going to be greater — especially if it's used in the hands of expert scientists who know what they're doing.

OpenBind 基准：泛化的硬证据

Brandon: We probably should have talked about this earlier — but you have some really exciting results from your Pearl model based upon a recent open data set challenge. Sorry we didn't talk about it before, but I'd like to give you a chance to talk about this. There's this OpenBind — was it EV-A71 2A protease — which was a very, very hard target that had a lot of things which made it hard for the community or for co-folding methods to internally work. You ran this internally, and had some fun results.

Sergey: We can also go back to our conversation on evals and what's wrong with evals in general. If you look at the open-source models and their performance on public benchmarks, it's kind of very similar across the board. What was surprising when OpenBind came up with this new benchmark is that you see a wide variety of performance of the models on it. In part it's because it's just a single target — okay, I get it. But it's also because this is a target that those models haven't seen during training. Nobody has optimized for that specific target before. Nobody climbed on it.

So it was very interesting for us to see how our models are going to perform out of the box on this particular target that we also have never seen before during training or when we developed these models. When it came up, we obviously wanted to look into it. We ran the Pearl system on that, and we were genuinely surprised and excited about results. Our numbers are way higher than other published, openly available models.

To me, it reflects how Pearl actually behaves in real drug discovery programs. We have seen this kind of number differences, shifts, in internal programs or partnership programs before, but we were unable to talk about them because a lot of this data is partnership data or internal data we cannot disclose. Here you have a perfect example where it's an external target, but we didn't know — we just ran on it. We see this staggering difference.

There are some specifics of this target — it has this flexible loop that needs to move when you insert your ligand into the right location. A lot of methods are just unable to handle it. Where Pearl was exceptionally good is figuring out how to move this loop. We are basically correct for every single pose — we're predicting really well this movement.

RJ: I'm curious about this. What is it about Pearl that allows it to model a dynamic target like that?

Sergey: Pearl is trained to predict together protein structure and ligand. Protein structure and ligand together — they're not necessarily in the same shape or form as the protein in isolation. The whole training process kind of nudges Pearl into that direction.

Brandon: So are there lots of examples in the training set like that, where there's this dynamic nature, where once during the binding event, the conformation changes? There is induced fit throughout the training set? This is probably inherited from the physics-based simulations that you're using?

Sergey: It certainly helps.

Evan: The other component Sergey alluded to was: we haven't just been benchmark-maxing on the same — we obviously publish on Runs and Poses. We published the state-of-the-art numbers on Runs and Poses last year when we published the Pearl technical report with Nvidia at GTC at the end of last year. We use Runs and Poses, we use PoseBusters — Pearl does the best on those benchmarks.

However, as Sergey is alluding to, the gap from Pearl to next-best model is even larger on the partner drug targets that we work on. That's in part a reflection of: the objective of our company is not maxing benchmarks. The objective is creating value for biotechs and pharma companies that are usually working on hard drug targets that are different from what's in the PDB. We've had to build the models in a way that they're able to extrapolate. The OpenBind challenge was just the latest example of that.

收尾：瓶颈与行动呼吁

RJ: Before we wrap up, we always ask two questions. One is: if you could remove a bottleneck from your industry by fiat — and this is not for inputs/outputs business — what would that be?

Sergey: I can say where my main bottleneck right now is — it's in GPUs. GPU prices are going up and up and up, and LLM companies are kind of sucking up all of the GPU capacity out there. I firmly believe that what we're doing is genuinely important for humanity, like discovering new medicines for yourself, for your loved ones. It's something that we all need at some point in our life. So I think it's very important to unblock that GPU bottleneck for drug discovery.

RJ: Is Anthropic actually throttling science because of GPU purchases? Are you getting spicy?

Brandon: I don't know if you can ask this, but are you looking at non-Nvidia? I guess you guys are Nvidia partners. Some companies are pivoting towards a multi-architecture model so you can take advantage of other suppliers because of the shortage.

Evan: Nvidia's been a great supporter of us. They've invested twice in Genesis, and we collaborate closely — not just in terms of the capital, but we optimize kernels together. Pearl was co-authored by Genesis and Nvidia. They publicized the OpenBind results with us. We've been very grateful for that. They're a very, very big company, and we're not at the scale of a customer like Meta or something. So we're grateful that they've given us time and attention nonetheless.

Part of that is a self-serving aspect: obviously no one can time the market, but the amount of hype and investment in the traditional LLM space for chatbots, coding sort of things — at some point the amount of alpha will not be what's desired compared to the amount of investment. Regardless of the vicissitudes of the economy, medicines will always be high in demand. I think whether it's a desire to help humanity or a self-serving interest in looking for the next big thing, chipmakers including Nvidia are going to want to get a lot more invested in life sciences, because they will always be high in demand and the amount of alpha left in pure LLM space is just getting a little questionable.

RJ: All the LLM companies are starting looking into life sciences right now anyway, right? GPT Roslin, there's a Cloud initiative, lots of startups.

Evan: Yep. We think those are all great tailwinds for us.

RJ: What about you? What's the bottleneck besides GPUs?

Evan: Oh, I had the same answer as Sergey for sure. If I could wave a magic wand, I'd love to have just an enormous H100 cluster. That would be amazing.

招聘与呼吁

RJ: The second question we ask is: do you have a call to action for our audience? AI engineers plus scientists — get involved, come apply for jobs?

Sergey: We are definitely hiring. We're definitely looking for top talent in the space. I personally transitioned from the LLM space to drug discovery — I've never done drug discovery before during Genesis. I find this field fascinating and extremely interesting. In LLM companies, you often find research scientists excited about working on architectures but kind of forced to work on boring stuff. And honestly, LLM architectures are relatively boring. I don't know — I probably alienate half of your audience.

RJ: I think they probably think the same thing.

Sergey: But it's a transformer layer in the end. The paper was published in 2017, and you go to any LLM lab today, you will see very, very similar pieces in their models.

RJ: Yes, there is a little bit of flavor of MoEs, but otherwise architectures are fundamentally very similar to what was discovered back in 2017.

Sergey: Right. Well, our architectures and our models are actually very different and very interesting to work with. So if somebody is excited about working on architectures, this space is actually a very interesting place to work.

Brandon: I like to comment — not only is your architecture very different from the LLM space, but you're actually very different from what a lot of the other bio ML space is doing as well. It's unique both in their subdomain and more broadly.

Evan: We saw a recent guest you had — one of the progenitors of ESMFold. They cite our model architecture paper that we published and open-sourced a few months ago. So the work you do here at Genesis as an AI researcher — there are lots of opportunities for publication, open source, and influencing the field, beyond the obvious ways to influence the field by working with top customers that are actually making medicines that change people's lives. It's both incredibly intellectually engaging and a lot of opportunities to influence the field and get your name out there. But if you want, you could also just stay being a cog in the machine of an LLM company. No shade.

RJ: No shade. All right, well thank you so much for coming and speaking with us. I've personally really enjoyed this — hopefully the audience as well. Evan, Sergey, thank you. We look forward to seeing how things develop, and hopefully you get some good job applications out of this.

Evan: We love the show, so we really appreciate taking the time to chat.

Sergey: Yeah, thank you for having us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 当"过时的工具"成为前沿的实验场
最让人意外的观察藏在 Evan 一句轻描淡写的话里：**当下最前沿的 diffusion 研究，不在图像生成领域，而在 3D 蛋白质结构预测里**。这是一个反直觉到几乎需要重读三遍的论断——主流叙事一直是"Sora、Midjourney 在推动 diffusion 的极限"，而药物发现被默认是"应用方"，是技术的下游消费者。
但仔细想想其中的系统性逻辑就豁然开朗：图像 diffusion 已经在快速被 autoregressive 模型反噬（参考新一代视频模型），因为图像的容错率高、约束弱、人类感知粗糙；而蛋白质-小分子结合需要 **sub-anstrom（亚埃，0.1 纳米级）的精度**，几乎没有任何冗余度可以让你"差不多就行"。**正是约束最严酷的领域，反而成了某种生成范式真正能被压榨到极限的地方**——就像高压锅决定了某些化学反应只能在那里发生。这是一种"领域选择技术、而非技术选择领域"的反向适配。
## 隐藏的对偶：LLM 的 inference-time scaling 与晶体结构生成
Sergey 揭示了一个极其优美的跨域同构

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Machiavelli was NOT Machiavellian – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的真相：「马基雅维利式」恰恰是马基雅维利本人最不会做的事

我们用"Machiavellian"一词形容那些精于算计、为达目的不择手段、随时可以背叛雇主的人。但 Palmer 揭示的历史马基雅维利却是一个近乎宗教式的"祖国原教旨主义者"——他本可以去任何欧洲宫廷拿三倍薪水当御用历史学家，却选择在托斯卡纳的乡下烂掉，只为讨好那个刚刚拷打过他的政权。

这里藏着一个语义学黑色幽默：一个词在五百年的传播中，会被其反面所占据。这与"epicurean（伊壁鸠鲁式）"被误解为纵欲、"cynical（犬儒式）"被误解为冷嘲、"Platonic（柏拉图式）"被误解为无肉欲，是同一类"概念漂移"——那些最被引用的思想家，往往是被引用得最离谱的思想家。这背后甚至可以推出一条规律：一个思想被简化为形容词的那一刻，它就开始走向自己的反面。

二、跨领域连接：《君主论》是文艺复兴版的"核机密"

Palmer 给了一个绝妙的类比——马基雅维利对待《君主论》就像一个掌握外交机密的核物理学家对待自己的发明。这个对照值得继续拉远：

  • 曼哈顿计划的科学家为了"自由世界"的存续，把核秘密交给单一政权。
  • 马基雅维利为了佛罗伦萨的存续，把"权力秘密"交给唯一一个他认定的祖国。

这意味着《君主论》在历史上是第一份知识产权式的"国家战略资产"——而不是一本书。它的传播逻辑不是"出版"，而是"泄密"。今天我们读到的所有版本，本质上都是未经作者授权的盗版。如果按马基雅维利原本的意愿，这本书永远不该被你读到。

这反过来颠覆了一个文学常识：有些经典之所以伟大，恰恰是因为它违背了作者的意图被广泛阅读。卡夫卡烧稿、艾米莉·狄金森锁抽屉、马基雅维利想私藏——人类文明的相当一部分高峰，是由"违抗作者遗愿的人"贡献的。

三、出人意料的观察：流放制度其实是一种"组织忠诚度测试协议"

最让我意外的不是马基雅维利的故事，而是 Palmer 描述的佛罗伦萨流放制度本身——它根本不是惩罚，而是一套精密的 HR 系统。

把它翻译成现代语言： - 不信任你 → 把你外派到伦敦、布鲁日（带任务的远程办公） - 你完成 KPI → 召回总部、官复原职 - 你跳槽到竞争对手 → 永久封禁（违反竞业协议）

而马基雅维利收到的待遇，是这套系统中最阴毒的一种：被发配到没有任何政治资源的乡下小村——这等于现代公司里把高管调去"研究室主任"职位，没有下属、没有项目、没有预算。这是一种剥夺其全部社会资本的休眠测试：看你会不会忍。

这里有个让人脊背发凉的洞见：忠诚的最高考验，不是"你愿意为我做什么"，而是"你愿意为我什么都不做"。 行动是廉价的忠诚证明，无所事事却仍然不背叛，才是真正的忠诚。马基雅维利通过了这场测试，付出的代价是他人生最后十几年的政治生命——而我们今天却用他的名字来形容那种最快跳槽、最善背叛的人。

历史最大的讽刺，往往不是事实被遗忘，而是事实被精确地颠倒过来。

📝 转录文本

佛罗伦萨流放的真实含义

Exile is a very common thing in Florence and isn't the permanence that one expects in Florence. Exile means the people who are in charge of the regime distrust you right now. They want you out of the city, but they are testing your loyalty. They are testing whether you will stay true to them.

And you're told not get out of the city like a Roman exile, but go to a specific place — go to London, go to Bruge, go here, stay there, and we will send you instructions. And then you're expected to act as a kind of unofficial official emissary for the government of Florence while in your exile. And you'll be asked to do diplomatic missions after a while. And they'll say, you know, go talk to this person on our behalf, or go deliver this trusted letter.

And if you're good and you behave, then after some years of service to the republic, you'll be recalled. So it's a sort of a provisional exile. And they pick a specific place to send you. And if you go and are good and do what they say, then after a while they consider bringing you home. If you don't — if you leave and you don't stay where they said, if you run off to work for someone else — then okay, you're not allowed back in Florence anymore. You're an exile at this point.

马基雅维利得到的特殊"待遇"

Machiavelli's exile is unusual because they really don't trust him. And so they don't send him to Bruge or London or Barcelona or the Germans or any number of other places where he actually has political contacts — or doesn't. They send him to a middle-of-nowhere hamlet in the countryside outside of Florence in Tuscany, where there is nobody important and there is nothing to do.

And this isn't a "go wait for instructions." This is a "go rot," and we're testing whether you will faithfully stay and do basically nothing, and be forbidden to talk to important people, be in isolation.

所有人都以为他会跳槽

When that exile is given, everybody expects that Machiavelli's response will be: "Okay, they're not giving me even a second chance. I'm gonna run off and work for somebody else."

Because there are a jillion people in Europe who would love to employ a skillful classicist historian with military and diplomatic capacities, who has political contacts in Rome and in France and has visited the court of the emperor. He could have worked for any number of cardinals. He could have gotten a very prestigious diplomatic job in any of a number of a dozen courts.

A Florentine historian especially is something that you absolutely want to hire to write a flattery history of your own family. And for even a century before this, kings as far away as England had been trying to hire Florentine historians to come write about them. So he could easily do this — and this is what is expected.

但他没有

And he doesn't. Because Machiavelli says: "No, I'm going to stay and I'm going to rot and I'm going to write The Prince, which is my job application begging the new regime to bring me back and let me work for them and demonstrating my loyalty. And I'm going to send it to them and only them and my immediate friends. I'm not going to share it with anybody else."

Because Machiavelli is a patriot, and he will not serve any cause that is not his country. No matter whether the pay at a royal court somewhere will be three times what he would ever get at home — that doesn't matter to him. No matter whether this is the regime that just arrested, tortured, and exiled him despite him not having plotted against them — he wants to work for that, because Machiavelli fundamentally is possibly one of the most patriotic patriots in Earth's history.

And he will faithfully sit in the countryside and rot while begging to work for the people who ordered his torture, so long as they will recall him so that he can serve his country.

《君主论》：一份永远不该被你读到的手稿

And this connects to the question we always ask about the intended audience of The Prince. Because his other work — his Discourses, his histories, his comedic play — those were for public circulation. Those increased his fame. Those made important arguments. His history of Florence joined other important histories of Florence circulating, influencing the way people thought about politics.

Not The Prince. The Prince is secret, proprietary — the secret sauce of how to maintain power, and he will not let any other power have that. It's like a nuclear scientist with diplomatic secrets who is faithful to his country and will not sell out and let those secrets fall into other hands.

Machiavelli knows that he has the beginnings of a new world of political science, and he will only share that with the government of his country, because he wants it to protect his country, and he will not serve any other cause.

历史最大的讽刺

This is why it's so weirdly ironic to me that the reputation — the word "Machiavellian" — means self-serving. Well, Machiavelli himself is one of the most selfless men I've ever read about in the history of the earth: who will give up and sacrifice career, diplomacy, fame, friends, the opportunity to even be in a city and have a nice day, to rot in the countryside to be faithful to his country.

And he would rather serve nothing and no one than give an hour of his time to advancing anything that is not Florence.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的真相：「马基雅维利式」恰恰是马基雅维利本人最不会做的事
我们用"Machiavellian"一词形容那些精于算计、为达目的不择手段、随时可以背叛雇主的人。但 Palmer 揭示的历史马基雅维利却是一个近乎宗教式的"祖国原教旨主义者"——他本可以去任何欧洲宫廷拿三倍薪水当御用历史学家，却选择在托斯卡纳的乡下烂掉，只为讨好那个**刚刚拷打过他**的政权。
这里藏着一个语义学黑色幽默：**一个词在五百年的传播中，会被其反面所占据**。这与"epicurean（伊壁鸠鲁式）"被误解为纵欲、"cynical（犬儒式）"被误解为冷嘲、"Platonic（柏拉图式）"被误解为无肉欲，是同一类"概念漂移"——**那些最被引用的思想家，往往是被引用得最离谱的思想家**。这背后甚至可以推出一条规律：一个思想被简化为形容词的那一刻，它就开始走向自己的反面。
## 二、跨领域连接：《君主论》是文艺复兴版的"核机密"
Palmer 给了一个绝妙的类比——马基雅维利对待《君主论》就像一个掌握外交机密的核物理学家对待自己的发明。这个对照值得继续拉远：
- **曼哈顿计划的科学家**为了

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] The Two Harvard Dropouts Who raised $800M to take on NVIDIA
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察：他们卖的不是芯片，是「热力学套利」

大多数人以为 Etched 的优势是"为推理专门设计架构"——这是表层叙事。真正的核心赌注是一个物理学套利：电压的平方与功耗成正比（Dennard 定律）。GPU 必须以高电压运行，是因为它要兼容地球上所有可能的负载——从冰窖里的传感器到桑拿房里的手机。而 AI 数据中心永远不会低于 80°C。

这意味着 GPU 的高电压是在为一种不存在的"通用性税"买单。Etched 把电压砍掉一半以上，功耗骤降到四分之一以下——他们不是在"做得更好的芯片"，他们是在回收行业为了"普适性幻觉"而支付了几十年的能量税。

跨领域连接：这与比特币矿机的逻辑完全同构。Bitcoin ASIC 之所以能用 GPU 1/4 的电压运行，是因为它只做一件事。Gavin 在访谈里轻描淡写地提到这点，但这其实是整个公司的"原罪式洞察"——专用性不是商业策略，是热力学策略。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：他们在做"代币界的福特流水线"

Rob 反复提到"production is the product"——但这句话被严重低估了。注意他的隐喻：今天的代币像文艺复兴时期手工车出来的螺丝，每一颗略有不同；他想要的是 iPhone 式的规模经济，让"代币"这个商品也获得边际成本趋零的工业品属性。

这里有个出人意料的对照：福特之前，汽车是手工艺品；福特之后，汽车是工业品。从手工艺到工业品的临界点不是"更好的汽车设计"，而是装配线、垂直整合、可复制的生产单元。Etched 在 Bangalore 设双班倒、在台湾建 mini 工厂、把 Brian Lerer（NVIDIA 整机架团队的人）拉过来——这些动作不是"芯片公司在做的事"，这是福特在 1913 年做的事。

更深的连接：他们在赌的是 AI 推理会经历从工匠到工业品的相变。如果赌对了，最后赢家不会是"芯片设计最好的"，而会是"最早建好流水线的"。这就是为什么他们说"the best vendor is no vendor"——这是亨利·福特的原话改写版（"the best part is no part"）。

三、最反常识的人才哲学：他们刻意制造「门槛悖论」

Gavin 一句话让我反复回味："你必须脑子有点病才会加入我们公司"。这听起来像玩笑，其实是精密的自我选择机制。当一个机会从外部看起来"明显不合理"（年轻 CEO + 半导体 + 对抗 NVIDIA + 搬去圣何塞），它会自动过滤掉机会主义者，只留下两类人：

  1. 传奇专家——他们已经在职业的顶端，金钱不是动机，"再做一件真正重要的事"才是
  2. 天真的天才——他们不知道这不可能，所以去做了

这两类人的化学反应才是关键。Gavin 说："传奇知道好的样子（because they've seen it），天真的人不知道哪里埋着尸体（所以敢踩雷区）"。

跨领域连接：这与贝尔实验室黄金时代、洛斯阿拉莫斯曼哈顿计划、甚至早期 SpaceX 的人才结构完全同构——一群"已经成功过的老法师" + 一群"不知道天高地厚的年轻人"。中间层（中年中产、经验丰富但风险厌恶）反而是组织的毒药。

意外的衍生洞察：他们担心"如果产品太成功，反而会吸引到错误的人"——这是反向品牌策略。大多数公司想让自己看起来诱人，Etched 想让自己看起来"sick in the head"。这是一种反共识的人才虹吸。

四、彩蛋：一个让我警醒的隐藏类比

Rob 用 ChatGPT 上传自己背上的肿瘤照片，6 个月没诊断出的病，AI 一秒看出"立即去医院"。这个故事被讲成"为什么要做推理芯片"——但更深的隐喻是：整个医疗系统、教育系统、科学发现系统，过去都是"延迟系统"。延迟来自专家稀缺、注意力稀缺、并发稀缺。

如果推理便宜 100 倍，世界不会变得"快 100 倍"，而是会出现一类全新的物种：连续性服务。今天你只能"约一次医生"，未来你会有一个持续运行的、永不下线的、全人类共享的诊断进程。这与电力史的相变同构——爱迪生不是让蜡烛更亮，他让"光"从离散事件变成了连续可得的环境。

代币也会经历这个相变。我们还在"约一次专家"的时代，但他们在建的是"环境化智能"的基础设施。

📝 转录文本

开场：与众不同的赌注

"We know inference is going to be the biggest market in the world. Whoever produces the most tokens is going to be the most valuable company in the world."

—— "We we had people quit. That people literally were like, 'This problem is unsolvable.' And uh best of luck guys."

—— "You kind of have to be sick in the head to join our company. You're going to convince your family to move to San Jose for the semiconductor company run by two what 24 year olds now going against the biggest companies in the world with a design that they're saying is not going to be like 10% better, but it's going to be 10 10x better."

—— "We need $100 million to do this. If we do this, we think this could be one of the most important companies of all time."

—— "You're sitting in that moment, you think, holy crap, we can't afford this. And I began looking at like, huh, how hard is it to go back to Harvard?"

一、面对整个行业的不信任

主持人： All right, gentlemen. It's been three years or so, Gavin, since you and I last did this, which is nuts. And at the time, I was just wildly intrigued by your story and what you were going to build. I didn't know a lot about chips at the time. I was considering investing in the company. And so, I was calling everyone I could conceive of that could give me an opinion or something.

And at the time, basically, the consensus was these kinds of companies are not built by young people. That the semis world, the best companies are founded by 40, 50-year-old people that have had a whole career's worth of experience, have learned all the problems, have shipped multiple chips. Two 21-year-olds are like not going to do this. Like it's just not going to work.

And it was indicative of a theme which was: nobody believes in us. That's obviously changed a lot now. You know, just walk the halls and talk to the people that have chosen to come work here. But in the early days, it felt like this was something that you had to face down. What was that like — facing that down where a set of incumbents and an industry worth of people and investors and everyone else sort of didn't believe in you? And what did that anneal in you to build the company the way that you are? Like what was the impact of that?

天真作为竞争优势

Rob： I think there's a certain level of naivity required to think that you could build a chip better than every other AI chip ever built and build a company to do it way faster than ever has been done. And we have the naivety. There's many times where we would say like "why isn't this possible?" and really push on it, and it turns out that everybody's answers are extremely siloed to a set of constraints that aren't true anymore.

The reality is the entire semiconductors and data center industry is built on buffer. And what I mean by that is every part of the stack — from the EDA tools to the power modules to the circuit boards to the chip design and standard cells — everything is built to be general purpose for everything, not just in the data center but IoT, on the edge, and so forth. And when you have a specific use case you're really trying to design for, you can change the constraints a lot.

I'll give you just a very simple example that we're not the only one who does: one of the things you care a lot about is the clock speed of your chip. It's proportional to the throughput of your system. When you are doing signoff for different timing, there's this concept called "corners," which is, what temperatures are you going to be able to run at this clock speed. The default configurations for a lot of these EDA tools assume that you're going to be running your chips in freezing temperatures. Now, I don't know about you, but I've never seen an AI data center with ice in it. So we can feel pretty confident that our chips don't need to run at full speed at 0°C. In fact, they're never really going to be running below 80°C anyway. And just by knowing that that's a constraint that doesn't matter, we can make a ton of changes throughout the entire system.

That's a very simple one, but there's many more that you get 20% here, 50% there, 2x here, and these compound to a system that can be radically better for inference.

早期支持者 Mark Ross 的故事

Gavin： I think you found two kinds of people. There are some folks who went purely on heuristics: "Okay, young founders. They claim they can go beat the biggest company in the world out performance. It cannot happen. And there is nothing you could go say to me that would make me change my mind."

But there's also people out there who are skeptical but willing to say, "I'll spend the time. I'll do the work. And is it actually possible?" For example, one of our earliest supporters was this guy Mark Ross. Mark was a very prestigious semiconductor expert, used to be CTO at Cypress Semi that sold for $9 billion.

When we met him, we were just a couple of guys in a dorm room. And we came to him and said, "Hey, we want to go build hardware for inference. We think we can be much faster than Nvidia." And Mark's like, "No, you can't. It will not work. But if you want to go convince me, write a white paper. Build a functional simulation and show me."

After a lot of very long nights, we went back to Mark and said, "Hey, here's a simulation. What do you think?" And he was like, "Huh, this works. But to do a company like this, you'll need a large amount of capital. You need at least $3 million even to get started." We ended up raising five, and a lot more after that. He was again surprised but got more involved, then became an adviser, a halftime adviser, and eventually a full-time CTO as he saw more and more of the development progress.

In general this has filtered really heavily — folks who want to be right regardless, versus folks who will be very truth-seeking and say, "Sure, I'm skeptical, but I will work through the numbers myself. And if I can figure out why this is possible, well, let's go build it."

二、产品的核心：不只是芯片，是完整推理方案

主持人： The specifics that you've made bets on, the way that you've built this system, are immensely interesting to me. And because so many people are trying to do this now — build new chips that will do a better job of serving inference at massive scale — the world is interested in the research approaches, the different architecture approaches that people are taking. I'd love you to just start by describing what this thing is, what it does, but maybe more interestingly the process you went through to decide what bets to take.

Prefill 和 Decode：装弹与开火

Rob： I think you start with the product. We're not just building a chip. We're building a full inference solution. That means a rack. That means the chip. That means the power delivery into the chip. That means the board in which it sits. That means the interconnect by which the chips talk to each other. That means the production for this mass volume of racks. Really, the production is the product.

We think about how we get our advantage. There are two key parts of running inference: there's prefill and there's decode. Now we have two key tags for both of these things. Prefill is reading in a huge volume of text. Decode is then using that data to generate output tokens. When you run prefill, your key job is not to predict tokens — you already know the text. Your job is to get the model's memory — what we call the KV cache — into the right state. Then you can run decode with that same KV cache.

So we often do what we call PD disaggregation — prefill/decode disaggregation. You'll transfer those model memories, those KV caches, over to the decode cluster and use that cluster to generate the next tokens.

主持人： So it's sort of like loading the gun and then firing it. If I think about it in super simple terms.

Rob： Yeah, you got it. It's getting the model to remember the right things and then using those things to do tasks.

探索没人走过的设计空间

Generally people think about this market a bit lazily: "Are you prefill chip? Are you decode chip? If you're decode chip, are you an HBM chip? Are you an SRAM chip? Are you a 3D RAM chip? Are you using optics, using copper?"

When we started this, we just wanted to understand why extremely smart people were working on these different directions. We seriously looked at architectures like having a bunch of DDR memory and a shared memory pool, looking at advanced packaging to break out of the shoreline. We looked at putting memory dies on top of compute dies. In doing so, we realized there's no free lunch — everything has a trade-off. With 3D RAM, you have a thermal issue, a supply chain issue, you have to figure out hybrid bonding, you have to figure out the flops.

So we went through everything on both the prefill and the decode side. And we realized there's a few design spaces that nobody had seriously tried to explore because they were never done in AI chips.

Prefill 端的核心赌注：低电压推理

We asked ourselves: what are the actual metrics that are going to matter the most?

On the prefill side, the thing that matters is flops and flops density. People talk about flops often as a headline number, but in reality, you should care about the flops you're getting when you're running real workloads. There's this concept called MFU — Model Flops Utilization — which is, for every peak flop advertised, how many cents on the dollar are you actually getting. On GPUs you often get somewhere between 20 and 50% depending on the workload. And actually you can provably not run at 100% because you have a thermal issue — as you increase the flop utilization you have more transistors going on and off, you draw more power, and the chip will self-regulate and lower its clock speed to make sure it doesn't overheat.

As we looked at inference, we said: if we want way more flops, because we want to run at way higher throughputs, we fundamentally need to solve the thermal problem before we even think about adding flops to the chip. If I just add more flops to a GPU today or another AI chip, I'm not actually going to get more performance because it's just going to thermal throttle.

Fundamentally, the essence of that is this concept of Dennard scaling: voltage is quadratically proportional to power. So if I 2x my voltage, my power goes up by 4x. If I cut my voltage in half, my power goes down by a quarter.

So we asked ourselves: how could we run voltages lower than GPUs? We flew out to Silicon Valley after dropping out and basically asked dozens of people in semiconductors at all these different chip companies how they did it. And the answer we got was, "You can't. You can't run at voltages lower than GPUs."

This was very dissatisfying because there are many different industries of chips that run at voltages lower than GPUs. I mean, Bitcoin miners run at under a quarter of the voltage of GPUs. So this is obviously physically possible. The question is, are there issues with GPU architectures that make them unable to run at these voltages?

When we looked at the problem for a long time, we were able to create a new mechanism of running at much lower voltages — a new type of power delivery that we call low voltage inference. We think all AI chips in the future are going to be low voltage chips. They're going to have to cram way more flops in the same silicon area and, without thermal throttling, run at way lower voltages.

Decode 端的核心赌注：Cluster Scale Memory

For decode, it is all a memory game. The more memory bound, the faster you can load the model, load the KV cache, and serve more tokens per second per user. We think people ask the wrong question here. People often ask, "How much memory bandwidth is on your chip?" You should be asking, "How much memory bandwidth is on your full scale-up cluster?"

What we are able to do is add way, way more bandwidth and a much lower latency from chip to chip on our interconnects. It allows us to serve models at much higher speeds because you can use the SRAM and the HBM from the full scale-up cluster as a single pool. That's our second key technical bet, what we call cluster scale memory.

On GPUs today, the cluster memory bandwidth is often very badly utilized because the time to hop from one GPU to another is extremely long. For example, on Blackwell chips, it can be about 4,000 nanoseconds to go point to point. That means if you go to an 8x TP setup, you will get way less than an 8x improvement in your tokens per second per user.

What we did is built our own totally custom interconnect stack. We took everything above the second layer of Ethernet, built it fully custom, and we can do far better latencies and bandwidths this way. We can cut this by more than a factor of 5x. That allows us to use the memory of other chips much more effectively. As you scale the world size, your time per token goes down proportionally.

It's not that surprising — given all these architectures were built before ChatGPT — that if we're trying to build a chip for the modern workloads, it's going to look very different. The way we organize our flops, the way we do our voltage domains, the way we do our power planes are going to look super different. The way we do the packaging is going to look super different. The way we do the board design is going to look different. And on the decode side, the way we connect everything is going to look very different.

We're now bringing forward our first generation of this low voltage inference technology, which is running at under half the voltage of any other AI chip.

【广告：Ramp / WorkOS / Felix by Rogo】

RAMP is the only platform built to make your finance team leaner, faster, and better, saving businesses 5% annually on average...
